WorkArena++ (Boisvert et al., 2024)\n7. ITBench (Jha et al., 2025)\n8. WorkBench (Styles et al., 2024)\n9. TheAgentCompany (Xu et al., 2024)\n10. CRMArena (Huang et al., 2025)\n11. CRMArena-Pro (Huang et al., 2025)\n\nFor EACH paper, extract these 6 fields:\n- benchmark_name: Short name only — do not include paper subtitle. Report only the benchmark identifier (e.g., report \"ACEBench\" not \"ACEBench: Who Wins the Match Point in Tool Usage?\", report \"WorkBench\" not \"WorkBench: a Benchmark Dataset for...\").\n- num_domains: Number of distinct task domains, content categories, stakeholder personas, or job departments the benchmark covers. Stakeholder roles/personas (e.g., service agent, analyst, manager) and job departments (e.g., SDE, HR, Finance) count as valid domain categories. If the paper organises tasks only by pure cognitive/skill dimensions (e.g., planning, retrieval, reasoning, memorization) with no content domain separation, report \"not provided\".\n- num_tasks: Total number of tasks in the benchmark. For benchmarks with multiple domains, report the combined total across all domains.\n- has_human_task_curation (boolean): Whether tasks or evaluation data were manually created, annotated, or curated by humans.\n- has_refusal_ability (boolean): Whether the benchmark includes tasks where the agent must refuse, reject, or flag unsolvable/infeasible/unanswerable requests. This includes: tasks requiring detection of missing/erroneous parameters, infeasible task identification, non-answerable query detection, policy-based refusal, and transfer-to-human scenarios.\n- has_human_plans (boolean): Whether the paper provides human-authored step-by-step action plans, solution sequences, or oracle action traces as ground truth. This includes: Playwright oracle functions that solve tasks step-by-step, annotated API call chains, ground-truth database write action sequences, and solution functions specifying ordered tool calls. Set to false if evaluation uses only checkpoint-based scoring of environment states (pass/fail milestones) or exact-match of final answers without any reference to intermediate action sequences.\n\nFor EACH field you extract (except benchmark_name), you must also provide a \"statement_from_the_paper\" field containing an array of 1-3 exact verbatim quotes from the paper that justify your extracted value. Field name format: <field_name>_statement_from_the_paper\n\nYou must search for and actually read each paper. Do not guess values. Write the final result to output/output.json.\n\n\n---\n\n## Output Instructions\n\nWrite your final answer to `/logs/agent/output.json` in this exact JSON format:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"benchmarks\": [\n    {\n      \"benchmark_name\": \"<str>\",\n      \"num_domains\": \"<int>\",\n      \"num_domains_statement_from_the_paper\": [\n        \"<str>\"\n      ],\n      \"num_tasks\": \"<str>\",\n      \"num_tasks_statement_from_the_paper\": [],\n      \"has_human_task_curation\": \"<bool>\",\n      \"has_human_task_curation_statement_from_the_paper\": [\n        \"<str>\"\n      ],\n      \"has_refusal_ability\": \"<bool>\",\n      \"has_refusal_ability_statement_from_the_paper\": [],\n      \"has_human_plans\": \"<bool>\",\n      \"has_human_plans_statement_from_the_paper\": [\n        \"<str>\"\n      ]\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nDo not write anything else to that file."}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:2","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: API-Bank: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs\nauthor: Li; Minghao; Zhao; Yingxiu; Yu; Bowen; Song; Feifan; Hangyu; Haiyang; Zhoujun; Huang; Fei; Yongbin\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08244v2\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Recent research has demonstrated that Large Language Models (LLMs) can enhance their capabilities by utilizing external tools. However, three pivotal questions remain unanswered: (1) How effective are current LLMs in utilizing tools? (2) How can we enhance LLMs' ability to utilize tools? (3) What obstacles need to be overcome to leverage tools? To address these questions, we introduce API-Bank, a groundbreaking benchmark, specifically designed for tool-augmented LLMs. For the first question, we develop a runnable evaluation system consisting of 73 API tools. We annotate 314 tool-use dialogues with 753 API calls to assess the existing LLMs' capabilities in planning, retrieving, and calling APIs. For the second question, we construct a comprehensive training set containing 1,888 tool-use dialogues from 2,138 APIs spanning 1,000 distinct domains. Using this dataset, we train Lynx, a tool-augmented LLM initialized from Alpaca. Experimental results demonstrate that GPT-3.5 exhibits improved tool utilization compared to GPT-3, while GPT-4 excels in planning. However, there is still significant potential for further improvement. Moreover, Lynx surpasses Alpaca's tool utilization performance by more than 26 pts and approaches the effectiveness of GPT-3.5. Through error analysis, we highlight the key challenges for future research in this field to answer the third question.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2023-04-14\n---\nComputer Science > Computation and Language\n[Submitted on 14 Apr 2023 (v1), last revised 25 Oct 2023 (this version, v2)]\nTitle:API-Bank: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs\nView PDFAbstract:Recent research has demonstrated that Large Language Models (LLMs) can enhance their capabilities by utilizing external tools. However, three pivotal questions remain unanswered: (1) How effective are current LLMs in utilizing tools? (2) How can we enhance LLMs' ability to utilize tools? (3) What obstacles need to be overcome to leverage tools? To address these questions, we introduce API-Bank, a groundbreaking benchmark, specifically designed for tool-augmented LLMs. For the first question, we develop a runnable evaluation system consisting of 73 API tools. We annotate 314 tool-use dialogues with 753 API calls to assess the existing LLMs' capabilities in planning, retrieving, and calling APIs. For the second question, we construct a comprehensive training set containing 1,888 tool-use dialogues from 2,138 APIs spanning 1,000 distinct domains. Using this dataset, we train Lynx, a tool-augmented LLM initialized from Alpaca. Experimental results demonstrate that GPT-3.5 exhibits improved tool utilization compared to GPT-3, while GPT-4 excels in planning. However, there is still significant potential for further improvement. Moreover, Lynx surpasses Alpaca's tool utilization performance by more than 26 pts and approaches the effectiveness of GPT-3.5. Through error analysis, we highlight the key challenges for future research in this field to answer the third question.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Minghao Li [view email][v1] Fri, 14 Apr 2023 14:05:32 UTC (330 KB)\n[v2] Wed, 25 Oct 2023 06:54:12 UTC (1,265 KB)\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.FetchURL:4","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"---\ntitle: $τ$-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains\nauthor: Yao; Shunyu; Shinn; Noah; Razavi; Pedram; Narasimhan; Karthik\nurl: https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.12045v1\nhostname: arxiv.org\ndescription: Existing benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human users or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying them in real world applications. We propose $τ$-bench, a benchmark emulating dynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a language agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines. We employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database state at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose a new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple trials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like gpt-4o) succeed on <50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 <25% in retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability of agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-06-17\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Artificial Intelligence\n[Submitted on 17 Jun 2024]\nTitle:$τ$-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains\nView PDFAbstract:Existing benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human users or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying them in real world applications. We propose $\\tau$-bench, a benchmark emulating dynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a language agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines. We employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database state at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose a new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple trials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like gpt-4o) succeed on <50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 <25% in retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability of agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Karthik Narasimhan [view email][v1] Mon, 17 Jun 2024 19:33:08 UTC (647 KB)\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? In this paper, we establish a series of Helly-type theorems for convexity spaces derived from separated $d$-intervals. Our results encompass the Radon number, Helly number, colorful Helly number, fractional Helly number, colorful fractional Helly theorem, $(p,q)$ theorem, and two kinds of colorful $(p,q)$ theorems for these convexity spaces. The primary tools employed in our proofs involve simplicial complexes and collapsibility.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-01-06\nlicense: CC BY-SA 4.0\n---\nMathematics > Combinatorics\n[Submitted on 6 Jan 2025 (v1), last revised 22 May 2025 (this version, v2)]\nTitle:Helly-type theorems for separated $d$-intervals\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:A separated $d$-interval is defined as a disjoint union of $d$ convex sets from the real line $\\mathbb R$. In this paper, we establish a series of Helly-type theorems for convexity spaces derived from separated $d$-intervals. Our results encompass the Radon number, Helly number, colorful Helly number, fractional Helly number, colorful fractional Helly theorem, $(p,q)$ theorem, and two kinds of colorful $(p,q)$ theorems for these convexity spaces. The primary tools employed in our proofs involve simplicial complexes and collapsibility.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Wei Rao [view email][v1] Mon, 6 Jan 2025 18:38:52 UTC (14 KB)\n[v2] Thu, 22 May 2025 07:09:48 UTC (18 KB)\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nPapers with Code (What is Papers with Code?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? When performing numerical simulations of resistive magnetohydrodynamics, one is faced with the issue that Amperes law becomes stiff in the high conductivity limit which poses challenges to the numerical evolution. We show that using a description of resistive magnetohydrodynamics based on higher form symmetry, one can perform simulations with a generalized dual Faraday tensor without having to use Amperes Law, thereby avoiding the stiffness problem. We also explain the relation of this dual model to a traditional description of resistive magnetohydrodynamics and how causality is guaranteed by introducing second order corrections.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-01-08\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nAstrophysics > High Energy Astrophysical Phenomena\n[Submitted on 8 Jan 2025 (v1), last revised 9 Sep 2025 (this version, v2)]\nTitle:Resistive relativistic magnetohydrodynamics without Amperes Law\nView PDFAbstract:Resistive magnetohydrodynamics is thought to play a key role in transient astrophysical phenomena such as black hole flares and neutron star magnetospheres. When performing numerical simulations of resistive magnetohydrodynamics, one is faced with the issue that Amperes law becomes stiff in the high conductivity limit which poses challenges to the numerical evolution. We show that using a description of resistive magnetohydrodynamics based on higher form symmetry, one can perform simulations with a generalized dual Faraday tensor without having to use Amperes Law, thereby avoiding the stiffness problem. While adiabatic tapers are well suited for small size differences, they become impractically long for expansion factors around x100 which are often required when coupling integrated waveguides and free-space beams. Evanescent couplers and bragg deflectors can be used in this scenario, but their operation is inherently limited in bandwidth. Here we propose a solution based on a parabolic dielectric interface that couples light from a 0.5 um-wide waveguide to a 285 um-wide waveguide, i.e. an expansion factor of x570. We experimentally demonstrate an unprecedented bandwidth of more than 380 nm with insertion losses below 0.35 dB. We furthermore provide analytical expressions for the design of such parabolic spot-size-converters for arbitrary expansion factors.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2025-01-06\n---\nPhysics > Optics\n[Submitted on 6 Jan 2025]\nTitle:Parabolic dielectric reflector for extreme on-chip spot-size conversion with broad bandwidth\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:Spot-size converters are key for efficient coupling of light between waveguides of different sizes. While adiabatic tapers are well suited for small size differences, they become impractically long for expansion factors around x100 which are often required when coupling integrated waveguides and free-space beams. Evanescent couplers and bragg deflectors can be used in this scenario, but their operation is inherently limited in bandwidth. Here we propose a solution based on a parabolic dielectric interface that couples light from a 0.5 um-wide waveguide to a 285 um-wide waveguide, i.e. an expansion factor of x570. We experimentally demonstrate an unprecedented bandwidth of more than 380 nm with insertion losses below 0.35 dB. Our study aims to enhance the security framework of these systems by leveraging wake words for initial activation and speaker recognition to validate user permissions. By incorporating these AI-driven methodologies, we propose a robust solution that restricts system usage to authorized individuals, thereby mitigating unauthorized access risks. This research delves into the algorithms and technologies underpinning wake word detection and speaker recognition, evaluates their effectiveness in real-world applications, and discusses the potential for their implementation in various embedded systems, emphasizing security and user convenience. The findings underscore the feasibility and advantages of employing these AI techniques to create secure, user-friendly voice-activated systems.\nsitename: arXiv.org\ndate: 2024-07-25\nlicense: CC BY 4.0\n---\nComputer Science > Sound\n[Submitted on 25 Jul 2024]\nTitle:Implementation and Applications of WakeWords Integrated with Speaker Recognition: A Case Study\nView PDF HTML (experimental)Abstract:This paper explores the application of artificial intelligence techniques in audio and voice processing, focusing on the integration of wake words and speaker recognition for secure access in embedded systems. With the growing prevalence of voice-activated devices such as Amazon Alexa, ensuring secure and user-specific interactions has become paramount. Our study aims to enhance the security framework of these systems by leveraging wake words for initial activation and speaker recognition to validate user permissions. By incorporating these AI-driven methodologies, we propose a robust solution that restricts system usage to authorized individuals, thereby mitigating unauthorized access risks. This research delves into the algorithms and technologies underpinning wake word detection and speaker recognition, evaluates their effectiveness in real-world applications, and discusses the potential for their implementation in various embedded systems, emphasizing security and user convenience. The findings underscore the feasibility and advantages of employing these AI techniques to create secure, user-friendly voice-activated systems.\nSubmission history\nFrom: Elisa Ayumi Masasi De Oliveira [view email][v1] Thu, 25 Jul 2024 02:02:26 UTC (77 KB)\nCurrent browse context:\ncs.SD\nReferences & Citations\nexport BibTeX citation\nLoading...\nBibliographic and Citation Tools\nBibliographic Explorer (What is the Explorer?)\nConnected Papers (What is Connected Papers?)\nLitmaps (What is Litmaps?)\nscite Smart Citations (What are Smart Citations?)\nCode, Data and Media Associated with this Article\nalphaXiv (What is alphaXiv?)\nCatalyzeX Code Finder for Papers (What is CatalyzeX?)\nDagsHub (What is DagsHub?)\nGotit.pub (What is GotitPub?)\nHugging Face (What is Huggingface?)\nScienceCast (What is ScienceCast?)\nDemos\nRecommenders and Search Tools\nInfluence Flower (What are Influence Flowers?)\nCORE Recommender (What is CORE?)\narXivLabs: experimental projects with community collaborators\narXivLabs is a framework that allows collaborators to develop and share new arXiv features directly on our website.\nBoth individuals and organizations that work with arXivLabs have embraced and accepted our values of openness, community, excellence, and user data privacy. arXiv is committed to these values and only works with partners that adhere to them.\nHave an idea for a project that will add value for arXiv's community? {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:30","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"API-Bank: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs\n†\n\nMinghao Li1∗ , Yingxiu Zhao2∗, Bowen Yu1 , Feifan Song3\nHangyu Li1 , Haiyang Yu1 , Zhoujun Li4 , Fei Huang1 , Yongbin Li1†\n1\nAlibaba Group, 2 Hong Kong University of Science and Technology,\n3\nPeking University, 4 Shenzhen Intelligent Strong Technology Co., Ltd,\n{lmh397008, yubowen.ybw, hangyu.lhy, yifei.yhy, f.huang, shuide.lyb}@alibaba-inc.com\nsongff@stu.pku.edu.cn, yzhaocx@connect.ust.hk\nlizhoujun@aistrong.com\nAbstract\n\narXiv:2304.08244v2 [cs.CL] 25 Oct 2023\n\nRecent research has demonstrated that Large\nLanguage Models (LLMs) can enhance their capabilities by utilizing external tools. However,\nthree pivotal questions remain unanswered: (1)\nHow effective are current LLMs in utilizing\ntools? (2) How can we enhance LLMs’ ability\nto utilize tools? (3) What obstacles need to be\novercome to leverage tools? To address these\nquestions, we introduce API-Bank, a groundbreaking benchmark, specifically designed for\ntool-augmented LLMs. For the first question,\nwe develop a runnable evaluation system consisting of 73 API tools. We annotate 314\ntool-use dialogues with 753 API calls to assess the existing LLMs’ capabilities in planning, retrieving, and calling APIs. For the second question, we construct a comprehensive\ntraining set containing 1,888 tool-use dialogues\nfrom 2,138 APIs spanning 1,000 distinct domains. Using this dataset, we train Lynx, a\ntool-augmented LLM initialized from Alpaca.\nExperimental results demonstrate that GPT-3.5\nexhibits improved tool utilization compared to\nGPT-3, while GPT-4 excels in planning. However, there is still significant potential for further improvement. Moreover, Lynx surpasses\nAlpaca’s tool utilization performance by more\nthan 26 pts and approaches the effectiveness of\nGPT-3.5. Through error analysis, we highlight\nthe key challenges for future research in this\nfield to answer the third question 1 .\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nOver the past several years, significant progress has\nbeen made in the development of large language\nmodels (LLMs), including GPT-3 (Brown et al.,\n2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), ChatGPT, and\nimpressive GPT-4 (Bubeck et al., 2023). These\n∗\n\nEqual Contributions\nCorresponding author.\n1\nThe data and code are publicly available at\nhttps://github.com/AlibabaResearch/DAMO-ConvAI/\ntree/main/api-bank\n†\n\nmodels exhibit increasingly human-like capabilities, such as powerful conversation, in-context\nlearning, and code generation across a wide range\nof open-domain tasks (Bubeck et al., 2023).\nDespite their utility, LLMs are still constrained\nby training data (Brown et al., 2020; Zhao et al.,\n2023; Song et al., 2023). This data can quickly become outdated and may not cover all scenarios (Mialon et al., 2023). Consequently, there has been\na surge of research focused on enhancing LLMs\nby enabling them to leverage external API tools,\nsuch as accessing up-to-date information (Izacard\net al., 2022) and interacting with third-party services (Liang et al., 2023). Traditionally, tool usage\nhas been viewed as a uniquely human behavior,\nwith its emergence considered a significant milestone in primate evolution (Ambrose, 2001). Drawing an analogy to the timeline of human evolution,\nwe argue that it is currently imperative to address\nthree pivotal questions: (1) How effective are current LLMs at utilizing tools? (2) How can we\nenhance LLMs’ ability to utilize tools? (3) What\nobstacles still need to be overcome for LLMs to\neffectively leverage tools?\nTo tackle these inquiries, we present API-Bank,\na groundbreaking benchmark specifically designed\nfor tool-augmented LLMs. In order to determine\nthe users’ needs regarding the utilization of toolaugmented LLMs, we initially conducted interviews with 500 users. Taking into account their\nfeedback, we establish the design principles for\nAPI-Bank. The evaluation scope of API-Bank must\nencompass three essential capabilities: planning,\nretrieving, and calling API tools, in order to fulfill the users’ requirements. Additionally, while\nconstructing the benchmark, it is imperative to consider various aspects such as domain diversity, API\ndiversity, API authenticity, and evaluation authenticity. Subsequently, to answer the first research\nquestion, we implement the evaluation system of\nAPI-Bank, adhering to design principles. The sys-\n\n\ftem comprises 73 commonly used APIs, along with\n314 tool-use dialogues containing 753 API calls,\nall manually annotated, thus forming the first executable and authentic system capable of evaluating\nthe effectiveness of LLM utilizing tools.\nFor the second question, we develop a comprehensive tool-augmented LLM training dataset\nwithin API-Bank. This dataset comprises 2,138\ndistinct APIs and includes 1,888 dialogues with\na total of 4,149 API calls, reflecting three different levels of API usage abilities. However, annotating thousands of APIs and the corresponding dialogues, adhering to our design principles,\nis not only prohibitively expensive but also timeconsuming. In this work, we introduce Multi-agent,\na novel method using LLMs instead of humans to\nautomatically mass-produce tool-augmented LLM\ntraining data. Multi-agent consists of five collaborative agents that step-by-step generate domains,\nAPIs, user queries, API calls & responses, while\nalso ensuring the quality of the generated data\naligns with our design principles. Multi-agent remarkably reduces the annotation cost by 98% compared to human annotation. To verify the effectiveness of our training set, subsequently, we fine-tune\na popular LLM Alpaca-7B (Taori et al., 2023), resulting in our own tool-augmented LLM Lynx.\nWe conduct extensive experiments on API-Bank\nand obtained insightful results regarding the API\ntool usage capabilities of LLMs. Our findings reveal that even smaller models like Alpaca-7B and\nChatGLM-6.2B possess a certain level of API call\naccuracy, about 20%. However, their proficiency in\nAPI retrieval and planning is negligible. In contrast,\nthe larger-scale GPT-3 Davinci exhibits almost no\ncapability in API usage, indicating that API usage\nmight not be an inherent feature of LLMs. Our\nLynx, demonstrates an average improvement of\n24% in the three API usage capabilities compared\nto Alpaca-7B. While it approaches the performance\nof GPT-3.5, there remains a 21% gap from GPT-4.\nAdditionally, we present a comprehensive experimental analysis that sheds light on the primary\nchallenges encountered by GPT-4 and Lynx in utilizing APIs, addressing the third research question.\n\n2\n\nDesign Principles of API-Bank\n\nDue to the absence of authoritative ability definitions and benchmarks about tool-augmented LLM,\nwe conducted an extensive questionnaire survey\nin the initial phase. Through interviews with over\n\n500 users who expressed interest in incorporating\nadditional tools into LLM, we collected their requirements. Based on this foundation, we provide,\nfor the first time, a definition to measure the abilities of tool-augmented LLM and the data standards\nfor training and evaluating its abilities. We believe\nthat these design principles, which reflect the actual needs of users, can assist researchers in the\nfield and contribute to the future development of\ntool-augmented LLM benchmarks.\n2.1\n\nAbility Grading\n\nAn ideal tool-augmented LLM should enable users\nto define the APIs they require in a private API\nPool and request the LLM to invoke these APIs at\nthe appropriate times to fulfill their needs. Based\non our interviews with users, we have identified\ntwo dimensions that encompass the requirements\nfor tool-augmented LLM:\n• Few vs. Many APIs in Pool: Different users\nmay define varying numbers of APIs. When\nusers specify a small number of APIs (e.g.,\n2-3), the relevant information of all the specified APIs (such as name, definition, input\nparameters, and output parameters) and the\nuser’s query can be provided as input to the\nLLM, allowing it to determine which API to\ncall to meet the user’s needs. However, when\nusers have defined a large number of APIs\n(e.g., hundreds), it is not feasible to input all\nthe APIs to the LLM due to input length restrictions. In such cases, the LLM needs to\nretrieve potentially suitable APIs to fulfill the\nuser’s needs before calling them.\n• Single vs. Several API calls per Turn: Many\nuser requirements are complex and necessitate the combination of multiple API calls to\nachieve. Some users are willing to patiently\ndecompose requirements into multiple queries\nand guide the LLM gradually to fulfill their\nneeds, with each query involving a single API\ncall. But some users prefer to directly present\ncomplex requirements, expecting the LLM to\nautonomously perform step-by-step API calls\nwithin a single turn to meet their needs.\nAs shown in Figure 2, these two dimensions give\nrise to four expected conditions: Few APIs, Single\nCall; Few APIs, Multiple Calls; Many APIs, Single\nCall; and Many APIs, Multiple Calls. During implementation, we discovered that the difficulty level\n\n\fAPI Pool\n\nLLM\n\nAPI Search\n\nAPI\n\nRetrieve\n\nCall\nQuery\n\nCall\n\nResponse\n\nCall\nCall\nPlan\n\nRetrieve\n\nPlan\n\nRetrieve\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:31","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"τ -bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User\nInteraction in Real-World Domains\nShunyu Yao∗\n\nNoah Shinn\n\nPedram Razavi\n\nKarthik Narasimhan\n\narXiv:2406.12045v1 [cs.AI] 17 Jun 2024\n\nSierra\n\nAbstract\nExisting benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human\nusers or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying\nthem in real world applications. We propose τ -bench, a benchmark emulating\ndynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a\nlanguage agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines.\nWe employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database\nstate at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose\na new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple\ntrials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like\ngpt-4o) succeed on < 50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 < 25%\nin retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability\nof agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nThere is increasing excitement around the potential of language agents [20, 24, 18, 1] to enable new\nlevels of automation across various industries. However, their deployment in real-world systems\nrequires several key desiderata to be satisfied. Agents must (1) interact seamlessly with both humans\nand programmatic APIs over long horizons to incrementally gather information and resolve intents,\n(2) accurately adhere to complex policies and rules specific to a task or domain, and (3) maintain\nconsistency and reliability at scale, across millions of interactions. For instance, consider the case of\nan airline booking agent (Figure 1). When a user wants to change their flight reservation to a different\ndestination airport, the agent needs to gather the required information by interacting with the user,\ncheck the airline policies using the guidelines provided, and find new flights and (if possible) rebook\nthe user using complex reservation APIs. In addition, the agent should be consistent in its behavior\nacross different kinds of users with the same request, and robust to small changes in the conversation\nflow that should not affect the end outcome.\nModeling realistic human interaction and rule following in agent evaluation is vital for developing\nand deploying trustworthy agents in the wild, and tackling challenges like long-context reasoning\nand planning in a methodical fashion. Existing benchmarks [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] for language agents\noften feature simplified instruction-following setups, where the agent autonomously interacts with an\nenvironment (web, code terminal, or APIs) given all the information upfront, without any human-inthe-loop interaction and without the need to consult any domain-specific guidelines.\nIn this work, we introduce τ -bench (short for Tool-Agent-User Interaction Benchmark) to measure\nan agent’s ability to interact with (simulated) human users and programmatic APIs while following\ndomain-specific policies in a consistent manner. τ -bench is built in a modular framework with\n(1) realistic databases and APIs, (2) domain-specific policy documents, and (3) instructions for\n∗\n\nWork done during internship. Code and data: https://github.com/sierra-research/tau-bench.\n\nPreprint. Under review.\n\n\f(a) τ-bench setup\n\nTools\n\n(b) Example trajectory in τ-airline\n\nget_user_details\n\nbook_reservation\n\ncancel_reservation\n\nupdate_reservation_flights\n\nChange flight\n……\n\n……\n\nAgent\n\nDomain policy as system prompt\nCurrent time is 2024-5-15 15:00:00 EST.\n- Basic economy cannot be modified.\n- Basic economy cannot be cancelled after 24 hours\nof booking… (more rules omitted)\n\nUser\n\nUser instruction as system prompt\nYou are mia_li_2017, and want to change the your\nmost recent reservation to fly to SF instead of LA on\nthe same day. If change is not possible, you want the\nagent to cancel and rebook … You are concise.\n\nget_reservation_details[JK9O19]\n{‘cabin’: ‘basic_economy’,\n‘created_at’: ‘20240514-1800’…} (Read database)\nJK9O19 is basic economy and cannot\nbe changed. But since it is within\n24h, I can cancel it and book a new\none. Do you want me to do it?\nOh… In that case just cancel it\ncancel_reservation[JK9O19]\n{…, ’status’: ‘cancelled’}\n……\n\n(Write database)\n\nFigure 1: (a) In τ -bench, an agent interacts with database API tools and an LM-simulated user to\ncomplete tasks. The benchmark tests an agent’s ability to collate and convey all required information\nfrom/to users through multiple interactions, and solve complex issues on the fly while ensuring it\nfollows guidelines laid out in a domain-specific policy document. (b) An example trajectory in\nτ -airline, where an agent needs to reject the user request (change a basic economy flight) following\ndomain policies and propose a new solution (cancel and rebook). This challenges the agent in\nlong-context zero-shot reasoning over complex databases, rules, and user intents.\ndiverse user scenarios and corresponding ground truth annotations. As a first demonstration, we\nfocus on the realm of customer service and create two different domains where agents need to assist\nsimulated users with diverse requests (τ -retail and τ -airline). We leverage the generative capabilities\nof language models (LMs) for data creation and realistic human user simulation [15] in conjunction\nwith manual annotation and verification.\nWe constructed τ -bench in three stages, including manual schema and API design, LM-assisted\ngeneration of data entries, and manual scenario generation and verification for the user simulator.\nOur evaluation scheme compares the database state at the end of each episode with the ground truth\nexpected state. This allows for objective measurement of the agent’s decision making, while also\nproviding room for stochastic variation in the conversation itself, since the user may pose the same\nrequest in different ways that result in the same end state of the database. We also introduce the\nmetric of pass^k, which measures the consistency and robustness of the agent across k i.i.d. trials.\nOur experiments reveal that agents built with simple LM constructs (like function calling or ReAct)\nperform poorly, highlighting the need for more sophisticated agent architectures. For instance, even\nstate-of-the-art LMs like gpt-4o achieve low task success rates (pass^1) using function calling\n(∼61% on τ -retail and ∼35% on τ -airline). With increasing k, the chance of consistently solving a\ntask drops rapidly, to as low as ∼25% for pass^8 on τ -retail for the same model. This showcases\nthe fragile nature of such agents in handling stochasticity and partial information, which is common\nin human-agent interaction. Upon analyzing the failure cases, we find that current agents struggle\nwith complex reasoning over databases, understanding and following ad-hoc policies, and handling\ncompound (more than one) requests. We hope that τ -bench enables the evaluation and development\nof more consistent and capable agents for real-world digital tasks involving human interaction.\n\n2\n\nRelated Work\n\nMost existing benchmarks for agents and task-oriented dialogue systems focus on evaluating either\nconversational or tool-use capabilities. τ -bench aims to unify both under realistic settings, while also\ntesting how well agents can follow domain-specific policies in a consistent manner.\nBenchmarks for language agents and tool use. Several benchmarks have been developed to\nevaluate agents powered by LMs [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] . Recent efforts have focused specifically on\nevaluating tool use capabilities of LMs, i.e., their ability to generate the right function calls from a\nset of functions in an API. Projects like the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) [23],\n2\n\n\fToolBench [22] and MetaTool [11] test tool use/function calling in multiple programming languages\nand propose various methods for evaluating the accuracy of function calls. ToolEmu [16] uses\nlanguage models themselves to emulate the execution of tools, with a focus on exposing potential\nsafety risks when LM agents fail to use tools correctly. However, all these works only contain a\nsingle-step user interaction, where the human interacting with the agent provides an initial instruction\ncontaining all the required information. In contrast, our benchmark focuses on a more realistic setting\nwhere the agent has to interact with human users to gather information and authorization.\nTask-oriented dialogue. Task-oriented dialogue has been a long-standing challenge for NLP, with\nseveral efforts over the years to build domain-specific offline datasets or user simulators. The former\ntypes of benchmarks are static and only test the conversational agent on pre-collected conversation\ntrajectories[4, 3, 2]. The latter either rely on user simulators that are rule-based or rely on symbolic\nspecifications [17, 8] or perform tests with real humans through crowdsourcing platforms [9]. Some\nvery recent work explores the use of LMs as response raters to train dialogue systems [10] or evaluates\ntheir capability for simulating users [7]. τ -bench leverages the powerful text generation capabilities\nof state-of-the-art LMs to simulate realistic user utterances and long-context conversations using\ntextual scenario descriptions, with the goal of evaluating agents. The stochastic sampling from LMs\nallows for diverse yet faithful variations in the dialogue when re-run with the exact scenario – this is\nextremely useful for testing agent consistency, as we show in § 5.1.\nUser simulation with LMs. Our work is also related to recent efforts on using LMs as simulators\nof human characters. This includes papers on simulating non-player characters (NPCs) in text\nadventure games [13] multiple agents in human-like societies [15] or specific collaborative tasks [21],\nand enabling human-in-the-loop interaction for tasks like online shopping [6] or web search [28].\nHowever, all these past works have not used such simulators to benchmark the reliability of agents,\ninstead focusing on showcasing the ability of LMs to enable realistic simulations. Our work uses\nsuch realistic user simulation to provide an accurate assessment of the reliability and robustness of\nAI agents for deployment in systems that undertake millions of real-world interactions with humans.\n\n3\n\nτ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction\n\nEach individual task in τ -bench can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process\n(POMDP) (S, A, O, T , R, U) with state space S, action space A, observation space O, transition\nfunction T : S × A → S × O, reward function R : S → [0, 1], and instruction space U. The agent\ninteracts with both (1) databases (db) via API tools, and (2) a (simulated) user (user) to complete a\ntask, i.e., S = Sdb ⊗ Suser , A = Adb ∪ Auser , O = Odb ∪ Ouser . In addition, the agent is provided a\ndomain-specific policy document containing rules it must adhere to – one can think of this as partially\ndescribing the world model of the domain. We describe each component in more detail below.\nDatabases and APIs. Each τ -bench domain has several databases and associated APIs. The\ncontents of the database form the state sdb (Figure 2a), which is hidden from the agent and the\nuser, and can only be read from or written to using API actions adb , which are usually in the\nform tool_name(**kwargs). When an action is executed on the database, the transition Tdb :\n(sdb , adb ) 7→ (s′db , odb ) is deterministic and implemented as a Python function (Figure 2b).\nDomain policy. Each domain has a policy (Figure 2c) that explains the domain databases, task procedures, and restrictions for the agent to follow in its interactions. Some restrictions are implemented\nas checks in the API, e.g., using a payment ID not in the user profile will lead to odb = \"Error:\npayment not found\", and others not, e.g., the airline policy states different baggage allowances for\ndifferent membership statuses and cabin classes, but the agent needs to fill in the number of baggage\nitems to be paid for in the book_reservation API, similar to the freedom given real-world agents.\nUser simulation. We use a language model (gpt-4-0613) to simulate a human user interacting with\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Here we propose a new method for a general construction\nof optimally localized Wannier functions from such Chern bands. We do so through an optimal gauge\nchoice of the Bloch states of a Chern band with the singularity placed at any desired position in\nmomentum space. We apply this method to construct the optimally localized Wannier functions for\nkagome lattice, and use it to identify channels of interactions that are favorable to the development\nof fractional Chern insulators. Implications of the approach for the interplay between correlations\nand topology in broader contexts are discussed.\n\nIntroduction. Correlated flat bands provide a rich setting to realize a variety of quantum phases and their transitions [1–4]. Recent years have seen increasing recognition of the potential of the correlated flat band materials for merging the topology of electronic wavefunctions\nand strong correlations that go beyond the single particle\npicture [5, 6]. In addition to tunable flat bands of moiré\nsystems [3, 4], there has been rapid recent progress on\nflat band physics in frustrated lattice systems with electron motions experiencing a destructive interference [7–\n9]. In systems such as kagome (and pyrochlore) metals,\nisolated topological flat bands develop from the interplay between lattice geometry and spin-orbit coupling.\nWhen the Coulomb interaction is large enough to mix\nthe flat and accompanying wide bands (but still smaller\nthan the width of the wide bands), the notion of compact\nmolecular orbitals allows for the representation of the flat\nand wide bands in real space [10–12]. The molecular orbitals, exponentially localized, provide a means to treat\nthe effect of Coulomb interactions in terms of a topological Kondo lattice [13, 14]. This route has led to a\nphase diagram containing a quantum critical point with\nstrange metallicity. The theoretical prediction has since\nbeen supported by pressure-tuning experiments in a new\nkagome metal [15].\nAn alternative regime is realized when the Coulomb\ninteraction is larger than the flat bandwidth but still\nsmaller than both the energy gap separating the flat and\nwide bands and the width of the wide bands. In this\nregime, the flat bands are to be considered in isolation.\nFor kagome metals, it is this regime which pertains to\nfractional Chern insulators (FCI) [16].\nTo be specific, consider the case of a flat Chern band,\nwith width Dflat , the energy gap ∆g that separates it\nfrom wide bands, and the interaction strength U . When\nthese three energy scales satisfy the following hierarchy,\nthe system can be described by keeping only the Chern\n\n∗ fx7@rice.edu\n\nband states as the active Hilbert space [17, 18]:\n(Dflat , U ) ≪ ∆g .\n\n(1)\n\nTo treat the effect of interactions, it is desirable to represent the bands in real space. The projection of the\nCoulomb interactions in the real space basis is important\nnot only for studying the non-perturbative effects of the\ninteractions in general, but also to analyze the competition between the fractional Chern insulator and other\ncorrelated phases.\nGeneral construction of Wannier functions. Wannier\nfunctions, which are defined as the discrete Fourier transformations of Bloch states [19], form an orthogonal complete basis in the Hilbert space spanned by the bands\nof interest. When the gauge choice of the Bloch states\nare smooth over the whole Brillouin zone, the Wannier\nfunctions can be exponentially localized in real space [20].\nAs such, the Wannier functions allow for the construction\nof a real space effective model [21]. In one dimensional\nsystems, one can always construct a smooth gauge for\na band that is gapped from other bands by finding the\neigenstates of the projected position operator to minimize the spread of the Wannier function [22]. In two\ndimensions, in contrast, such a construction fails due to\nthe noncommutativity of the two components of the projected position operator. Thus, numerical methods are\nusually necessary to find optimal Wannier functions in\ntwo dimensions. Another closely related issue is the difficulty in using Wannier functions when there is topological obstruction. To overcome the difficulty, one needs\nto consider the complementary bands to trivialize the\nband topology, as discussed for example in the context\nof twisted bilayer graphene [23–25]. When the topological nontrivial band is considered alone, the existence of a\nnon-zero Chern number in a two dimensional system prohibits a globally smooth gauge, and the Wannier function\nof a Chern band can only exhibit a power-law decay in\nreal space [26–29]. In using such Wannier functions, minimizing the spread becomes extremely important. For\ncertain Chern band systems, including the lowest Lan-\n\n\f2\nthese Wannier functions to be useful in understanding\nother correlated phenomena in generic Chern bands as\nwell.\nGauge fixing.\nThe Wannier function localization\nfunctional (WFLF), which quantitatively measures the\nspread of the Wannier function, is denoted as F [W ] and\ndefined as the variance of the position operator [21].\nFor an isolated energy band gapped from other bands,\nthe WFLF can also be interpreted as a functional of\na U (1) gauge transformation defined over the Brillouin\nzone F [χ(k)]. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:38","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"1\n\nParabolic dielectric reflector for extreme on-chip\nspot-size conversion with broad bandwidth\nLaureano Moreno-Pozas, Miguel Barona-Ruiz, Robert Halir, José de-Oliva-Rubio, Jorge Rivas-Fernández, Iñigo\nMolina-Fernández, J. Gonzalo Wangüemert-Pérez, Alejandro Ortega-Moñux\n\narXiv:2501.03206v1 [physics.optics] 6 Jan 2025\n\nAbstract\nSpot-size converters are key for efficient coupling of light between waveguides of different sizes. While adiabatic tapers are\nwell suited for small size differences, they become impractically long for expansion factors around ×100 which are often required\nwhen coupling integrated waveguides and free-space beams. Evanescent couplers and bragg deflectors can be used in this scenario,\nbut their operation is inherently limited in bandwidth. Here we propose a solution based on a parabolic dielectric interface that\ncouples light from a 0.5 µm-wide waveguide to a 285 µm-wide waveguide, i.e. an expansion factor of ×570. We experimentally\ndemonstrate an unprecedented bandwidth of more than 380 nm with insertion losses below 0.35 dB. We furthermore provide\nanalytical expressions for the design of such parabolic spot-size-converters for arbitrary expansion factors.\n\nSilicon photonics is a burgeoning technology for optical communications due to its compatibility with complementary metaloxide-semiconductor (CMOS) processes, which enables the integration of photonic circuits and microelectronics [1], [2], [3].\nSpot-size converters (SSC) are key components in such photonic systems as they enable efficient coupling between waveguides\nof different sizes. In particular, SSCs are often used to feed gratings that couple light to fibers [4], or project it into free-space\n[5], [6], [7], [8]. The latter requires spot-size expansions to hundreds of micrometers to achieve beams with Rayleigh ranges\nof the order of centimeters [7]. This is achieved by first using a SSC to expand light on-chip in the lateral direction from a\nsingle-mode waveguide to the desired width (typically exceeding 100 µm). The resulting expanded mode then feeds a weak\ngrating that gradually couples the light into free-space, thereby providing the desired longitudinal expansion [5]. In this scenario\ntraditional adiabatic tapering cannot provide the required spot size conversion within reasonable footprints and therefore other\nsolutions are preferred in the literature.\nSilicon-nitride (SiN) platforms have leveraged evanescent couplers to feed very large gratings [5], [6], [7], [9]. However,\nin these SSCs the propagating angle of the expanded modes varies with wavelength. This is due to variations in the effective\nindex of the mode traveling through evanescently coupled waveguide and leads to oblique incidence on the grating which\ncauses spurious changes in its radiation angle [5]. In the Silicon-on-insulator (SOI) platform, SSC designs based on Bragg\ndeflectors [10], [11], [12] have been presented. However, due to their resonant nature these SSCs are also strongly wavelength\ndependent. Integrated lenses could potentially overcome these bandwidth limitations, but so far designs have been limited to\nMFD below 40 µm [13], [14].\nAn interesting approach based on parabolic dielectric reflectors in SOI was presented in [15]. Based on total internal reflection,\nan expansion from a single mode waveguide to a MFD of 7 µm was demonstrated, with losses below 0.25 dB in a bandwidth\nof 100 nm. Here, we show, for the first time, how this approach can be exploited for extreme SSCs, as illustrated in Fig. 1. We\nderive analytical expressions to i) minimize power loss due to imperfect total internal reflection , and (ii) minimize the area of\nthe device. Using this approach we demonstrate coupling from a the fundamental TE mode of a 0.5 µm wide waveguide to the\nfundamental mode of a 285 µm wide waveguide, with measured losses below 0.35 dB across a bandwidth exceeding 380 nm,\nlimited by the measurement setup. In terms of the bandwidth × expansion figure of merit, this is an 98-fold improvement\nwith respect to the state-of-the-art. We believe that this broadband behavior paves the way for the development of different\napplications, including free-space projection, grating couplers, far-field trapping sensors and wireless chip interconnects, in\nwhich one of the limiting performance factors is the reduced bandwidth of conventional beam expanders.\nThe geometry of the SSC is depicted in Fig. 1(a) and (b), expanding the fundamental TE mode from a Win = 0.5 µm wide\nphotonic wire with a thickness of 220 nm to an output waveguide with a width of Wout . Both the top and bottom claddings are\nsilicon dioxide, with a refractive index nc = 1.44 at λ0 = 1.55 µm. Its operation is analogous to an offset parabolic antenna,\nwith the dielectric interface acting as reflector with a focal length F . The parabolic interface follows the equation [15]\nx2\n.\n(1)\n4F\nThe input waveguide at port P 1 is adiabatically tapered over a length Lt to a width Wap , as shown in Fig. 1b. Light then\ntravels through a free propagation region, experiencing diffraction with a divergence angle θdiv , until it reflects at the parabolic\ninterface and eventually reaches the output port with width Wout .\nThe aperture size Wap governs the divergence angle as\ny=\n\nθdiv =\n\n2.35λ0\n,\nWap nslab\n\n(2)\n\n\f2\n\n(a)\n\nnc\n\nP2\n\n(b)\n\nx\n\nLct\n\nRemoved\nzones\nsince there\nis no field\n\nLcv\n\nnslab\n\nx\nParabolic\ninterface\n\nSi\n\nL\n\nx\n\nθdiv\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:39","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Resistive relativistic magnetohydrodynamics without Ampère’s Law\nRuben Lier,1, 2, 3, ∗ Akash Jain,1, 2, 3, † Jay Armas,1, 2, 3, 4, ‡ and Oliver Porth5, §\n\narXiv:2501.04638v2 [astro-ph.HE] 9 Sep 2025\n\n1\n\nInstitute for Theoretical Physics, University of Amsterdam, 1090 GL Amsterdam, The Netherlands\n2\nDutch Institute for Emergent Phenomena, 1090 GL Amsterdam, The Netherlands\n3\nInstitute for Advanced Study, University of Amsterdam,\nOude Turfmarkt 147, 1012 GC Amsterdam, The Netherlands\n4\nNiels Bohr International Academy, The Niels Bohr Institute,\nUniversity of Copenhagen, Blegdamsvej 17, DK-2100 Copenhagen Ø, Denmark\n5\nAnton Pannekoek Institute, Science Park 904, 1098 XH, Amsterdam, The Netherlands\n(Dated: September 11, 2025)\nResistive magnetohydrodynamics is thought to play a key role in transient high-energy astrophysical\nphenomena such as flares from black hole and neutron star magnetospheres. When performing\nnumerical simulations of resistive magnetohydrodynamics, one is faced with the issue that Ampère’s\nlaw becomes stiff in the high conductivity limit which poses challenges to the numerical evolution.\nWe show that using a description of resistive magnetohydrodynamics based on higher-form symmetry,\none can perform simulations with a generalized dual Faraday tensor without having to use Ampère’s\nLaw, thereby avoiding the stiffness problem. We also explain the relation of this dual model to\na traditional description of resistive magnetohydrodynamics and how causality is guaranteed by\nintroducing second order corrections.\n\n1.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nRelativistic magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) provides a\npowerful means to study and model diverse high-energy\nastrophysical phenomena [1–4]. While the broad-brush\nglobal dynamics is well described by dissipationless “ideal”\nMHD, the formation of small scale (turbulent) structures\ninevitably leads to localized dissipation sites. A wellknown example are reconnecting current-sheets, which\nare able to drive high-energy particle acceleration leading to non-thermal transient emission [5–11]. Ultimately,\nin the small-scale current-sheets, magnetic reconnection\nis required to release magnetic energy and change the\ntopology of the magnetic field lines. However, as dictated\nby Alfven’s theorem [12], magnetic reconnection only occurs when one accounts for finite plasma resistivity. Thus,\nwhile the magnetic Reynolds number (which signifies the\nratio between system- and resistive-scales) of many astrophysical systems is extremely large [13], when zooming\ninto current sheets to study microscopic effects, resistivity\nneeds to be included in the model explicitly [14–17]. To\ndo so, in the relativistic case, one traditionally introduces\nthe electric field as a nonhydrodynamic variable, whose\nlifetime is set by the conductivity that enters as a damping\nterm in Ampère’s law, thereby introducing stiffness to the\nequations. This stiffness forces the time-step in explicit\nsimulations to be extremely small which can be avoided\nby considering more complex numerical schemes, namely\nthe implicit-explicit solver method (IMEX) [18].\nIn this work, we take a different approach to solving\nthe issue of magnetic dissipation, which involves thinking\n\n∗ rlier@uva.nl\n\ndifferently about what resistive MHD means compared to\nits traditional formulation. Electromagnetism has a oneform global symmetry [19] that is responsible for enforcing\nthe conservation of magnetic field lines as the associated\nstring-like Noether charge. Therefore, instead of viewing\nresistive MHD as a limit of relativistic hydrodynamics\ncoupled to electromagnetism, we can interpret it as a dissipative fluid with one-form symmetry, termed one-form\nMHD [20–24]. This dual viewpoint entirely circumvents\nthe pathological characteristic of resistive MHD because,\ninstead of generating reconnection through a large relaxation term, resistivity is introduced as a small coefficient\nwhich forms the magnetic equivalent of viscosity. We show\nthis by developing a numerical scheme for simulating astrophysical plasmas based on a causal model that avoids\nstiffness and is able to handle strong shocks at velocities\nclose to the speed of light.\n\n2.\n\nDISSIPATIVE MHD AS ONE-FORM FLUID\n\nWhen describing relativistic plasmas involved in astrophysical phenomena, the relevant conserved hydrodynamic\nquantities are four-momentum and mass density, with\nthe respective stress-energy tensor T µν and mass current\nρµ . Furthermore, a magnetohydrodynamic plasma has a\ndynamical magnetic field, which can be viewed as a oneform “string density”. The associated conserved current\nis the dual Faraday’s tensor J µν = 12 ϵµνρσ Fρσ , which is\nconserved due to the Bianchi identity, where Fµν is the\nelectromagnetic field strength tensor. The conservation\nlaws are thus given by\n∇ν T µν = 0 ,\n\n∇ν J µν = 0 ,\n\n∇ν ρν = 0 .\n\n(1)\n\n† ajain@uva.nl\n‡ j.armas@uva.nl\n§ o.j.g.porth@uva.nl\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Our results encompass the Radon number, Helly number, colorful Helly\nnumber, fractional Helly number, colorful fractional Helly theorem, (p, q) theorem, and two\nkinds of colorful (p, q) theorems for these convexity spaces. The primary tools employed in our\nproofs involve simplicial complexes and collapsibility.\n\nKeywords Helly theorem; (p, q) theorem; d-interval; convexity space\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\n1.1\n\nHelly-type theorems and d-collapsibility\n\nIn 1923, Helly [Hel23] published a foundational theorem on the intersection patterns of convex\nsets in Rd , where d is a positive integer. Specifically, for a finite family F of convex sets in Rd ,\nHelly’s theorem states that if every at most d + 1 members of F have a non-empty intersection, then\nall members of F intersect.\nOver the years, this result has been generalized and extended in various directions. Notable\nexamples include the colorful Helly theorem, first proven by Lovász and later fully detailed by\nBárány [Bár82]; the fractional Helly theorem introduced by Katchalski and Liu [KL79]; and the\ncolorful fractional Helly theorem, also developed by Bárány et al. [BFM+ 14]. Other significant\nadvancements include the (p, q) theorem by Alon and Kleitman [AK92], as well as two variations\nof the colorful (p, q) theorems by Bárány and Matoušek [BM03] and Bárány et al. [BFM+ 14],\nrespectively. For more details, refer to [BK22, Bár21]. Rather than immediately delving into these\ntheorems, we first introduce the concepts of abstract simplicial complex and d-collapsibility, which\nwas introduced by Wegner in [Weg75]. The d-collapsibility is a powerful tool for establishing a series\nof Helly-type theorems.\nLet V be a finite set. A collection K of subsets of V is an abstract simplicial complex if for\nany α ∈ K and β ⊆ α, we have β ∈ K. Elements of K are called faces of K. The dimension\nof a face α ∈ K is defined as dim α := |α| − 1. The dimension of a complex K is defined as\ndim K := max{dim α : α ∈ K}. Let T\nI be a finite set of indices and F be a family of sets (Ci )i∈I .\nThe nerve of F is K(F) := {J ⊆ I : j∈J Cj ̸= ∅, J is finite}. One can easily verify that the nerve\nis an abstract simplicial complex.\nA face σ ∈ K is free if it is contained in a unique inclusion-maximal face. An elementary\nd-collapse of K is a step in which a free face σ with dim σ ≤ d − 1 and all faces containing σ are\nremoved. Moreover, we denote the result by coll(K, σ) = K \\ {τ ∈ K : σ ⊆ τ }. A simplicial complex\nK is d-collapsible if K can be reduced to the empty complex by a sequence of elementary d-collapses.\nFor more details on the notion related to d-collapsibility and the consequence of d-collapsibilty, refer\nto [Tan13]. In this paper, we shall use the following recent result.\n∗ Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, Institutskiy per. 9, 141700, Dolgoprudny, Russia. Email address:\nraowei1998@gmail.com\n\n1\n\n\fTheorem 1.1 (The optimal colorful fractional Helly theorem for d-collapsible complexes [BGT21]).\nLet K be a d-collapsible simplicial complex with the set of vertices N = N1 ⊔ · · · ⊔ Nd+1 divided into\nd + 1 disjoint subsets of sizes |Ni | = ni , respectively. If K contains at least αn1 . . . nd+1 colorful\nd-faces, where α ∈ (0, 1], that is, faces σ with |σ ∩Ni | = 1 for every i ∈ [d+1]. Then there is an index\ni ∈ [d+1] such that the dimension of the induced subcomplex on Ni is at least (1−(1−α)1/(d+1) )ni −1,\nthat is, dim K[Ni ] ≥ (1 − (1 − α)1/(d+1) )ni − 1.\n\n1.2\n\nSeparated d-intervals\n\nNow, we are ready to introduce our research subjects and some related results. A homogeneous\nd-interval is a union of at most d convex sets from the real line R. A separated/nonhomogeneous\nd-interval is a homogeneous d-interval consisting of d convex set components I (1) , . . . , I (d) such that\nI (i+1) ⊆ (i, i + 1) for 0 ≤ i ≤ d − 1, where (i, i + 1) is an open interval. For convenience, we employ\nthe following equivalent definition.\nDefinition F\n1.2. A separated\nS d-interval I is a disjoint union of d convex sets from the real line R,\nthat is, I = i∈[d] I (i) = i∈[d] {(x, i) ∈ R × [d] : x ∈ I (i) ⊆ R}, where all I (i) are convex sets in R.\nMoreover, we say I (i) is the i-th level of I and any point (x, i), where x ∈ R, is in the i-th level.\nThe reader may realize that there is some relation between the axis-parallel boxes in Rd and\nthe separated d-intervals. For more details, refer to Section 10.2.\nLet F be a finite family of subsets on the ground set X. The transversal number of F, denoted\nby τ (F), is the minimum integer k such that there is a set S ⊆ X of size at most k such that\nS ∩ C ̸= ∅ for any C ∈ F. The matching number of F, denoted by ν(F), is the maximum integer k\nsuch that there is a subfamily F ′ ⊆ F of size k, consisting of pairwise disjoint sets. Tardos [Tar95]\nand Kaiser [Kai97] proved the following results.\nTheorem 1.3 (Tardos [Tar95], Kaiser [Kai97]). If F is a finite family of homogeneous d-intervals,\nthen τ (F) ≤ (d2 − d + 1)ν(F). If F is a finite family of separated d-intervals, then τ (F) ≤ (d2 −\nd)ν(F).\nThen Frick and Zerbib [FZ19] established the colorful version. Recently, a sparse colorful version\nand a matroid colorings version were proven in [MZ24] and [McG24], respectively.\n\n1.3\n\nAbstract convexity spaces and main results\n\nIn this paper, we shall study the convexity spaces constructed on separated d-intervals. Moreover, throughout the paper, we will use the term d-intervals to refer to separated d-intervals.\nDefinition 1.4. A convexity space is a set system C on a ground set X that satisfies the following\nthree properties:\n1. ∅, X ∈ C;\n2. V. Tsvetkova,1 Ya. I. Rodionov,2, 3 and P. D. Grigoriev4, 1\n1\n\narXiv:2501.00341v2 [cond-mat.mes-hall] 22 Jan 2025\n\nNational University of Science and Technology MISIS, Moscow, 119049 Russia\n2\nInstitute for Theoretical and Applied Electrodynamics,\nRussian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, 125412 Russia\n3\nNational Research University Higher School of Economics, Moscow 101000, Russia\n4\nL.D. Landau Institute of Theoretical Physics, RAS, Chernogolovka, 143423, Russia\n(Dated: January 23, 2025)\nWe study the effects of imperfect nesting in the density wave (DW) state on various electronic\nproperties within a simple 2D tight-binding model. The discussed model reflects the main features\nof quasi-1D metals where the DW emerges. We show that a DW with imperfect nesting leads to\nunusual singularities in the quasiparticle density of states and to a power-law renormalization of\nthe superconducting critical temperature. Our results are derived at arbitrary large antinesting and\nmay help to understand the phase diagram of the wide class of density-wave superconductors. We\nalso compute the conductivity tensor in a wide temperature range, including the DW transition,\nand obtain a satisfactory agreement with the experimental data on rare-earth trichalcogenides and\nmany other DW materials.\nPACS numbers: 72.10.-d, 72.15.Gd, 71.55.Ak, 72.80.-r\n\nI.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nThe interplay between superconductivity (SC) and\ncharge or spin density wave (DW) attracts a vast research activity. The SC-DW competition and coexistence appears in various strongly-correlated electron\nsystems, including the high-temperature cuprate [1–9]\nand iron-based [10–15] superconductors, NbSe2 [16–18]\nand other transition-metal and rare-earth di and polychalcogenides [19–22], various organic superconductors\n[23–31], and many other materials (see, e.g., Refs. [32–\n35] for reviews).\nThe DW in metals creates a spatial modulation of the\ncharge or spin density of conducting electrons with the\nwave vector Q and opens a gap ∆ at the Fermi level in\nthe electronic spectrum, which reduces the electron energy [36]. Depending on the electron dispersion ϵ(k) in\nthis metal, two different DW states are possible. The\nfirst, called the perfect nesting of the Fermi surface (FS),\noccurs when ϵ(k) + ϵ(k + Q) < ∆ for all k on the FS.\nThen the DW covers the entire FS and the metal becomes\na semiconductor in a DW state. In this case a uniform\nSC on a DW background is hardly possible, but SC may\nappear via a spatial segregation with DW, as happens in\nvarious materials [29, 31, 37–39]. This SC heterogeneity\ncan be visualized by the scanning tunneling microscopy\n(STM) and spectroscopy [37, 40–48], by the local diamagnetic probe [49, 50], or detected and analyzed using the resistivity anisotropy measurements [51–56]. The\nspatial DW-SC phase segregation usually appears on a\nlarge length scale, greater than the DW coherence length\nξDW = ℏvF /(π∆), as it happens in organic superconductors [29, 31, 55], or on the microscopic scale, i.e. in the\nform of DW soliton walls [57–59].\nThe second scenario of DW-SC coexistence appears in\nthe case of imperfect FS nesting when ϵ(k) + ϵ(k + Q) >\n\n∆0 for some k at the FS. In this case the metallic\nconductivity survives in a DW state till T → 0, being anisotropically reduced, e.g., as in various rare-earth\nthree-chalcogenides [60] and many other materials [32–\n34, 36]. One could expect an exponential decrease of the\nsuperconducting transition temperature Tc in the presence of the DW background even in the imperfect-nesting\ncase, because according to the BCS theory in the weakcoupling regime\nTc ∼ ωD exp(−1/gνF )\n\n(1)\n\nexponentially depends on the electron density of states\n(DoS) at the Fermi level νF multiplied by the coupling\nconstant g, with the Debye frequency ωD in the preexponential factor. As the DW opens a gap, at least\nin some parts of FS, the DoS νF is reduced by the DW,\nleading to destructive SC-DW interference [61, 62]. However, usually, one observes a more complicated SC-DW\ninterplay: the superconducting transition temperature\nTc is the highest at the quantum critical point (QCP)\nwhere the density wave (DW) gets suppressed by some\nexternal parameter, such as doping level [1, 2, 4–7], pressure [10–13, 23–29], cooling rate [30, 31], disorder [17],\netc. The Tc dome-like shape near the DW QCP results\nfrom the enhancement of electron-electron (e-e) interaction g → g∗ (Q) in the Cooper channel by the critical\nDW fluctuations, which can be described as the SC vertex renormalization in the language of Feynman diagram\ntechnique [63, 64]. Note that this vertex renormalization\nchanges its momentum dependence and may favor the\nunconventional superconductivity [65–67].\nBelow we consider only the second scenario of the uniform DW in the case of imperfect nesting. What is\nstronger, the SC coupling renormalization g → g∗ (Q)\nor the DoS reduction νF → νF ∗ by the DW? The answer\nto this question dramatically affects the SC transition\n\n\f2\ntemperature in the weak-coupling regime given by Eq.\n(1).\nAccording to the theoretical calculations [63–66] the\nSC coupling enhancement g → g∗ (Q) is not very strong,\nespecially far from the DW QCP, when the DW order\nparameter ∆ is larger than the SC energy gap. On the\ncontrary, the gapped FS area in the DW state is, usually,\nrather large, as visualized by ARPES measurememts in\nvarious compounds [9, 68–77]. How the observed increase\nof Tc in the presence of the DW, implying the increase of\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
