{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:48","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"arXiv:2407.19116v1 [math.NA] 26 Jul 2024\n\nA Novel Gaussian filter-based Pressure Correction\nTechnique with Super Compact Scheme for Unsteady 3D\nIncompressible, Viscous Flows\nAshwani Punia1 , Rajendra K. Ray2\n1,2. School of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi,\nMandi, Himachal Pradesh, 175005, India\nmr.punia11@gmail.com, rajendra.iitmandi.ac.in\n\nAbstract\nThis work deals with a novel Gaussian filter-based pressure correction technique with a\nsuper compact higher order finite difference scheme for solving unsteady three-dimensional\n(3D) incompressible, viscous flows. This pressure correction technique offers significant\nadvantages in terms of optimizing computational time by taking minimum iterations to\nreach the required accuracy, making it highly efficient and cost-effective. Pressure fields often exhibit highly nonlinear behavior, and employing the Gaussian filter can help to enhance\ntheir reliability by reducing noise and uncertainties. On the other hand, the super compact\nscheme uses minimum grid points to produce second-order accuracy in time and fourthorder accuracy in space variables. The main focus of this study is to enhance the accuracy\nand efficiency and minimize the computational cost of solving complex fluid flow problems. The super compact scheme utilizes 19 grid points at the known time level (i.e., nth\ntime level) and only seven grid points from the unknown time level (i.e., n + 1 time level).\nBy employing the above strategies, it becomes possible to notably decrease computational\nexpenses while maintaining the accuracy of the computational scheme for solving complex\nfluid flow problems. We have implemented our methodology across three distinct scenarios: the 3D Burger’s equation having analytical solution, and two variations of the lid-driven\ncavity problem. The outcomes of our numerical simulations exhibit a remarkable concordance, aligning exceptionally well with both the analytical benchmarks and previously validated numerical findings for the cavity problems. This robust agreement underscores the\nefficacy and reliability of our approach. Furthermore, we conducted a detailed analysis of\nthe pressure correction technique in terms of streamline contours and pressure correction\nresults. The findings of this study have significant implications for various engineering and\nscientific disciplines, such as aerodynamics, hydrodynamics, and fluid-structure interaction\nanalysis.\n\nKeywords:\nThree-dimension, Convection-Diffusion Equation, Super Compact Scheme, Higher Order Accuracy, Pressure Correction Technique.\n\n\f1\n\nIntroduction\n\nNumerical simulations have a crucial role in understanding complex fluid flow phenomena and\npredicting their behavior in various engineering applications. The Navier-Stokes equations,\ngoverning the dynamics of fluid motion, are fundamental in such simulations. These developments have occurred across three prominent methodologies: finite element, finite volume,\nand finite difference methods. While finite difference methods excel in their ease of implementation, both finite element and finite volume approaches offer greater flexibility when it\ncomes to directly applying them to irregular domains. Nonetheless, it’s worth acknowledging\nthat every approach presents a unique array of benefits and hurdles. Notably, the finite difference approach stands out as the most straightforward to implement. Over the past few years,\na multitude of specialized methods have been pioneered, e.g., high-order compact (HOC) finite difference (FD) schemes known for their computational efficiency. Numerous researchers\nhave innovated with fourth-order compact finite difference schemes, specifically designed for\nconvection-diffusion equations on both two-dimensional uniform grids [18,21,29,30] and threedimensional spaces [11, 13, 19, 32]. The finite difference approach relies primarily on Taylor\nseries expansion, a powerful mathematical tool used to approximate functions. This simplicity\nin implementation has contributed to its popularity among researchers and engineers seeking\nto tackle complex fluid flow problems. By discretizing the governing equations into a grid-like\nstructure, finite difference methods enable the conversion of continuous partial differential equations into discrete algebraic equations that can be numerically solved on a computer. It is important to emphasize that most finite difference schemes developed to address the Navier-Stokes\nequations have been tailored primarily for the context of two-dimensional (2D) flows. Notably,\nthese schemes, extensively applied in the stream function-vorticity (ψ − ω) formulation, have\nbeen the focus of studies conducted by researchers such as Ghia et al. (1982) [1], Lecointe\nand Piquet (1984) [3], Weinan and Liu (1996) [9], Kupferman et al. (2001) [16], Tian et al.\n(2003) [23], Kalita and Chhabra (2006) [26], Ray et al. (2010) [29], Wang et al. (2019) [35] and\nYadav et al. (2023) [36]. Consequently, due to their inherent limitations, these finite difference\nschemes were unable to be readily extended to handle three-dimensional (3D) flows. Only a\nsmall number of cases exist where high-order compact (HOC) schemes were specifically designed for three-dimensional convection-diffusion equations. However, even in these instances,\nthe application scope remained restricted to scenarios involving steady-state linear convectiondiffusion or the solution of 3D Poisson equations (Gupta and Kouatchou (1998) [12], Zhang\net al. (2000) [14, 15], McTaggart et al. (2004) [24]). The challenges in extending these HOC\nschemes to more complex 3D flows have remained a subject of ongoing research and exploration\nin the field of numerical simulations.\nHowever, solving these equations can be computationally intensive, especially for three - dimensional problems or at high Reynolds numbers, which require fine grids and numerous time steps.\nTo address these challenges, researchers have explored various numerical methods and techniques to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of simulations. One such approach is the Modified\nCompressibility technique [6], wherein pressure is iteratively solved to fulfill the incompressibility constraint within the Navier-Stokes equations. Although widely used, this technique can\nstill impose significant computational costs, especially for large-scale problems. Kalita [32]\n\n\fintroduced an exceptionally efficient super compact higher-order scheme for simulating 3D incompressible flow. This approach utilized a modified compressibility method for pressure calculation, which, while effective, did have a significant drawback in terms of computational cost\nwhen applied to solving 3D fluid flow problems. It requires lots of pressure iterations at each\ntime level, resulting in heightened computational costs.\nIn today’s rapidly evolving computational simulation landscape, optimizing efficiency without\ncompromising accuracy is a paramount goal. Reducing computational costs is crucial, especially\nin the context of simulating 3D fluid flow problems, where the computational demands are significantly high. In this context, we propose a new and efficient pressure correction technique that\nreduces the computational cost while maintaining high accuracy. Our approach is inspired by the\nneed to accelerate the convergence of pressure iterations in the Modified Compressibility technique and enhance the overall efficiency of numerical simulations. By introducing a Gaussianbased pressure correction technique to the pressure field, we aim to achieve faster convergence\nrates and lower computational costs without compromising accuracy. The Gaussian filter is a\ncommonly used image processing technique employed to enhance or modify images by reducing\nnoise and smoothing out details while preserving the overall structure. This observation serves\nas inspiration for applying Gaussian filtering techniques in the context of pressure fields, where\nits capacity to reduce errors and fluctuations is particularly valuable. In this study, we present our\nproposed pressure correction technique and its application to three different fluid flow problems.\nFirstly, we investigate the validation of the three-dimensional Burger’s equation, a fundamental\nnonlinear partial differential equation that serves as an ideal test case due to its known analytical\nsolutions. We conduct a comparison between our computed results and the analytical solutions\nto assess the accuracy and efficiency of the scheme. Next, we apply our novel technique to\ntwo variations of the lid-driven cavity problem, a classic benchmark in fluid dynamics. The 2D\nlid-driven square cavity has been extensively studied, but its 3D counterpart remains relatively\nunexplored due to its increased complexity and memory requirements. Our approach allows\nus to efficiently tackle this challenging 3D problem at different Reynolds numbers. Throughout our investigation, we systematically analyze the computational efficiency and importance\nof our proposed technique. We conduct a comparison of outcomes obtained through varying\ntime steps and Reynolds numbers to demonstrate the robustness and versatility of our approach.\nAdditionally, we investigate the flow patterns and pressure contours in the lid-driven cavity to\ngain deeper insights into the fluid behavior under different conditions. In summary, this study\naims to contribute to the advancement of numerical simulations in fluid dynamics by proposing\na novel and efficient pressure correction technique. The success of our approach in accurately\ncapturing flow phenomena while reducing computational costs has significant implications for a\nwide range of applications, from fundamental research to engineering simulations.\n\n2\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:49","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"LLM Chain Ensembles for Scalable and Accurate\nData Annotation\nDavid Farr∗† , Nico Manzonelli2, Iain Cruickshank‡ , Kate Starbird∗ and Jevin West∗\n∗ University of Washington, Seattle, WA\n† Army Cyber Technology and Innovation Center, Augusta, GA\n\narXiv:2410.13006v2 [cs.LG] 1 Nov 2024\n\n‡ Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA\n\nAbstract—The ability of large language models (LLMs) to\nperform zero-shot classification makes them viable solutions\nfor data annotation in rapidly evolving domains where quality\nlabeled data is often scarce and costly to obtain. However, the\nlarge-scale deployment of LLMs can be prohibitively expensive.\nThis paper introduces an LLM chain ensemble methodology\nthat aligns multiple LLMs in a sequence, routing data subsets\nto subsequent models based on classification uncertainty. This\napproach leverages the strengths of individual LLMs within a\nbroader system, allowing each model to handle data points where\nit exhibits the highest confidence, while forwarding more complex\ncases to potentially more robust models. Our results show that\nthe chain ensemble method often exceeds the performance of the\nbest individual model in the chain and achieves substantial cost\nsavings, making LLM chain ensembles a practical and efficient\nsolution for large-scale data annotation challenges.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nQuality-labeled data is fundamental to the development of\nmachine learning (ML) models, which facilitate advanced data\nanalysis in decision-making processes across both research\nand industry. Obtaining structured data with accurate labels\npresents significant challenges, particularly in dynamic fields\nlike Computational Social Science (CSS), where classification\nobjectives are less defined and may change frequently due\nto current events. Additionally, traditional data annotation\nmethods that rely on human annotators are expensive and\ntime-consuming making them impractical for rapidly evolving\ndomains that require massive datasets. In response, there is\na growing trend of using large language models (LLMs) for\ndata annotation [1], [2], [3].\nLLMs are a promising solution to fields with dynamic\ndata annotation requirements due to their ability to classify\ninstances that were previously unseen in training data, also\nknown as zero-shot classification [4]. Zero-shot classification\noffers advantages in speed, scalability, and flexibility. However, despite these benefits, the use of LLMs also introduces\nnew complexities. As the volume of data increases, so do\nthe computational demands and associated costs of deploying\nhigh-parameter LLMs. Furthermore, the financial burden of\naccessing these models through commercial APIs can become\nprohibitively high, particularly for large-scale annotation tasks.\nFinally, zero-shot annotation by LLM alone is usually insufficient to produce a high-quality, labeled dataset [5], [6].\nIn this paper, we introduce an LLM chain ensemble method\nto address the challenge of obtaining accurate labels for text\n\nFig. 1. This is a system diagram of our chaining methodology. The system\ndepicts routing paths for subsets of data to pass to subsequent LLMs informed\nby a calculated confidence metric. After all data has been labeled by at least\none LLM, we assign final labels informed by our rank-based ensemble.\n\ndata in a time, computational resource, and cost-efficient manner. Previous work in text classification for CSS tasks using\nLLMs focuses mainly on tasks such as sentiment, stance, ideology, or misinformation detection [1], [7], [5]. These works\nplace an emphasis on prompt engineering techniques and\nevaluating performance comparisons across different LLMs.\nOur approach marks a significant departure from this trend.\nRather than focusing on prompt engineering or comparing\nLLMs, we treat individual LLMs as integral components\nwithin a broader system designed to enhance classification\nperformance and reduce costs.\nOur proposed methodology aligns LLMs in a sequence,\nrouting subsets of the data that each LLM is uncertain about to\nsubsequent models in the sequence based on zero-shot classification uncertainty measures. This approach enables each LLM\nin the chain to label the data in which it is most confident,\nwhile forwarding more challenging instances to subsequent,\npotentially more robust models. After each LLM-link assigns\nlabels to its data, the predictions and their corresponding\nconfidence scores are aggregated using a rank-based ensemble\nmethod to derive the final labels. This hierarchical structure\nallows earlier, ideally, less costly or resource-intensive LLMs,\nto handle easier examples to classify, reserving more complex\ncases for later stages in the chain that may benefit from an\nensemble.\nOur chain ensemble approach demonstrates the capacity to\n\n\fexceed the performance of the most accurate or expensive\nindividual model in the chain, despite using that model to\nlabel only a fraction of the dataset. Our approach improves on\nexisting benchmarks across three distinct zero-shot CSS tasks\nusing straightforward prompting techniques.\nIn contrast to more intricate prompting methods, such as\nchain-of-thought or tree-of-thought, our approach deliberately\nminimizes token usage by constraining LLM responses to a\nsingle word. This strategy not only increases classification\nspeed but also offers substantial cost savings, delivering up to\na 90-fold reduction in expenses compared to other complex\nprompt engineering techniques employed with commercial\nLLMs, all while maintaining comparable or superior performance. This combination of cost-efficiency, speed, and high\nperformance underscores the practicality and effectiveness of\nour chain ensemble methodology in large-scale data annotation\ntasks.\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:50","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Compact superconducting vacuum-gap capacitors with low microwave loss and high\nmechanical coherence for scalable quantum circuits\nAmir Youssefi,1, 2, 3, ∗ Mahdi Chegnizadeh,2, 3, ∗ Marco Scigliuzzo,2, 3 and Tobias J. Kippenberg2, 3, †\n1\n\nEDWATEC SA, EPFL Innovation Park, Lausanne, Switzerland.\nInstitute of Physics, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland.\n3\nInstitute of Electrical and Micro Engineering, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland.\n\narXiv:2501.03211v2 [quant-ph] 15 May 2025\n\n2\n\nVacuum-gap capacitors have recently attracted significant interest in superconducting circuit platforms due to their compact design and exceptionally low dielectric losses in the microwave regime.\nTheir intrinsic ability to support mechanical vibrational modes makes them well-suited for circuit\noptomechanics. However, precise control over the gap size and the realization of high-coherence\nmechanical modes remain longstanding challenges. Here, we present a detailed and scalable fabrication process for vacuum-gap capacitors that support ultra-high-coherence mechanical motion,\nexhibit low microwave loss, and occupy a significantly smaller footprint compared to conventional\nplanar geometries. By employing a planarized SiO2 sacrificial layer, we achieve vacuum gaps on\nthe order of 150 nm. Using this platform, we have recently demonstrated ground-state cooling\nand motion squeezing of a mechanical oscillator with a quality factor of 40 million—a 100-fold\nimprovement compared to prior works—as well as a single-photon optomechanical coupling rate\nof approximately 15Hz [1]. Additional achievements include the realization of an optomechanical\ntopological lattice with 24 sites [2] and the observation of quantum collective dynamics in a mechanical hexamer [3]. Collectively, these results underscore the potential of vacuum-gap capacitors as\na platform for coupling superconducting qubits to mechanical systems, enabling quantum storage,\nand probing gravitational effects in quantum mechanics.\n\n1.\n\nIntroduction\n\nOver the past two decades, quantum control of mechanical systems has been firmly established, following the quantum control of individual atoms [4] and\nions [5] in the first wave of development, and superconducting circuits [6] in the second wave. This progress\nhas been particularly catalyzed by cavity optomechanics [7], which utilizes radiation-pressure coupling between mechanical oscillators and electromagnetic cavities. More recently, these advancements have been extended to coupling mechanical systems with superconducting qubits via quantum acoustics [6, 8]. These developments have paved the way for quantum optomechanics [9], enabling breakthroughs such as the cooling of lowfrequency mechanical oscillators to their quantum ground\nstate [10, 11]—unattainable with passive cooling alone.\nOther milestones include the generation of entanglement\nbetween electromagnetic fields and mechanical oscillators [12, 13], entanglement among macroscopic mechanical oscillators [14, 15], observation of quantum sideband\nasymmetry in micromechanical oscillators [1, 16], realization of back-action-evading measurements of mechanical\nmotion [17, 18], ponderomotive squeezing of light [19],\nquantum-coherent coupling of light and mechanical oscillators [20], and real-time quantum feedback control of\nmechanical oscillators [21]. Furthermore, quantum optomechanics has spurred novel quantum technological innovations, such as interfaces for converting microwave to\noptical fields with minimal added noise [22, 23] and have\nbeen used to amplify microwave signals [24].\n\nA particularly promising platform for the quantum\ncontrol of mechanical oscillators is circuit optomechanics [10, 25], where a mechanically compliant vacuumgap capacitor is shunted with an inductor to form a\nmicrowave resonator. These capacitors were first introduced in the field of circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) to reduce losses in microwave resonators by\neliminating the lossy dielectric layer typical of capacitors and increasing the participation ratio of the electric field in vacuum [26]. Over the years, such circuits\nhave enabled remarkable achievements, including mechanical ground-state cooling [10], even below the backaction limit [27], mechanical squeezing [28–30], entanglement [12, 14, 15, 31], non-classical state storage [32, 33],\nand non-reciprocal circuits [34–36]. However, despite\nthese experimental advances, the design and fabrication\nprocesses for vacuum-gap capacitors have not kept pace.\nIn particular, circuit optomechanics remains hindered by\nlimited mechanical quality factors, predominantly dictated by the fabrication methods employed for vacuumgap capacitors, as well as variations in microwave and\nmechanical properties. These limitations pose significant\nchallenges to scaling up and realizing large-scale lattices.\nIn this work, we present a comprehensive account of\nthe detailed fabrication process for low-loss vacuum gap\ncapacitors, tailored for circuit quantum optomechanics\nand circuit quantum electrodynamics applications, and\noutline the challenges we encountered. We introduce the\n“flat-geometry” vacuum gap capacitor, developed to address the limitations of the conventional platform [29, 37–\n39], and describe the full optimization process. As shown\nin Fig. 1a, the flat geometry offers significant advantages,\nenabling precise control of both the gap and the mechanical frequency through the lithographic process. Chester,a Tobias Hansenb and De-liang Zhonga\na\nb\n\nBlackett Laboratory, Imperial College, Prince Consort Road, London, SW7 2AZ, UK\nDepartment of Mathematical Sciences, Durham University, Stockton Road, Durham, DH1 3LE, UK\n\nE-mail: s.chester@imperial.ac.uk, tobias.p.hansen@durham.ac.uk,\nd.zhong23@imperial.ac.uk\nAbstract: We consider tree level scattering of gravitons in type IIA string theory on AdS4 ×\nCP3 to all orders in α′ , which is dual to the stress tensor correlator in U (N )k ×U (N )−k ABJM\ntheory in the planar large N limit and to all orders in large λ ∼ N/k. The small curvature\nexpansion of this correlator, defined via a Borel transform, is given by the flat space VirasoroShapiro amplitude plus AdS curvature corrections. We fix curvature corrections by demanding\nthat their resonances are consistent with the superconformal block expansion of the correlator\nand with a worldsheet ansatz in terms of single-valued multiple polylogarithms. The first\ncorrection is fully fixed in this way, and matches independent results from integrability, as well\nas the R4 correction at finite AdS curvature that was previously fixed using supersymmetric\nlocalization. We are also able to fix the second curvature correction by using a few additional\nassumptions, and find that it also satisfies various non-trivial consistency checks. Most notably, because the user can give\nand take back control over the UI at any point, UI assistants\ncan provide varying levels of automation ranging from\npartial assistance (such as finding a menu or filling a form) to\ncomplete task execution (like placing an order), akin to the\nsix levels of automation in autonomous driving (SAE, 2021).\n\nWe study the use of large language model-based\nagents for interacting with software via web\nbrowsers. Unlike prior work, we focus on measuring the agents’ ability to perform tasks that span\nthe typical daily work of knowledge workers utilizing enterprise software systems. To this end, we\npropose WorkArena, a remote-hosted benchmark\nof 33 tasks based on the widely-used ServiceNow\nplatform. We also introduce BrowserGym, an\nenvironment for the design and evaluation of such\nagents, offering a rich set of actions as well as multimodal observations. Our empirical evaluation\nreveals that while current agents show promise\non WorkArena, there remains a considerable gap\ntowards achieving full task automation. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:56","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"τ -bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User\nInteraction in Real-World Domains\nShunyu Yao∗\n\nNoah Shinn\n\nPedram Razavi\n\nKarthik Narasimhan\n\narXiv:2406.12045v1 [cs.AI] 17 Jun 2024\n\nSierra\n\nAbstract\nExisting benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human\nusers or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying\nthem in real world applications. We propose τ -bench, a benchmark emulating\ndynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a\nlanguage agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines.\nWe employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database\nstate at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose\na new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple\ntrials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like\ngpt-4o) succeed on < 50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 < 25%\nin retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability\nof agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nThere is increasing excitement around the potential of language agents [20, 24, 18, 1] to enable new\nlevels of automation across various industries. However, their deployment in real-world systems\nrequires several key desiderata to be satisfied. Agents must (1) interact seamlessly with both humans\nand programmatic APIs over long horizons to incrementally gather information and resolve intents,\n(2) accurately adhere to complex policies and rules specific to a task or domain, and (3) maintain\nconsistency and reliability at scale, across millions of interactions. For instance, consider the case of\nan airline booking agent (Figure 1). When a user wants to change their flight reservation to a different\ndestination airport, the agent needs to gather the required information by interacting with the user,\ncheck the airline policies using the guidelines provided, and find new flights and (if possible) rebook\nthe user using complex reservation APIs. In addition, the agent should be consistent in its behavior\nacross different kinds of users with the same request, and robust to small changes in the conversation\nflow that should not affect the end outcome.\nModeling realistic human interaction and rule following in agent evaluation is vital for developing\nand deploying trustworthy agents in the wild, and tackling challenges like long-context reasoning\nand planning in a methodical fashion. Existing benchmarks [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] for language agents\noften feature simplified instruction-following setups, where the agent autonomously interacts with an\nenvironment (web, code terminal, or APIs) given all the information upfront, without any human-inthe-loop interaction and without the need to consult any domain-specific guidelines.\nIn this work, we introduce τ -bench (short for Tool-Agent-User Interaction Benchmark) to measure\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:57","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"A PILE OF SHIFTS I: CROSSED PRODUCTS\n\narXiv:2412.00854v1 [math.OA] 1 Dec 2024\n\nSHELLEY HEBERT, SLAWOMIR KLIMEK, AND MATT MCBRIDE\nAbstract. We discuss C∗ -algebras associated with several different natural shifts on the\nHilbert space of the s-adic tree, that is, the tree of balls in the space of s-adic integers.\n\n1. Introduction\nIt is well known that Cantor sets could be treated as the boundary of a directed rooted\ntree [16] via Michon correspondence. Consider a s-regular infinite directed rooted tree called\nthe s-adic tree. We adopt here the following definition of such a tree: all vertices have the\nout-degree equal to s and all vertices except the root have the in-degree equal to one (the\nin-degree of the root is equal to zero). For such a tree, the boundary is the set of infinite\nout-paths starting at the root. Those can be naturally identified with the ring of s-adic\nintegers Zs , see equation (2.1).\nThe vertices V of the s-adic tree can be thought of as corresponding to balls in Zs , with\nball inclusion defining edges of the s-adic tree, which can be considered as a coarse grained\napproximation of the space of s-adic integers. The corresponding Hilbert space ℓ2 (V) carries\na straightforward, diagonal representation of the algebra of continuous functions on Zs . In\nprevious papers [9], [11], [12] of some of the authors, a version of a unilateral shift on ℓ2 (V)\nwas used to construct a “forward” derivative leading to a spectral triple over C(Zs ). However,\nthere are other interesting shifts on that Hilbert space corresponding to different arithmetic\nand geometric aspects of the space of s-adic integers.\nThe purpose of this paper and the companion paper [8] is to study natural C∗ -algebras in\nℓ2 (V) generated by C(Zs ) along with specific shifts. Those algebras are interesting examples\nof C∗ -algebras with connections to number theory and combinatorics, a subject of much\ninterest [4], [18].\nGiven a C∗ -algebra A and an endomorphism there is a number of ways to construct a new\nC∗ -algebra, desirably an extension of A, called the crossed product. Often an intermediate\nextension procedure is needed to enlarge the algebra A up to a better behaved “coefficient”\nC∗ -algebra. This paper serves as a source of interesting examples of such constructions and\nwe determine how each of these shift algebras can be identified with a crossed product by an\nendomorphism, in fact a monomorphism with a hereditary range. In the companion paper\nwe delve deeper into the structure of the shift algebras and compute their K-Theory.\nThis paper is organized as follows. In the next section we introduce four shifts on the s-adic\ntree and the corresponding C∗ -algebras. Then we discuss coefficient algebras and the related\ncrossed products. Particularly useful here is the reference [13] which considers, in general,\nC∗ -algebras generated by a given concrete C∗ -algebra and a partial isometry. What makes\nDate: December 3, 2024.\n1\n\n\f2\n\nHEBERT, KLIMEK, AND MCBRIDE\n\nour considerations special is the existence of what we call a “gauge action”, a symmetry that\nleaves C(Zs ) invariant.\nAfter the preliminaries, each algebra is studied in turn in its own dedicated section. While\nthe algebras are quite different, there are number of similarities. Propositions 4.2, 5.2, 6.5 and\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Ideally, users are allowed to roam\naround freely, while audiovisual content is delivered wirelessly to\ntheir Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs). Therefore, truly immersive experiences will require massive amounts of data, in the\nrange of tens of gigabits per second, to be delivered reliably at\nextremely low latencies. We identify Millimeter-Wave (mmWave)\ncommunications, at frequencies between 24 and 300 GHz, as a\nkey enabler for such experiences. In this article, we show how\nthe mmWave state of the art does not yet achieve sufficient\nperformance, and identify several key active research directions\nexpected to eventually pave the way for extremely-high-quality\nmmWave-enabled interactive multi-user XR.\nIndex Terms—Extended Reality, Virtual Reality, MillimeterWave, Beamforming, Multimedia Streaming\n\nwireless connectivity using Wi-Fi on the 5 GHz frequency\nband. However, as uncompressed XR content may require tens\nof gigabits per second, a high compression rate is applied,\nresulting in increased latency and visual artifacts. Achieving\nhigh Quality of Experience (QoE) in XR is further complicated\nby the fact that it requires a motion-to-photon latency of at\nmost 20 ms, to avoid cybersickness [1]. This encompasses the\ntotal latency between a user’s motion and the corresponding\nupdate of the visual image on the HMD. These requirements\nbecome even more stringent in interactive multi-user XR\nexperiences, where users interact with each other, as well\nas with the virtual or hybrid environment. They may be colocated, or may participate from different physical locations.\nEnabling such seamless interactivity requires extremely low\nlatency, alongside dense multi-user and wide-area connectivity.\n\nI. Given that the recent\nstudy in TMS simulation suggests a noticeable difference in\noutcomes when using different anatomical details, cortical shape\nshould play a more significant role in deciding the optimal TMS\ncoil pose. In this work, we introduce an image-guided robotic\nsystem for TMS that focuses on (1) establishing standardized\nplanning methods and heuristics to define a reference (true\nzero) for the coil poses and (2) solving the issue that the\nmanual coil placement requires expert hand-eye coordination\nwhich often leading to low repeatability of the experiments.\nTo validate the design of our robotic system, a phantom study\nand a preliminary human subject study were performed. Our\nresults show that the robotic method can half the positional\nerror and improve the rotational accuracy by up to two orders\nof magnitude. The accuracy is proven to be repeatable because\nthe standard deviation of multiple trials is lowered by an order\nof magnitude. The improved actuation accuracy successfully\ntranslates to the TMS application, with a higher and more\nstable induced voltage in magnetic field sensors.\n\nI. It can be used in research to map\nbrain function, investigate central disease mechanisms, and\nstudy spatial orientation perception [1]. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:60","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Sharp electromagnetically induced absorption via balanced interferometric excitation\nin a microwave resonator\nMichael T. Hatzon, Graeme R. Flower, Maxim Goryachev, Jeremy F. Bourhill, and Michael E. Tobar\n\narXiv:2410.01333v3 [physics.ins-det] 6 Feb 2025\n\nQuantum Technologies and Dark Matter Labs, Department of Physics,\nUniversity of Western Australia, 35 Stirling Highway, Crawley, WA 6009, Australia.∗\n(Dated: February 7, 2025)\nA cylindrical TM0,1,0 mode microwave cavity resonator was excited using a balanced interferometric configuration that allowed manipulation of the electric field and potential within the resonator\nby adjusting the phase and amplitude of the interferometer arms driving the resonator. With precise tuning of the phase and amplitude, 25 dB suppression of the electric field at the resonance\nfrequency was achieved while simultaneously resonantly enhancing the time-varying electric-scalar\npotential. Under these conditions, the system demonstrated electromagnetically induced absorption\nin the cavity response due to the annulment of the electric field at the resonance frequency. This\nphenomena can be regarded as a form of extreme dispersion, and led to a sharp increase in the cavity\nphase versus frequency response by an order of magnitude when compared to the cavity Q-factor.\nThis work presents an experimental setup that will allow the electric-scalar Aharonov-Bohm effect\nto be tested under conditions involving a time-varying electric-scalar potential, without the presence\nof an electric field or magnetic vector potential, an experiment that has not yet been realised.\n\nI.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nIn classical physics, electromagnetic fields are considered physical observables [1], with field measurements being a standard procedure in many applications. These\nfields exert forces on charges through the Lorentz force.\nAlternatively, potentials like the electric-scalar poten⃗ offer a\ntial V and the magnetic-vector potential A\ndifferent way to describe classical electrodynamics [2],\nthough their physical influence on charged particles is\nless straightforward than that of the fields. In 1959,\nAharonov and Bohm (AB) published a paper demonstrating that potentials can interact with charges without the direct influence of electromagnetic fields, leading to an accumulated phase (now known as a Berry\nphase). This effect, often observed as the phase shift of an\nelectron wavefunction, has been confirmed and measured\nthrough interactions with quantum dots for example [3].\nAdditional theoretical support [4] and experimental evidence [5] strongly validate the interaction of charge with\nthe magnetic-vector potential. There has also been an\nexperiment that measured charge interactions involving\nboth the scalar-electric and magnetic-vector potentials,\nalthough it occurred in the presence of fields [6].\nThe AB effect [4] suggests that in quantum mechanics, scalar and vector potentials hold a more significant\nphysical role than what classical electromagnetism implies. In classical physics, a particle’s motion can be entirely described by fields, as the Lorentz force relies on\nfields, charges, and currents. In contrast, quantum mechanics requires a more rigorous approach through the\ncanonical formalism, which necessitates the inclusion of\npotentials in the fundamental equations of motion. This\nincorporation has been demonstrated to lead to effects\n\n∗ 22873723@student.uwa.edu.au, michael.tobar@uwa.edu.au\n\nsuch as shifts in interference fringes in particle beams\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:62","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Gradient Rewiring for Editable Graph Neural Network Training\n\narXiv:2410.15556v2 [cs.LG] 25 Oct 2024\n\nZhimeng Jiang1 , Zirui Liu2 , Xiaotian Han3 , Qizhang Feng1 , Hongye Jin1 ,\nQiaoyu Tan4 , Kaixiong Zhou5 , Na Zou6 , Xia Hu7\n1\nTexas A&M University, 2 University of Minnesota, 3 Case Western Reserve University,\n4\nNYU Shanghai, 5 North Carolina State University, 6 University of Houston, 7 Rice University\nOctober 29, 2024\nAbstract\nDeep neural networks are ubiquitously adopted in many applications, such as computer vision, natural\nlanguage processing, and graph analytics. However, well-trained neural networks can make prediction\nerrors after deployment as the world changes. Model editing involves updating the base model to correct\nprediction errors with less accessible training data and computational resources. Despite recent advances\nin model editors in computer vision and natural language processing, editable training in graph neural\nnetworks (GNNs) is rarely explored. The challenge with editable GNN training lies in the inherent\ninformation aggregation across neighbors, which can lead model editors to affect the predictions of other\nnodes unintentionally. In this paper, we first observe the gradient of cross-entropy loss for the target\nnode and training nodes with significant inconsistency, which indicates that directly fine-tuning the base\nmodel using the loss on the target node deteriorates the performance on training nodes. Motivated by\nthe gradient inconsistency observation, we propose a simple yet effective Gradient Rewiring method for\nEditable graph neural network training, named GRE. Specifically, we first store the anchor gradient of\nthe loss on training nodes to preserve the locality. Subsequently, we rewire the gradient of the loss on the\ntarget node to preserve performance on the training node using anchor gradient. Experiments demonstrate\nthe effectiveness of GRE on various model architectures and graph datasets in terms of multiple editing\nsituations. The source code is available at https://github.com/zhimengj0326/Gradient_rewiring_editing.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nGraph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated exemplary performance for graph learning tasks, such as\nrecommendation, link prediction, molecule property analysis (Ying et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2020; Hu et al.,\n2020; Jiang et al., 2022; Duan et al., 2022; Han et al., 2022; Ling et al., 2023). With message passing, GNNs\nlearn node representations by recursively aggregating the neighboring nodes’ representations. Once trained,\nGNN models are deployed to handle various high-stake tasks, such as credit risk assessment in financial\nnetworks (Petrone and Latora, 2018) and fake news detection in social networks (Shu et al., 2017). However,\nthe impact of erroneous decisions in such influential applications can be substantial. For instance, misplaced\ncredit trust in undetected fake news can lead to severe financial loss.\nAn ideal approach to tackle such errors should possess the following properties: 1) the ability to rectify\nsevere errors in the model’s predictions, 2) the capacity to generalize these corrections to other similar\ninstances of misclassified samples, and 3) the ability to preserve the model’s prediction accuracy for all\nother unrelated inputs. To achieve these goals, various model editing frameworks have been developed to\nrectify errors by dynamically adjusting the model’s behavior when errors are detected (Sinitsin et al., 2020;\nMitchell et al., 2021). The core principle is to implement minimal changes to the model to correct the error\nwhile keeping the rest of the model’s behavior intact. However, model editing is not a simple plug-and-play\nsolution. These frameworks often require an additional training phase to prepare for editing before they\n1\n\n\fcan be used effectively for editing (Sinitsin et al., 2020; Mitchell et al., 2021, 2022; De Cao et al., 2021).\nAlthough model editing techniques have shown significant utility in computer vision and language models,\nthere is rare work focused on rectifying critical errors in graph data. The unique challenge arises from the\ninherent message-passing mechanism in GNNs when edits involve densely interconnected nodes (Liu et al.,\n2023b; Zhong et al., 2024). Specifically, editing the behavior of a single node can unintentionally induce a\nripple effect, causing changes that propagate throughout the entire graph. (Liu et al., 2023b) theoretically\nand empirically demonstrate the complexity of editing GNNs through the lens of the loss landscape of\nthe Kullback-Lieber divergence between the pre-trained node features and edited final node embeddings.\nMoreover, a simple yet effective model structure, named EGNN, is proposed with stitched peer multi-layer\nperception (MLP), where only the stitched MLP is trained during model editing.\nIn this work, we investigate the model editing problem for GNNs from a brand-new gradient perspective,\nwhich is compatible with existing work (Liu et al., 2023b). Specifically, we first found a considerable\ninconsistency between the gradients of the cross-entropy loss for the target node and the training nodes for\nGNNs. Such inconsistency implies that direct fine-tuning of the base model using the loss of the target node\ncan lead to a deterioration in the performance on the training nodes. Motivated by the above observation,\nwe propose a simple yet effective Gradient Rewiring method for Editable graph neural network training,\nnamed GRE. Specifically, we first calculate and store the anchor gradient of the loss on the training nodes.\nThis anchor gradient represents the original learning direction that we wish to preserve. Then, during the\nediting process, we adjust the gradient of the loss on the target node based on the stored anchor gradient.\nThis adjustment, or “rewiring”, ensures that the changes made to the target node do not adversely affect the\nperformance on the training nodes. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method for\nvarious model structures and graph datasets. Moreover, the proposed method is compatible with the existing\nEGNN baseline and further improves the performance.\nCora\n\nFlickr\n\nArxiv\n\nA-photo\n\nFigure 1: (a) Top: RMSE distance between the gradients of cross-entropy loss over training datasets and over the\ntargeted sample for different architectures. (b) Middle: Cross-entropy loss over training datasets when the model is\nupdated using target loss. (c) Bottom: Cross-entropy loss over the targeted sample when the model is updated using\ntarget loss.\n\n2\n\n\f2\n\nPreliminary and Related Work\n\nWe first introduce the notations used throughout this paper. A graph is given by G = (V, E) ,where\nV = (v1 , · · · , vN ) is the set of nodes indexed from 1 to n, and E = (e1 , · · · , em ) ⊆ V × V is the set of edges.\nn = |V| and m = |E| are the numbers of nodes and edges, respectively. Let X ∈ Rn×d be the node feature\nmatrix, where d is the dimension of node features. A ∈ Rn×n is the graph adjacency matrix, where Ai,j = 1\n1\n1\nif (vi , vj ) ∈ E else Ai,j = 0. Ã = D̃ − 2 (A + I)D̃ − 2 is the normalized adjacency matrix, where D̃ is the\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:63","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"SOME GAUSSIANITY CRITERIA FOR KHINCHIN FAMILIES\n\narXiv:2501.04375v2 [math.PR] 16 Jan 2025\n\nVÍCTOR J. MACIÁ\nAbstract. In this note we establish simple and verifiable analytical conditions for\na power series f in the class K, and its associated Khinchin family, to be Gaussian. We give several moment criteria for general power series with non-negative\ncoefficients in K and also provide several applications of these criteria.\n\n1 Introduction\nP∞\nTo each power series f (z) = n=0 an z n ∈ K, with non-negative Taylor coefficients\nand having radius of convergence R > 0 (see the definition of the class K below),\nwe can assign a family of discrete random variables (Xt )t∈[0,R) with values in the\nnon-negative integers. For each t ∈ (0, R) the random variable Xt has mass function\nan tn\nP(Xt = n) =\n, for any integer n ≥ 0 ,\nf (t)\nand we define X0 ≡ 0. The theory of Khinchin families examines the behavior of this\nfamily of random variables, and its normalized version, as t ↑ R, see [3], [4] and [5]\nfor further details.\np\nFor each t ∈ (0, R), we denote the normalization of Xt by X̆t = (Xt − E(Xt ))/ V(Xt ),\nwhere E and V represent the expectation and variance, respectively. We say that a\npower series f ∈ K, or its associated Khinchin family, is Gaussian if\nd\n\nX̆t → N(0, 1),\n\nd\n\nas t ↑ R ,\n\nwhere → denotes convergence in distribution and N(0, 1) means standard normal.\nEquivalently, by virtue of Lévy’s continuity theorem, this holds if and only if we have\nthe following pointwise convergence of the characteristic function:\n2\n\nlim E(eiθX̆t ) = e−θ /2 ,\nt↑R\n\nfor any θ ∈ R.\n\nThe question of whether a power series in the class K is Gaussian or not is central\nin the theory of Khinchin families. Some of the most relevant classes of functions\nin this theory, such as the class of strongly Gaussian power series and the Hayman\nfunctions, are contained in the class of Gaussian power series, see, for instance, [3] for\nthe definition of these classes and also for a proof of this fact.\nThis question is significant in its own right as well; classifying the limit distributions\nof Khinchin families associated with power series having non-negative coefficients\n(specifically, power series in the class K) is an interesting question.\nIn this note we provide simple and verifiable analytical conditions for a power\nseries f ∈ K, and its associated Khinchin family, to be Gaussian. We establish\n\n(Vı́ctor J. Maciá) Departamento de Matemáticas, CUNEF Universidad, Spain.\nE-mail address: victor.macia@cunef.edu.\n2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 30B10, 30D20, 60E05, 60F05.\nKey words and phrases. Power series with non-negative coefficients, Power series distributions,\nmoments, Khinchin families, Gaussian Khinchin families.\n1\n\n\f2\n\nV. J. MACIÁ\n\nmoment criteria for power series with non-negative coefficients in K and present several\napplications of these results. For instance, we prove that the ordinary generating\nfunction (OGF) for the partitions of integers with parts in a set A of positive density\nis Gaussian, and that entire functions of genus 0 with negative zeros f ∈ K, satisfying\nlimt→+∞ σf2 (t) = +∞, are also Gaussian.\n1.1 Asymptotic notations. For two function α and β, we say that α and β are\nasymptotically equivalent, as t ↑ R, denoted α(t) ∼ β(t), as t ↑ R, if\nlim\n\nα(t)\n\nt↑R β(t)\n\n= 1.\n\nSimilarly, we say that α and β are comparable, as t ↑ R, denoted α(t) ≍ β(t), as\nt ↑ R, if there are constants C, c > 0 such that\ncβ(t) ≤ α(t) ≤ Cβ(t) ,\n\nas t ↑ R.\n\nAcknowledgements. Gonzalo Wangüemert-Pérez, Alejandro Ortega-Moñux\n\narXiv:2501.03206v1 [physics.optics] 6 Jan 2025\n\nAbstract\nSpot-size converters are key for efficient coupling of light between waveguides of different sizes. While adiabatic tapers are\nwell suited for small size differences, they become impractically long for expansion factors around ×100 which are often required\nwhen coupling integrated waveguides and free-space beams. Evanescent couplers and bragg deflectors can be used in this scenario,\nbut their operation is inherently limited in bandwidth. Here we propose a solution based on a parabolic dielectric interface that\ncouples light from a 0.5 µm-wide waveguide to a 285 µm-wide waveguide, i.e. an expansion factor of ×570. We experimentally\ndemonstrate an unprecedented bandwidth of more than 380 nm with insertion losses below 0.35 dB. We furthermore provide\nanalytical expressions for the design of such parabolic spot-size-converters for arbitrary expansion factors.\n\nSilicon photonics is a burgeoning technology for optical communications due to its compatibility with complementary metaloxide-semiconductor (CMOS) processes, which enables the integration of photonic circuits and microelectronics [1], [2], [3].\nSpot-size converters (SSC) are key components in such photonic systems as they enable efficient coupling between waveguides\nof different sizes. In particular, SSCs are often used to feed gratings that couple light to fibers [4], or project it into free-space\n[5], [6], [7], [8]. The latter requires spot-size expansions to hundreds of micrometers to achieve beams with Rayleigh ranges\nof the order of centimeters [7]. This is achieved by first using a SSC to expand light on-chip in the lateral direction from a\nsingle-mode waveguide to the desired width (typically exceeding 100 µm). The resulting expanded mode then feeds a weak\ngrating that gradually couples the light into free-space, thereby providing the desired longitudinal expansion [5]. In this scenario\ntraditional adiabatic tapering cannot provide the required spot size conversion within reasonable footprints and therefore other\nsolutions are preferred in the literature.\nSilicon-nitride (SiN) platforms have leveraged evanescent couplers to feed very large gratings [5], [6], [7], [9]. However,\nin these SSCs the propagating angle of the expanded modes varies with wavelength. This is due to variations in the effective\nindex of the mode traveling through evanescently coupled waveguide and leads to oblique incidence on the grating which\ncauses spurious changes in its radiation angle [5]. In the Silicon-on-insulator (SOI) platform, SSC designs based on Bragg\ndeflectors [10], [11], [12] have been presented. However, due to their resonant nature these SSCs are also strongly wavelength\ndependent. Integrated lenses could potentially overcome these bandwidth limitations, but so far designs have been limited to\nMFD below 40 µm [13], [14].\nAn interesting approach based on parabolic dielectric reflectors in SOI was presented in [15]. Based on total internal reflection,\nan expansion from a single mode waveguide to a MFD of 7 µm was demonstrated, with losses below 0.25 dB in a bandwidth\nof 100 nm. Here, we show, for the first time, how this approach can be exploited for extreme SSCs, as illustrated in Fig. 1. We\nderive analytical expressions to i) minimize power loss due to imperfect total internal reflection , and (ii) minimize the area of\nthe device. Using this approach we demonstrate coupling from a the fundamental TE mode of a 0.5 µm wide waveguide to the\nfundamental mode of a 285 µm wide waveguide, with measured losses below 0.35 dB across a bandwidth exceeding 380 nm,\nlimited by the measurement setup. In terms of the bandwidth × expansion figure of merit, this is an 98-fold improvement\nwith respect to the state-of-the-art. We believe that this broadband behavior paves the way for the development of different\napplications, including free-space projection, grating couplers, far-field trapping sensors and wireless chip interconnects, in\nwhich one of the limiting performance factors is the reduced bandwidth of conventional beam expanders.\nThe geometry of the SSC is depicted in Fig. 1(a) and (b), expanding the fundamental TE mode from a Win = 0.5 µm wide\nphotonic wire with a thickness of 220 nm to an output waveguide with a width of Wout . Both the top and bottom claddings are\nsilicon dioxide, with a refractive index nc = 1.44 at λ0 = 1.55 µm. Its operation is analogous to an offset parabolic antenna,\nwith the dielectric interface acting as reflector with a focal length F . The parabolic interface follows the equation [15]\nx2\n.\n(1)\n4F\nThe input waveguide at port P 1 is adiabatically tapered over a length Lt to a width Wap , as shown in Fig. 1b. Light then\ntravels through a free propagation region, experiencing diffraction with a divergence angle θdiv , until it reflects at the parabolic\ninterface and eventually reaches the output port with width Wout .\nThe aperture size Wap governs the divergence angle as\ny=\n\nθdiv =\n\n2.35λ0\n,\nWap nslab\n\n(2)\n\n\f2\n\n(a)\n\nnc\n\nP2\n\n(b)\n\nx\n\nLct\n\nRemoved\nzones\nsince there\nis no field\n\nLcv\n\nnslab\n\nx\nParabolic\ninterface\n\nSi\n\nL\n\nx\n\nθdiv\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

M. Dunster · A. Gil ·\nD. Ruiz-Antolı́n · J. Segura\n\nSubmitted: Nov 27, 2024\n\nAbstract A numerical algorithm (implemented in Matlab) for computing the\nzeros of the parabolic cylinder function U (a, z) in domains of the complex\nplane is presented. The algorithm uses accurate approximations to the first\nzero plus a highly efficient method based on a fourth-order fixed point method\nwith the parabolic cylinder functions computed by Taylor series and carefully\nselected steps, to compute the rest of the zeros. For |a| small, the asymptotic\napproximations are complemented with a few fixed point iterations requiring\nthe evaluation of U (a, z) and U ′ (a, z) in the region where the complex zeros are\nlocated. Liouville-Green expansions are derived to enhance the performance\nof a computational scheme to evaluate U (a, z) and U ′ (a, z) in that region.\nSeveral tests show the accuracy and efficiency of the numerical algorithm.\nKeywords Parabolic cylinder functions · Complex zeros · Fixed point\nmethods · Matlab software.\nMathematics Subject Classification (2010) 65H05 · 33F05 · 34C10 ·\n30E10\nT. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:69","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Predictor-Feedback Stabilization of\nGlobally Lipschitz Nonlinear Systems with\nState/Input Quantization ⋆\nFlorent Koudohode ∗ Nikolaos Bekiaris-Liberis ∗\n∗\n\narXiv:2501.14696v1 [math.OC] 24 Jan 2025\n\nDepartment of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Technical\nUniversity of Crete, University Campus, Akrotiri, Chania, Greece\nAbstract: We develop a switched nonlinear predictor-feedback control law to achieve global\nasymptotic stabilization for nonlinear systems with arbitrarily long input delay, under state\nquantization. The proposed design generalizes the nonlinear predictor-feedback framework by\nincorporating quantized measurements of both the plant and actuator states into the predictor\nstate formulation. Due to the mismatch between the (inapplicable) exact predictor state and\nthe predictor state constructed in the presence of state quantization, a global stabilization\nresult is possible under a global Lipschitzness assumption on the vector field, as well as\nunder the assumption of existence of a globally Lipschitz, nominal feedback law that achieves\nglobal exponential stability of the delay/quantization-free system. To address the constraints\nimposed by quantization, a dynamic switching strategy is constructed, adjusting the quantizer’s\ntunable parameter in a piecewise constant manner—initially increasing the quantization range,\nto capture potentially large system states and subsequently refining the precision to reduce\nquantization error. The global asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system is established\nthrough solutions estimates derived using backstepping transformations, combined with smallgain and input-to-state stability arguments. We also extend our approach to the case of input\nquantization.\nKeywords: Predictor-Feedback, Nonlinear Systems, Dynamic Quantization, Backstepping\n1. INTRODUCTION\nThe challenge of compensating long input delays in nonlinear control systems has been addressed through predictorbased feedback control design techniques. These methods\nensure stability and robustness under ideal continuous-intime implementations, as demonstrated in, for example,\nthe works by Krstic (2009); Bekiaris-Liberis and Krstic\n(2013); Karafyllis and Krstic (2017a). However, real-world\napplications often involve digital implementation effects,\nsuch as sampling and quantization, which can degrade\nperformance or even destabilize the system if not properly\naddressed, see, for example, Mazenc and Fridman (2016);\nKarafyllis and Krstic (2017a). Consequently, ensuring stability under such digital implementation constraints for\nnonlinear systems with long input delays is a challenging\nproblem of theoretical and practical significance.\nTo address these challenges, previous studies investigate\nvarious digital effects in control systems under predictor\nfeedback. The impact of sampling on measurements and\ncontrol inputs is studied in Karafyllis and Krstic (2011);\nZhu and Fridman (2021); Selivanov and Fridman (2016b);\nBattilotti (2019); Weston and Malisoff (2018); whereas\nrelevant, event-triggered predictor-based designs are devel⋆ Funded by the European Union (ERC, C-NORA, 101088147).\nViews and opinions expressed are however those of the authors only\nand do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the\nEuropean Research Council Executive Agency. Neither the European\nUnion nor the granting authority can be held responsible for them.\n\noped in González et al. (2019); Mazenc et al. (2022); Nozari\net al. (2020), and Sun et al. (2022). Quantization effects in\ndelay systems are explored for linear time-delay systems\nwith saturation by Fridman and Dambrine (2009) and\nfor linear networked control systems by Liu et al. (2015),\nfor logarithmic quantizers within an event-based control\nframework by Garcia and Antsaklis (2012), for nonlinear systems with (small) measurement delay by Liberzon\n(2006), and for nonlinear systems with state delay by\nDi Ferdinando et al. (2020, 2024). Practical stabilization\nunder static quantization is also investigated in Espitia\net al. (2017) and Tanwani et al. (2016) for first-order\nhyperbolic PDE systems, while Selivanov and Fridman\n(2016a) and Katz and Fridman (2022) address parabolic\nPDE systems. Dynamic quantizers, which can adjust online their range and precision, achieving global asymptotic\nstabilization, are considered for first-order hyperbolic systems in Bekiaris-Liberis (2020), for linear systems with\ninput delay in Koudohode and Bekiaris-Liberis (2024),\nand for general infinite-dimensional discrete-time systems\nin Wakaiki (2024). There exists no result addressing the\nproblem of simultaneous compensation of long input delay\nand state/input quantization for nonlinear systems.\nIn this paper, we develop a novel, switched nonlinear\npredictor-feedback control law that achieves global asymptotic stabilization (in the supremum norm of the actuator\nstate), for nonlinear systems with state quantization. The\ndesign approach that we introduce relies on two main ingredients—a quantized version of nonlinear predictor feed-\n\n\fback, in which quantized measurements of both plant and\nactuator states are involved in the predictor state formula;\nand a dynamic switching strategy that properly adjusts\nthe quantizer’s tunable parameter in a piecewise constant\nmanner (inspired by Brockett and Liberzon (2000); Liberzon (2003) for the case of delay-free systems). In particular,\nthe control design involves two main stages, referred to as\n“zooming out” and “zooming in” phases. In the zoomingout phase, in which the system operates in open loop,\nthe quantization range expands until the system’s infinitedimensional state gets within the quantizer’s range, while\nin the zooming-in (closed-loop) phase, the range is refined\nto reduce quantization error. Stability of the closed-loop\nsystem is established using backstepping transformations,\nin combination with derivation of solutions estimates and\nutilization of small-gain and input-to-state stability (ISS)\narguments. Due to the mismatch between the (inapplicable) exact predictor state and the predictor state constructed in the presence of state quantization, a global\nstabilization result, which is established here, is possible\nunder a global Lipschitzness assumption on the vector\nfield, as well as under the assumption of existence of a globally Lipschitz, nominal feedback law that achieves global\nexponential stability of the delay/quantization-free system\n(see also, for example, Bresch-Pietri and Krstic (2014)).\nWe also study existence and uniqueness of solutions under\na locally Lipschitz assumption on the quantizers, which\nenables us to employ the results from Karafyllis and Krstic\n(2019). While such an assumption on the quantizer may\nappear as restrictive, even though it is in fact employed\nonly for analyzing existence/uniqueness and the stability\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:70","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Compact superconducting vacuum-gap capacitors with low microwave loss and high\nmechanical coherence for scalable quantum circuits\nAmir Youssefi,1, 2, 3, ∗ Mahdi Chegnizadeh,2, 3, ∗ Marco Scigliuzzo,2, 3 and Tobias J. Kippenberg2, 3, †\n1\n\nEDWATEC SA, EPFL Innovation Park, Lausanne, Switzerland.\nInstitute of Physics, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland.\n3\nInstitute of Electrical and Micro Engineering, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland.\n\narXiv:2501.03211v2 [quant-ph] 15 May 2025\n\n2\n\nVacuum-gap capacitors have recently attracted significant interest in superconducting circuit platforms due to their compact design and exceptionally low dielectric losses in the microwave regime.\nTheir intrinsic ability to support mechanical vibrational modes makes them well-suited for circuit\noptomechanics. However, precise control over the gap size and the realization of high-coherence\nmechanical modes remain longstanding challenges. Here, we present a detailed and scalable fabrication process for vacuum-gap capacitors that support ultra-high-coherence mechanical motion,\nexhibit low microwave loss, and occupy a significantly smaller footprint compared to conventional\nplanar geometries. By employing a planarized SiO2 sacrificial layer, we achieve vacuum gaps on\nthe order of 150 nm. Using this platform, we have recently demonstrated ground-state cooling\nand motion squeezing of a mechanical oscillator with a quality factor of 40 million—a 100-fold\nimprovement compared to prior works—as well as a single-photon optomechanical coupling rate\nof approximately 15Hz [1]. Additional achievements include the realization of an optomechanical\ntopological lattice with 24 sites [2] and the observation of quantum collective dynamics in a mechanical hexamer [3]. Collectively, these results underscore the potential of vacuum-gap capacitors as\na platform for coupling superconducting qubits to mechanical systems, enabling quantum storage,\nand probing gravitational effects in quantum mechanics.\n\n1.\n\nIntroduction\n\nOver the past two decades, quantum control of mechanical systems has been firmly established, following the quantum control of individual atoms [4] and\nions [5] in the first wave of development, and superconducting circuits [6] in the second wave. This progress\nhas been particularly catalyzed by cavity optomechanics [7], which utilizes radiation-pressure coupling between mechanical oscillators and electromagnetic cavities. More recently, these advancements have been extended to coupling mechanical systems with superconducting qubits via quantum acoustics [6, 8]. These developments have paved the way for quantum optomechanics [9], enabling breakthroughs such as the cooling of lowfrequency mechanical oscillators to their quantum ground\nstate [10, 11]—unattainable with passive cooling alone.\nOther milestones include the generation of entanglement\nbetween electromagnetic fields and mechanical oscillators [12, 13], entanglement among macroscopic mechanical oscillators [14, 15], observation of quantum sideband\nasymmetry in micromechanical oscillators [1, 16], realization of back-action-evading measurements of mechanical\nmotion [17, 18], ponderomotive squeezing of light [19],\nquantum-coherent coupling of light and mechanical oscillators [20], and real-time quantum feedback control of\nmechanical oscillators [21]. Furthermore, quantum optomechanics has spurred novel quantum technological innovations, such as interfaces for converting microwave to\noptical fields with minimal added noise [22, 23] and have\nbeen used to amplify microwave signals [24].\n\nA particularly promising platform for the quantum\ncontrol of mechanical oscillators is circuit optomechanics [10, 25], where a mechanically compliant vacuumgap capacitor is shunted with an inductor to form a\nmicrowave resonator. These capacitors were first introduced in the field of circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) to reduce losses in microwave resonators by\neliminating the lossy dielectric layer typical of capacitors and increasing the participation ratio of the electric field in vacuum [26]. Over the years, such circuits\nhave enabled remarkable achievements, including mechanical ground-state cooling [10], even below the backaction limit [27], mechanical squeezing [28–30], entanglement [12, 14, 15, 31], non-classical state storage [32, 33],\nand non-reciprocal circuits [34–36]. However, despite\nthese experimental advances, the design and fabrication\nprocesses for vacuum-gap capacitors have not kept pace.\nIn particular, circuit optomechanics remains hindered by\nlimited mechanical quality factors, predominantly dictated by the fabrication methods employed for vacuumgap capacitors, as well as variations in microwave and\nmechanical properties. These limitations pose significant\nchallenges to scaling up and realizing large-scale lattices.\nIn this work, we present a comprehensive account of\nthe detailed fabrication process for low-loss vacuum gap\ncapacitors, tailored for circuit quantum optomechanics\nand circuit quantum electrodynamics applications, and\noutline the challenges we encountered. We introduce the\n“flat-geometry” vacuum gap capacitor, developed to address the limitations of the conventional platform [29, 37–\n39], and describe the full optimization process. As shown\nin Fig. 1a, the flat geometry offers significant advantages,\nenabling precise control of both the gap and the mechanical frequency through the lithographic process. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:71","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"MIOV: Reordering MOVI for even better locality\nPeter Perešı́ni1 , Nathaniel K. Brown2 , Travis Gagie3 , and Ben Langmead2\n1\n\narXiv:2407.18956v1 [cs.DS] 13 Jul 2024\n\n2\n\nComenius University, Bratislava, Slovakia\nJohns Hopkins University, Baltimore, USA\n3\nDalhousie University, Halifax, Canada\n\nMäkinen, Navarro, Sirén and Välimäki [5] found that for highly repetitive datasets such\nas pangenomes, FM-indexes built atop run-length compressed Burrows-Wheeler Transforms\n(RLBWTs) can be much smaller than standard FM-indexes, while still supporting fast counting\nqueries. Policriti and Prezza [8] showed how to locate one match for a pattern P quickly while\nstill using O(r) space, where r is the number of runs in the BWT of the dataset, and Gagie,\nNavarro and Prezza [4] showed how to locate all occ matches to P quickly in the same space.\nNishimoto and Tabei [7] showed how to speed up key operations in Gagie et al.’s index by using\ntable-lookup instead of rank queries on the RLBWT, which take predecessor-query time. They\nthus achieved optimal O(|P | + occ) time when the size of the alphabet is polylogarithmic in\nthe size of the dataset, still in O(r) space. There are now several implementations [2,6,10,1] of\nNishimoto and Tabei’s index, which they called the move structure. In particular, Zakeri et al.’s\nMOVI implementation keeps all the information about a run localized so that we can perform\na backward-step, for example, not just in constant time time in theory but with only one cache\nmiss in practice.\nFor each run BWT[i..k], MOVI stores a pointer to the run containing BWT[LF(i)] and\nBWT[LF(i)]’s offset in that run, where LF(i) is the position in the BWT of the character that\nprecedes BWT[i] in the dataset. If we are backward-stepping from BWT[j] with i ≤ j ≤ k,\nthen we look up the run containing BWT[LF(i)] and BWT[LF(i)]’s offset ℓ in that run, then\ncompute BWT[LF(j)]’s offset ℓ + j − i in that run. If ℓ + j − i is greater than the length of\nthe run then we subtract the length of the run from ℓ + j − i to get BWT[LF(j)]’s offset in\nthe next run in the BWT, repeating this “run-hopping” as often as necessary. (Nishimoto and\nTabei showed how we can split the runs, without increasing the overall space by more than a\nconstant factor, such that each backward-step requires a constant number of run-hops.) If we\nalso store pointers from each run to its successor in the BWT then we can view MOVI as an\nautomaton with the runs as states — and, for example, we can reorder the runs and still be\nable to invert the BWT and recover the dataset from it. This is not a completely new idea: in\nimplementations of Durbin’s [3] Positional BWT (PBWT), we improve locality by laying out\nin memory the compressed bitvectors for the columns in the same order the columns appear in\nthe matrix; Sirén et al. [9] use a similar idea in their Graph BWT (GBWT). Indeed, Sirén first\nsuggested this line of research to us.\nGood reorderings for the PBWT and GBWT are fairly obvious, but it is not clear how we\nshould reorder MOVI. For small datasets like the one shown in Figure 1, we can find the optimal\nreordering exactly with binary integer linear programming. To see why, consider how we visit\nthe runs while inverting the BWT, shown on the left in Figure 2. We move from run 1 to run 2\nonce, from run 1 to run 9 four times, from run 2 to run 3 once, from run 3 to run 9 six times,\netc.. Overall, we move from one run to the next run memory 23/53 ≈ 43% of the time. If we\nreorder the runs such that the current run 3 appears immediately before the current run 9, then\nthe six moves will be more local. Therefore, if we say xi,j is a binary indicator variable reflecting\nwhether run i precedes run j in memory, we can find the optimal reordering by maximizing\nx1,2 + 4 x1,9 + x2,3 + x3,4 + 6 x3,9 + 4 x5,6 + 3 x5,7 +\n7 x6,7 + 6 x7,3 + 4 x7,8 + 7 x9,5 + 3 x9,10 + 3 x10,11 + 3 x11,6\n\n\fBWT context\nT $AGATACA\nT $GATACA\nT $GATTACA\nT $GATTAGA\nA $GATTAGAT\nT A$GATTAGA\nT ACAT$AGA\nT ACAT$GA\nT ACAT$GAT\nT AGAT$GAT\nT AGATA$GAT\n$ AGATACAT\nC AT$AGATA\nC AT$GATA\nC AT$GATTA\n\nBWT context\nG AT$GATTA\nG ATA$GATTA\nG ATACAT$A\nG ATACAT$\nG ATTACAT$\nG ATTAGAT$\nG ATTAGATA$\nA CAT$AGAT\nA CAT$GAT\nA CAT$GATT\nA GAT$GATT\nA GATA$GATT\nA GATACAT$\n$ GATACAT\n$ GATTACAT\n\nBWT context\n$ GATTAGAT\n$ GATTAGATA\nA T$AGATAC\nA T$GATAC\nA T$GATTAC\nA T$GATTAG\nA TA$GATTAG\nA TACAT$AG\nA TACAT$G\nT TACAT$GA\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Lee, Martı́nez-Pedroza and Rodrı́guez-Quinche define two new\ngroup invariants, the strong cop number sCop and the weak cop number wCop,\nby examining winning strategies for certain combinatorial games played on\nCayley graphs of finitely generated groups. We show that a finitely generated\ngroup G is Gromov-hyperbolic if and only if sCoppGq “ 1. We show that G is\nvirtually free if and only if wCoppGq “ 1, answering a question by Cornect and\nMartı́nez-Pedroza. We show that sCoppZ2 q “ 8, answering a question from\nthe original paper. It is still unknown whether there exist finite cop numbers\nnot equal to 1, but we show that this is not possible for CAT(0)-groups. We\nprovide machinery to explicitly compute strong cop numbers and give examples by applying it to certain lamplighter groups, the solvable Baumslag-Solitar\ngroups, and Thompson’s group F.\n\n1. Introduction\nCops and Robbers is a class of combinatorial two-player games that are usually played on graphs or more generally, on metric spaces. Lee, Martı́nez-Pedroza\nand Rodrı́guez-Quinche [LMPRQ23] introduced a variant where the existence of a\nwinning strategy for either player is invariant under quasi-isometries, making the\ngame interesting from a geometric group theory perspective. The game depends on\na parameter called the number of cops. The cop number of a graph is the smallest\nnumber of cops such that there exists a strategy for the cop player where they\nalways win. The cop number of a finitely generated group is the cop number of one\nof its Cayley graphs. In this work, we characterize the cop numbers in terms of\nwell-known geometric invariants and resolve some open questions in the literature.\nThere are two versions of the game: the strong cop game and the weak cop game,\nyielding two notions of cop numbers, which are known to be different in general.\nHyperbolic groups are known to have strong cop number 1 [LMPRQ23, Theorem\nA]. We show that the converse also holds. Thus the strong cop number provides a\nnew characterization of hyperbolic groups.\nTheorem A (Theorem 3.4). A finitely generated group G is Gromov-hyperbolic if\nand only if its strong cop number is 1.\nWe provide a similar characterization for the weak cop number. As the strong\ncop number is at most equal to the weak cop number, we know that the weak cop\nnumber can only be 1 for hyperbolic groups. However, [LMPRQ23, Corollary J]\nstates that one-ended hyperbolic groups have infinite weak cop number. We prove\nthe following theorem that was first conjectured in [CMP24, Question 1.4].\nDate: February 10, 2025.\n2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 20F67, 05C57, 51F30.\nKey words and phrases. Gromov-hyperbolic, virtually free, cop number, coarse geometry.\n1\n\n\f2\n\nRAPHAEL APPENZELLER AND KEVIN KLINGE\n\nTheorem B (Theorem 3.6). A finitely generated group G is virtually free if and\nonly if its weak cop number is 1.\nThe weak cop numbers of some non-hyperbolic groups have been calculated. We\nupgrade some of these results to the strong cop numbers. The weak cop number\nof Z2 is 8 [LMPRQ23, Theorem C], but the value of the strong cop number of Z2\nwas left as an open question [LMPRQ23, Question D], which we resolve.\nTheorem C (Theorem 4.1). For any n ě 2, Zn has infinite strong cop number.\nBy [LMPRQ23, Theorem E], groups that retract to Z2 , such as Thompson’s\ngroup F , therefore also have strong cop number 8. Cornect and Martı́nez-Pedroza\nshow that the weak cop number of lamplighter groups are 8 [CMP24, Theorem\n1.5]. When the lamp group is finite, we strengthen this result for the strong cop\nnumbers.\nTheorem D (Theorem 5.3). Let L be a non-trivial finite group. Then the lamplighter group L ≀ Z has infinite strong cop number.\nFurthermore, we find both the weak and the strong cop numbers of the solvable\nBaumslag-Solitar groups\nTheorem E (Theorem 5.6). The solvable Baumslag-Solitar groups BSp1, nq have\ninfinite weak and strong cop numbers.\nAn important concept in the proof of Theorems A, C, D and E is what we call\na meta stage: where a stage consists of the cop player taking a move followed\nby the robber player taking a move, a meta stage consists of multiple moves for\nboth players. These moves have to be taken alternatingly as per the rules of the\ngame. But by choosing them carefully, we can pretend that first one player takes all\ntheir moves within a single meta stage and then the other player gets their moves,\nwithout providing a game-changing advantage to either player. From this point of\nview, results about the weak cop game can often be upgraded to the strong cop\ngame. The existence of a homothety is one condition that enables such an upgrade.\nWe call this strategy meta-gaming.\nTheorem F (Meta-gaming theorem, for details see Theorem 4.5 and Corollary 4.6).\nIf there is a sequence of quasi-surjective quasi-homotheties from a graph ∆ to a\ngraph Γ, then the strong cop number of Γ is equal to the weak cop number of ∆.\nIn fact, Theorems C and D are obtained from the meta-gaming theorem. On the\nother hand, when the weak and the strong cop numbers of a group are not equal,\nthe meta-gaming theorem implies that homotheties cannot exist. By Theorems A\nand B, examples of such groups are hyperbolic groups that are not virtually free.\nThe following are consequences about such groups and spaces that do not involve\ncop numbers. While the statements may be well known to experts, they serve as a\nproof of concept that the cop numbers can have broader applications.\nCorollary G (Corollary 5.4). If Γ is the Cayley graph of a finitely generated hyperbolic group that is not virtually free, then there does not exist a sequence of\nquasi-surjective homotheties.\nCorollary H (Corollary 5.5). The hyperbolic plane H2 does not admit a surjective\nhomothety h : H2 Ñ H2 .\n\n\fCOPS AND ROBBERS FOR HYPERBOLIC AND VIRTUALLY FREE GROUPS\n\n1\n2\n3\n..\n.\n\n1\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
