Recent advances in deep learning offer the potential to automate this process, but the effectiveness\nof different morphological representations for phylogenetic\ntrait extraction remains poorly understood. In this study, we\ncompare the performance of deep learning models using three\ndistinct morphological representations — full segmentations,\nbinary masks, and Fourier descriptors of beetle outlines. We\ntest this on the Rove-Tree-11 dataset, a curated collection of\nimages from 215 rove beetle species. Our results demonstrate\nthat the mask-based model outperformed the others, achieving a normalized Align Score of 0.33 ± 0.02 on the test set,\ncompared to 0.45 ± 0.01 for the Fourier-based model and\n0.39 ± 0.07 for the segmentation-based model. The performance of the mask-based model likely reflects its ability to\ncapture shape features while taking advantage of the depth\nand capacity of the ResNet50 architecture. These results also\nindicate that dorsal textural features, at least in this group of\nbeetles, may be of lowered phylogenetic relevance, though\nfurther investigation is necessary to confirm this. In contrast,\nthe Fourier-based model suffered from reduced capacity and\noccasional inaccuracies in outline approximations, particularly in fine structures like legs. These findings highlight the\nimportance of selecting appropriate morphological representations for automated phylogenetic studies and the need for\nfurther research into explainability in automatic morphological trait extraction.\n\nTextural bias in deep learning models is a known issue\n(Hermann, Chen, and Kornblith 2020), and as yet research\nhas not explored how this presents itself in deep learning\nderived morphological traits.\nWith their diverse morphologies and extensive species\nrichness, rove beetles provide a challenging but rewarding\ntest case for automated phylogenetic trait extraction. However, a critical question remains: which representation of\na beetle’s morphology is most effective for deep learning\nmodels to capture phylogenetically informative traits?\nThis study focuses on comparing the efficacy of three distinct approaches for deep learning derived morphological\ntrait extraction: 1) masks of the beetle’s body, which provide\na binary segmentation of the beetle’s silhouette; 2) Descriptors of Fourier epicycles derived from the beetle’s outline,\nwhich capture its shape in a mathematically compact form\n(Lestrel 1997); and 3) the full segmentation of the beetle\nbody from a dorsal view including color and external structure. Each approach offers unique strengths in representing\nmorphology: masks and fourier descriptors emphasize overall shape in two distinct ways and the segmentations provide\ntextural, external structural and color information.\nBy evaluating these representations in terms of their ability to predict phylogenetic traits using deep metric learning,\nthis study seeks to investigate how useful shape compared to\ntextural information is to phylogenetic trait extraction.\n\nBackground\nIntroduction\nPhylogenetic analysis is a cornerstone of evolutionary biology, providing insights into the relationships between\nspecies and their evolutionary histories. Traditionally, the reconstruction of phylogenies relies on genetic data, morphological traits, or a combination of both (Huelsenbeck, Bull,\nand Cunningham 1996). However, extracting morphological traits for phylogenetic studies is often labor-intensive,\nrequiring expert knowledge and manual annotation. Recent\nadvances in deep learning offer a promising avenue to automate the extraction of morphological features from images, potentially revolutionizing the integration of morphology into phylogenetics (Hofmann et al. 2024).\nCopyright © 2025, Association for the Advancement of Artificial\nIntelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved.\n\nThe extraction of phylogenetic traits from morphological\ndata has been a central challenge in evolutionary biology.\nPrior to genetic analysis, it was the cornerstone of initial\ninvestigations of phylogenetic relationships. Since genetic\ndata is becoming more accessible, there is an argument to\nbe made for removing the focus on morphological traits\nentirely. However, the use of morphological traits remains\ncommon (Lee and Palci 2015), partly because genetic data\ncannot always be extracted from older specimens, such as\nfossils or pinned insect collections, which form a significant\nfoundation for our current understanding of the tree of life.\nTraditionally, morphological analyses rely on manual\nmeasurements and qualitative descriptions of traits, requiring significant expertise and time. While these methods have\nprovided valuable insights into evolutionary relationships,\nthey are often subjective and difficult to scale for large\n\n\fFigure 1: Example input images. (left) original segmentation from dataset. (middle) binary mask extracted from segmentation.\nWhite is used as the background here to keep padding equivalent during data augmentations. (right) Outline produced using\n200 Fourier descriptors.\ndatasets, such as those involving highly diverse taxa like\nrove beetles. With the rise of image-based analysis and computational methods, automated approaches to morphological\ntrait extraction using AI are increasingly being explored to\novercome these limitations (Hoyal Cuthill et al. 2019; Hunt\nand Pedersen 2022; Tsutsumi et al. 2023; Hofmann et al.\n2024).\nDeep learning has shown remarkable success in automating feature extraction and classification tasks across various domains, including image recognition, medical imaging,\nand biodiversity monitoring. In evolutionary biology, deep\nlearning models have recently been applied to tasks such as\nspecies identification and behavioural analysis. We refer to\n(Borowiec et al. 2022) for an overview of the current state\nof deep learning applied to ecological problems.\nDeep learning has also been applied to phylogenetics,\nwith the emphasis on genetics. Unlike prior work, we focus on measuring the agents' ability to perform tasks that span the typical daily work of knowledge workers utilizing enterprise software systems. To this end, we propose WorkArena, a remote-hosted benchmark of 29 tasks based on the widely-used ServiceNow platform. We also introduce BrowserGym, an environment for the design and evaluation of such agents, offering a rich set of actions as well as multimodal observations. Our empirical evaluation reveals that while current agents show promise on WorkArena, there remains a considerable gap towards achieving full task automation. Notably, our analysis uncovers a significant performance disparity between open and closed-source LLMs, highlighting a critical area for future exploration and development in the field.\nGet this paper in your agent:\nhf papers read 2403.07718\nDon't have the latest CLI?\ncurl -LsSf https://hf.co/cli/install.sh | bash\nModels citing this paper 0\nNo model linking this paper\nDatasets citing this paper 2\nSpaces citing this paper 0\nNo Space linking this paper","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

However, existing benchmarks for evaluating LLMs' tool usage face several limitations: (1) limited evaluation scenarios, often lacking assessments in real multi-turn dialogue contexts; (2) narrow evaluation dimensions, with insufficient detailed assessments of how LLMs use tools; and (3) reliance on LLMs or real API executions for evaluation, which introduces significant overhead. To address these challenges, we introduce ACEBench, a comprehensive benchmark for assessing tool usage in LLMs. ACEBench categorizes data into three primary types based on evaluation methodology: Normal, Special, and Agent. \"Normal\" evaluates tool usage in basic scenarios; \"Special\" evaluates tool usage in situations with ambiguous or incomplete instructions; \"Agent\" evaluates tool usage through multi-agent interactions to simulate real-world, multi-turn dialogues. We conducted extensive experiments using ACEBench, analyzing various LLMs in-depth and providing a more granular examination of error causes across different data types.\nGet this paper in your agent:\nhf papers read 2501.12851\nDon't have the latest CLI?\ncurl -LsSf https://hf.co/cli/install.sh | bash\nModels citing this paper 0\nNo model linking this paper\nDatasets citing this paper 2\nSpaces citing this paper 0\nNo Space linking this paper","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

We introduce ITBench, a framework that offers a systematic methodology for benchmarking AI agents to address real-world IT automation tasks. Our initial release targets three key areas: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), Compliance and Security Operations (CISO), and Financial Operations (FinOps). The design enables AI researchers to understand the challenges and opportunities of AI agents for IT automation with push-button workflows and interpretable metrics. ITBench includes an initial set of 94 real-world scenarios, which can be easily extended by community contributions. Our results show that agents powered by state-of-the-art models resolve only 13.8% of SRE scenarios, 25.2% of CISO scenarios, and 0% of FinOps scenarios. We expect ITBench to be a key enabler of AI-driven IT automation that is correct, safe, and fast.\nGet this paper in your agent:\nhf papers read 2502.05352\nDon't have the latest CLI?\ncurl -LsSf https://hf.co/cli/install.sh | bash\nModels citing this paper 0\nNo model linking this paper","message":"The returned content is the main text content extracted from the page.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:84","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"ACEBench: Who Wins the Match Point in Tool Usage?\nChen Chen1† , Xinlong Hao2† , Weiwen Liu3* , Xu Huang1 , Xingshan Zeng2 ,\nShuai Yu2 , Dexun Li2 , Shuai Wang2 , Weinan Gan2 , Yuefeng Huang1 ,\nWulong Liu2 , Xinzhi Wang2 , Defu Lian1 , Baoqun Yin1 , Yasheng Wang2* , Wu Liu1* ,\n1\n\nUniversity of Science and Technology of China, 2 Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab,\n3\nShanghai Jiao Tong University,\nchenchen0318@mail.ustc.edu.cn haoxinlong@huawei.com\n\narXiv:2501.12851v8 [cs.CL] 20 Nov 2025\n\nAbstract\n\net al., 2024), programming (Xu et al., 2024), and\nreasoning (Chen et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2022;\nSurís et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). On one hand,\nintegrating tools into LLMs can enhance capabilities in multiple domains, for example, ToolTransformer (Schick et al., 2023) enhances the ability of\nLLMs to solve complex problems by utilizing tools.\nOn the other hand, adopting a tool usage paradigm\ncan improve the robustness of the response and\nthe transparency of the generation, thus increasing\nthe explainability and trust of usersusers (Schick\net al., 2023), as well as improving the system’s\nadaptability. As this field continues to evolve, it is\nessential to comprehensively evaluate all aspects\nof tool usage, particularly in complex scenarios.\n\nLarge Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in decision-making\nand reasoning, particularly when integrated\nwith various tools to effectively solve complex problems. However, existing benchmarks\nfor evaluating LLMs’ tool usage face several\nlimitations: (1) limited evaluation scenarios,\noften lacking assessments in real multi-turn dialogue contexts; (2) narrow evaluation dimensions, with insufficient detailed assessments of\nhow LLMs use tools; and (3) reliance on LLMs\nor real API executions for evaluation, which\nintroduces significant overhead. To address\nthese challenges, we introduce ACEBench, a\ncomprehensive benchmark for assessing tool\nusage in LLMs. ACEBench categorizes data\ninto three primary types based on evaluation\nmethodology: Normal, Special, and Agent.\n\"Normal\" evaluates tool usage in basic scenarios; \"Special\" evaluates tool usage in situations with ambiguous or incomplete instructions; \"Agent\" evaluates tool usage through\nmulti-agent interactions to simulate real-world,\nmulti-turn dialogues. We conducted extensive\nexperiments using ACEBench, analyzing various LLMs in-depth and providing a more granular examination of error causes across different data types.\n\n1\n\nWhile several studies have focused on evaluating\ntool usage (Yan et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2024; Wang\net al., 2024a; Qin et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b;\nZhuang et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2024), there are still\nsome shortcomings in the existing tool-use benchmarks. Firstly, existing benchmarks lack multi-turn\ndialogue evaluation in real-world scenarios. For\nexample, the multi-turn dialogues in BFCL (Yan\net al., 2024) and HammerBench (Wang et al.,\n2024a) are composed of predefined fixed content\ncombinations. Secondly, current tool-use benchmarks (Qin et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2024; Huang\net al., 2023; Li et al., 2023) lack fine-grained evaluation and personalized data assessment. Additionally, existing benchmarks (Qin et al., 2023;\nGuo et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b) ignore the\nassessment of special cases, or the evaluation methods are simplistic (Yan et al., 2024), as user instructions in real life are not always perfect(Wang\net al., 2024c). The model’s ability to recognize and\nhandle these issues is also crucial for evaluation.\nLastly, evaluation costs are high (Qin et al., 2023;\nGuo et al., 2024), as many studies rely on advanced\nlarge models for evaluation.\n\nIntroduction\n\nLarge Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT4 (Achiam et al., 2023), have demonstrated exceptional performance across numerous natural language processing tasks (Naveed et al., 2023; Qu\net al., 2025; Mialon et al., 2023). Studies have\nshown that incorporating tools can significantly expand LLM capabilities, particularly in specialized\ndomains such as mathematics (Das et al., 2024; Bulusu et al., 2024; Gou et al., 2023; Veerendranath\n†\nEqual Contributions. Work was done during an internship\nat Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab. * Corresponding authors.\n*\nThe code are already publicly available at GitHub.\n\nTo address these shortcomings, we propose\n1\n\n\fTable 1: Comparison of benchmarks across different evaluation criteria. \"LLM-Free\" refers to result evaluation\nwithout relying on LLMs. \"Robustness\" refers to incomplete or unclear user instructions. \"Interactiveness\" refers\nto the dynamic interaction between the model and the environment. \"Atomic-Level\" refers to analyzing from the\natomic-level capabilities. \"Personalization” refers to the inclusion of personal likes.\nBenchmark\nMetaTool (Huang et al., 2023)\nAPI-Bank (Li et al., 2023)\nStable ToolBench (Guo et al., 2024)\nBFCL (Yan et al., 2024)\nτ -Bench (Yao et al., 2024)\nHammerBench (Wang et al., 2024a)\nACEBench (Ours)\n\nLLM-Free\n\nRobustness\n\nInteractiveness\n\nAtomic-Level\n\nPersonalization\n\n✓\n✓\n✗\n✓\n✓\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n✗\n✓\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✗\n✓\n\nACEBench, a comprehensive tool-use benchmark\nthat includes the following categories:\nNormal. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:85","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"arXiv:2502.05352v1 [cs.AI] 7 Feb 2025\n\nITBench: Evaluating AI Agents across\nDiverse Real-World IT Automation Tasks\nSaurabh Jha * 1 Rohan Arora * 1 Yuji Watanabe * 1 Takumi Yanagawa 1 Yinfang Chen 2 Jackson Clark 2\nBhavya Bhavya 1 Mudit Verma 1 Harshit Kumar 1 Hirokuni Kitahara 1 Noah Zheutlin 1 Saki Takano 1\nDivya Pathak 1 Felix George 1 Xinbo Wu 2 Bekir O Turkkan 1 Gerard Vanloo 1 Michael Nidd 1 Ting Dai 1\nOishik Chatterjee 1 Pranjal Gupta 1 Suranjana Samanta 1 Pooja Aggarwal 1 Rong Lee 1 Pavankumar Murali 1\nJae-wook Ahn 1 Debanjana Kar 1 Ameet Rahane 1 Carlos Fonseca 1 Amit Paradkar 1 Yu Deng 1\nPratibha Moogi 1 Prateeti Mohapatra 1 Naoki Abe 1 Chandrasekhar Narayanaswami 1 Tianyin Xu 2\nLav R. Varshney 2 Ruchi Mahindru 1 Anca Sailer 1 Laura Shwartz 1 Daby Sow 1 Nicholas C. M. Fuller 1\nRuchir Puri 1\n\nAbstract\n\nwith this growing complexity. Various IT personas ranging\nfrom Chief Information Officers to Site Reliability Engineers, Security and Compliance officers and IT engineers\nin general are struggling to ensure resiliency, reliability,\nsecurity and cost effective operations of IT Systems.\n\nRealizing the vision of using AI agents to automate critical IT tasks depends on the ability to\nmeasure and understand effectiveness of proposed\nsolutions. We introduce ITBench, a framework\nthat offers a systematic methodology for benchmarking AI agents to address real-world IT automation tasks. Our initial release targets three\nkey areas: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE),\nCompliance and Security Operations (CISO), and\nFinancial Operations (FinOps). The design enables AI researchers to understand the challenges\nand opportunities of AI agents for IT automation\nwith push-button workflows and interpretable metrics. ITBench includes an initial set of 94 realworld scenarios, which can be easily extended by\ncommunity contributions. Our results show that\nagents powered by state-of-the-art models resolve\nonly 13.8% of SRE scenarios, 25.2% of CISO\nscenarios, and 0% of FinOps scenarios. We expect ITBench to be a key enabler of AI-driven IT\nautomation that is correct, safe, and fast.\n\nThe recent CrowdStrike outage highlighted these challenges\nas it brought down our society’s most critical systems from\nhospital services to air travel and was estimated to cost US\nFortune 500 companies a staggering $5.4 billion (Kerner,\n2024). This incident underlined the critical need for intelligent IT incident resolution, compliance and risk management capabilities, a topic also addressed in the Digital\nOperational Resiliency Act (DORA) in Europe (Parliament\nand the Council of the European Union, 2024).\nThe rising popularity of AI agents and their projected ability\nto handle intricate tasks have increased the demand for AI\nagents managing IT systems (John, 2024; Miguel Carreon,\n2024; Pujar et al., 2023). Given the complexity of IT tasks,\na major hurdle for this research is establishing systematic\nmethods to assess the effectiveness of AI agents prior to\ntheir production deployment (Bogin et al., 2024; Kapoor\net al., 2024). Consequently, there is an urgency to develop\nmethods for evaluation of AI agents based on real IT tasks\nand their corresponding environments.\n\n1. Introduction\n\nThis paper addresses this critical need and presents ITBench,\na first of its kind framework that is both comprehensive and\nvisionary for benchmarking real-life IT automation tasks.\nThe goal of ITBench is to measure the performance of AI\nagents across a wide variety of complex and real-life IT\ntasks across personas including, Site Reliability Engineering\n(SRE) focusing on availability and resiliency, Compliance\nand Security Operations (CISO) ensuring compliance and\nsecurity of IT implementations, and Financial Operations\n(FinOps) enforcing cost efficiencies and optimizing return\non investment, among others (as shown in Figure 1).\n\nModern IT systems are driving many facets of our economy.\nThey have grown significantly in complexity with the adoption of cloud computing and agile development practices\n(Harvard Business Review Research Report, 2022; Fuller,\n2024). Effective management of these systems is becoming\nextremely challenging as corporations struggle to keep up\n*\n\n1\nEqual contribution\nIBM 2 University of Illinois at\nUrbana-Champaign.\nCorrespondence to:\nSaurabh Jha\n<Saurabh.Jha@ibm.com>.\n\n1\n\n\fITBench\nSRE\nEnsures app resilience\nand performance\nResolve “High error rate on service\norder-management.”\nBackup directory ‘foo’ to data lake.\n\nCISO\nManage threats and\nassess policies\nAssess compliance posture for “new\ncontrol rule detected for RHEL 9.”\nAssess and report critical risks across\nthe failing controls.\n\nFinOps\nManage IT spend\n\nResolve “IT spend exceeded the\nbudget.”\nReport return on investment per\napplication.\n\nFigure 1: Sample personas and IT tasks. Bell icon represents event-triggered tasks. Information icon represents other tasks\nsuch as data analysis, preventive maintenance tasks, or continuous optimization.\nITBench aims to advance innovation and establish new standards in the field. Our contributions can be summarized\nalong the following three axes:\n\n(refer to Section 4).\nWe believe that, similar to the highly influential SWEBench\n(Jimenez et al., 2024), our new ITBench framework—which\nencapsulates and measures the ability of AI agents to automate complex, real-world IT tasks—will spur a comparable\nacceleration in the performance of real-world IT AI agents.\n\n• Reflecting the real world: ITBench addresses the IT\nautomation requirements that are relevant and prevalent\nin production settings. SRE scenarios are based on\nreal-world incidents observed in our own SaaS products,\nCISO’s are on CIS benchmark (for Internet Security, CIS).\nFinOps scenarios are identified by the FinOps Foundation\n(Foundation, 2025a) through key business outcomes.\n• Being open and extensible with comprehensive IT coverage: We view ITBench as a central hub for benchmarking AI-driven solutions across diverse IT automation use\ncases. To support this, we provide IT benchmark suites\nand a framework for vertical (i.e., adding more scenarios)\nand horizontal expansion (i.e., adding more personas),\nensuring extensive coverage of IT tasks. ITBench is an\nopen-source framework built with open-source technologies, while allowing organizations with proprietary technologies to use it for developing and benchmarking their\nsolutions.\n• Enabling automated evaluation with partial scoring:\nITBench is designed to provide constructive feedback to\ndrive improvements in the design of agentic solutions\nfor IT problems. It includes a comprehensive evaluation\nframework and leaderboard that provide feedback to users\nat various stages of their agents’ reasoning process.\n\n2. Related Work\nITBench targets a comprehensive set of tasks for a wide\nrange of personas within IT automation. The initial release\nof ITBench focuses on evaluating scenarios within IT Operations (ITOps). Figure 1 illustrates currently targeted\npersonas and exemplar tasks that they are routinely facing.\nThere is clearly a rising trend and interest in developing\nbenchmarks to evaluate AI and ML techniques in ITOps\nwith specific focus on SRE, CISO and FinOps.\nTrainTicket (Zhou et al., 2018) provides 22 scenarios collected through an industrial survey of real-world incidents,\nusing hardcoded faults in the TrainTicket application to focus on fault localization. AIOpsLab (Chen et al., 2024a)\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:86","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Atomic-scale Nucleation and Growth Pathways of Complex Plate-like\nPrecipitates in Aluminum Alloys\nJunyuan Bai1, Gaowu Qin1,2,3*, Xueyong Pang1,3, Zhihao Zhao1,3\n1\n\nKey Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials (Ministry of Education), School of Materials Science and\n\nEngineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China\n2\n\nInstitute for Strategic Materials and Components, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang 110142,\n\nChina\n3\n\nResearch Center for Metal Wires, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China\n\nAbstract:\nAluminum alloys, the most widely utilized lightweight structural materials, predominantly depend on\ncoherent complex-structured nanoplates to enhance their mechanical properties. Despite several\ndecades of research, the atomic-scale nucleation and growth pathways for these complex-structured\nnanoplates remain elusive, as probing and simulating atomistic events like solid nucleation is\nprohibitively challenging. Here, using theoretical calculations and focus on three representative\ncomplex-structured nanoplates—T1, Ω, and η’/η2—in commercial Al alloys, we explicitly demonstrate\ntheir associated structural transitions follow an “interlayer-sliding+shuffling” mode. Specifically,\npartial dislocations complete the interlayer-sliding stage, while atomic shuffling occurs upon forming\nthe unstable basic structural transformation unit (BSTU) of the nanoplates. By identifying these\nBSTUs, we propose structural evolution pathways for these nanoplates within the Al matrix, which\nalign well with experimental observations and enable the evaluation of critical nuclei. These findings\nprovide long-sought mechanistic details into how coherent nanoplates nucleate and grow, facilitating\nthe rational design of higher-performance Al alloys and other structural materials.\n\n*Corresponding author.\nEmail: qingw@smm.neu.edu.cn (G.W. Qin)\n\n\fIntroduction\nAluminum, the most abundant metallic element on Earth, is inherently soft. Nevertheless, the\ndiscovery of duralumin in 1906 opened a new era for extensive applications of age-hardening Al alloys\nacross industries. By precipitating numerous dense and coherent nanoprecipitates within the matrix\nduring aging treatment, the mechanical properties of Al alloys can be significantly improved1–3.\nAmong various nanoprecipitates discovered, coherent {111}Al-oriented plate-like precipitates are\nparticularly important as they often form interleaved three-dimensional networks, effectively impeding\ndislocation motion and even enhancing the creep resistance of Al alloys4–6. For instance, well-known\n{111}Al-oriented T1, Ω, and η’/η2 nanoplates are typically recognized as key strengthening phases in\ntheir respective alloys, as illustrated in Table 1. Moreover, the recently discovered V-Sc(Al2Cu)4\nnanoplate7, which forms in situ within the Ω nanoplates, can even raise the serving temperature of Al\nalloys to an unprecedented ~400℃. Unlike the metastable {100}Al-oriented θ’-Al2Cu nanoplate, these\n{111}Al-oriented complex-structured nanoplates are generally stable phases, offering substantial\npromise for enhancing the strength and creep resistance of Al alloys. Despite extensive research\nspanning several decades, the atomic-scale nucleation and growth mechanisms of these nanoplates\nremain poorly understood, which is undoubtedly indispensable for further developing high-strength\nand heat-resistant Al alloys.\nAt the atomic scale, tracking nucleation and growth pathways in solids is always challenging.\nExperimental measurements often lack the necessary spatiotemporal resolution to capture atomistic\nevents, while theoretical simulations may struggle due to inaccurate interatomic potentials or an\ninability to simulate rare events like solid nucleation within accessible timescales8–10. According to the\ncrystal structures of parent and product phases, in-situ precipitation of coherent nanoprecipitates in\nalloys can be classified into three types: Type-I (simple→simpleidentical, such as face-centered\ncubic(fcc)→L12), Type-II (simple→simpledistinct, such as fcc→hexagonal close-packed (hcp)), and\nType-III (simple→complex, such as hcp→topologically close-packed (TCP)). As indicated in Table 1,\napart from γ’-AlAg2 precipitation (fcc→hcp), the formation of coherent nanoplates in Al alloys\ngenerally involves simple→complex transformations. Actually, early investigations into plate-like\nprecipitates began with the γ’ phase, leading to the proposal of a terrace-ledge growth model11,12, where\n(a/6)<112̅> (where a is the lattice constant) Shockley partial dislocations at the ledge’s edge were\nthought to accomplish the fcc→hcp transformation. Despite subsequent observations of partial\ndislocations in precipitates like T113–15, Ω16, and θ’17, their specific roles in simple→complex\ntransformations remain elusive. Consequently, there is a lack of comprehensive physical models\ndescribing the atomic-scale nucleation and growth of these nanoplates, particularly regarding the\n\n\fconditions triggering structural transformations and the essential kinetic processes governing plate\nthickening.\nFor simple→complex transitions in Al alloys, our recent study18 on hcp→TCP transitions in Mg\nalloys provides valuable insights. Utilizing density functional theory (DFT) calculations to sample\nsolute clusters, we identified an unstable 3-layer hcp-ordering, which spontaneously collapses into a\nTCP (icosahedron) structure upon structural relaxation, serving as the basic structural transformation\nunit (BSTU) that governs the entire hcp→TCP transformation. Apart from atomic diffusion, only\nshuffle-based displacements are involved during TCP formation. Although technological constraints\nimpede modeling the complete precipitation kinetic process19 using techniques such as kinetic monte\ncarlo or molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, the identification of BSTU offers a workaround for\ndeducing the structural evolution pathway of TCP plates within the hcp matrix, enabling us to\nunderstand how precipitates nucleate and grow. Comparatively, TCP precipitation within the fcc-Al\nmatrix is more intricate than in the hcp-Mg alloys.\nThis study employs DFT and MD approaches to explore the atomic-scale nucleation and growth\npathways of complex nanoprecipitates in Al alloys, with three representative {111}Al-oriented\nnanoplates (T1, Ω, and η’/η2) as model systems. We demonstrate that the simple→complex diffusive\ntransitions in Al alloys typically follow an “Interlayer-sliding+shuffling” mode, in which partial\ndislocations complete the interlayer-sliding stage, followed by atomic shuffling upon BSTU formation.\nThe structural evolution pathways for T1, Ω, and η’/η2 nanoplates were established by identifying their\nBSTUs, and these pathways align well with experimental observations. Furthermore, these pathways\nenable a quantitative assessment of the critical nucleus and provide explanations for various intricate\nprecipitation behaviors. This study offers long-sought mechanistic insights into the atomic-scale\nnucleation and growth of complex nanoplates in Al alloys or other fcc-based alloys, and establishes a\nsolid theoretical foundation for rationally designing alloy compositions and enhancing the thermal\nstability of nanoprecipitates.\nTable 1. Orientation relationships and structural transformation types of {111}Al-oriented nanoplates\nin Al alloys.\nPhases\n\nAlloy Systems\n\nHabit Plane\n\nγ’-AlAg2\n\nAl-Ag\n\n{111}Al\n\nT1- Al6Cu4Li320,21\n\nAl-Cu-Li\n\n{111}Al\n\nΩ-Al2Cu\n\nAl-Cu-Mg-Ag\n\n{111}Al\n\nOrientation Relationships\n(0001)γ' //(111)Al,\n[112̅ 0]γ' //[11̅ 0]Al12\n(0001)T //(111)Al,\n1\n[101̅0]T1 //[11̅ 0]Al15\n(001)Ω //(111)Al,\n[010]Ω//[101̅ ]Al16\n\nStructural\nTransformation Types\nfcc→hcp, Type-II\nfcc→TCP, Type-III\nfcc→C16, Type-III\n\n\fV-Sc(Al2Cu)47\n\nAl-Cu-Mg-Ag-Sc\n\n{111}Al\n\nη’-Mg6Zn13**\n/η2-MgZn2\n\nAl-Mg-Zn-Cu\n\n{111}Al\n\n** represents that is was proposed in the present study.\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:87","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"arXiv:2407.18401v1 [math.OC] 25 Jul 2024\n\nSTACKELBERG GAMES WITH THE THIRD PARTY\nYIMING JIANG, YAWEI WEI, AND JIE XUE\nAbstract. In this paper, we introduce the third party to achieve the Stackelberg equilibrium with the time inconsistency in three different Stackelberg\ngames, which are the discrete-time games, the dynamic games, and the mean\nfield games. Here all followers are experiencing learning-by-doing. The role\nof a third party is similar to industry associations, they supervise the leader’s\nimplementation and impose penalties for the defection with the discount factor. Then we obtain different forms of discount factors in different models and\neffective conditions to prevent defection. These results are consistent and the\nthird party intervention is effective and maneuverable in practice.\n\n1. INTRODUCTION\n1.1. Research history and motivations. The model of learning-by-doing has\noften been identified as one of the important sources of economic growth. During\nthe production process, knowledge as the by-product may improve the technique\nlevel of the producer, which is clearly different from that in the research institutions.\nThe earliest work about learning-by-doing model can be traced back to Arrow [1] in\n1962. Later Sheshinski [29] and Romer [28] introduced this idea in the neoclassical\ngrowth model with optimizing agents. Benchekroun et al. [4] discussed a duopoly\nmarket in one-shot Stackelberg games.\nStackelberg games are hierarchical ones where there are several leaders with a\ndominant position over the followers, which was first proposed by Stackelberg in\n[30]. There are many scholars studied the Stackelberg game, see Bensoussan et al.\n[2] and Moon [23]. For mean field Stackelberg games, the mean field behavior of\nthe followers will depend on an arbitrary strategy of the leader. Mean field games\n(briefly, MFG) theory is devoted to the analysis of differential games with infinitely\nmany players. This theory has been introduced by Lasry and Lions in 2006 [21]\n[22]. At about the same time, Huang et al. [20] solved the large population games\nindependently. The MFG problem with major and minor players was considered in\nMoon and Bassar [24], Nguyen and Huang [26]. The mass behavior of the minor\nplayers is strongly affected by the major players through the cost function and\nstochastic differential equations (briefly, SDE). Here SDE is driven by the white\nnoise, but not limited to white noises, see Bo and Yang [7].\nThe time inconsistency is an obviously important problem for Stackelberg games,\nwhich makes Stackelberg equilibrium inappropriate. Dating back to Strotz [31],\nthere are three approaches to handle the time inconsistency: precommitment, equilibrium and naive strategies. A precommitment strategy optimizes the objective\n2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 49N10; 49N80; 49N90.\nKey words and phrases. Mean field games; Stackelberg equilibrium; Time inconsistency;\nLearning-by-doing.\nAcknowledgements: This work is supported by the NSFC under the grands 12271269 and\nsupported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.\n1\n\n\f2\n\nYIMING JIANG, YAWEI WEI, AND JIE XUE\n\nfunctional anticipated at the very beginning time and the leader sticks with this\nstrategy over the whole game period, see Zhou and Li [33]. An equilibrium strategy consistently optimizes the objective functional anticipated at every time point\nin the similar manner of dynamic programming but using the concept of subgame\nperfect equilibrium, see Pun [34]. A naive strategy is such that, at any time, it\ncoincides with the optimal strategy with respect to the preference at that time. In\nfact, the theory asserts that, smart followers will not believe any promises made by\nthe leader, and therefore the leader cannot adopt the naive strategy in this way.\nHowever, there are some disadvantages to precommitment and equilibrium strategies. On the one hand, the precommitment strategy is sometimes inferior because\nits strong commitment leads to time inconsistency in efficiency, see Cui et al. [13],\nand its error-accumulation property brings huge estimation errors, see Chiu et al.\n[12]. On the other hand, since precommitment is not always a feasible solution to\nthe problem of intertemporal conflict, the leader with insight into his future unreliability may adopt an equilibrium strategy and reject any plan which he will not\nfollow through. The leader’s goal is then to find the best plan among those that\nhe will actually follow. Although the equilibrium strategy is time consistent, it\nemphasizes local optimality which is always not globally optimal.\nCompared with precommitment and equilibrium strategies, the Stackelberg equilibrium strategy sometimes enables to gain more benefits for all players. Due to\nthe time inconsistency of Stackelberg equilibrium, followers find that the risk of\ntrusting the leader’s commitment is significant, and the leader also finds it difficult\nto withstand the temptation in the future. Introducing a third party is a good\napproach for leaders and followers in the Stackelberg game, even if players have to\npay a certain fee. This idea is inspired by the trigger strategy for Nash games in\nDockner et al. [14]. In [14], to keep the Nash equilibrium, every player has to agree\nto follow a certain target path and sustain this agreement with an effective threat\nto regulate any defector.\nThis motivated us to introduce the third party in the Stackelberg games with\ntime inconsistency. Here the duty of the third party is to enable all players to keep\nthe Stackelberg equilibrium strategy. In this paper, the Stackelberg game with the\nthird party is set as follows. First, the followers need to pay part of the fee and the\nleader needs to pay a certain deposit to begin with the game. Second, the third\nparty will supervise the leader’s execution during the game, and impose mandatory\npenalties in the event of his defection. The appreciated penalties by the third party\ncan ensure Stackelberg equilibrium is credible. Thus it provides a theoretical basis\nfor the game rules. Third, the leader’s deposit is returned and the followers pay\nthe remaining fee at the end of the game. Note that the Stackelberg equilibrium\nwith the third party can be regarded as a modified naive strategy. This discussion\nis similar to an inverse game problem, except that the third party here is not all\nplayers. A similar role to the third party is an industry association, government\ndepartment, or banking institution.\n1.2. Statement of the problems and main results. In this subsection, we first\nset up three different Stackelberg games, which are the discrete-time games, the\ndynamic games and the MFG. In the Stackelberg games, the leader announces his\noptimum strategy in advance by taking into account the rational reactions of the\nfollowers, where the followers are experiencing learning-by-doing with fewer knowledge. Then followers choose their optimum strategies non-cooperatively based on\nthe leader’s strategy. Finally, the leader comes back and implements his announced\n\n\fSTACKELBERG GAMES WITH THE THIRD PARTY\n\n3\n\nstrategy. Hence we obtain the resulting optimum strategies for the leader and\nfollowers by the Pontryagin maximum principle, which forms the Stackelberg equilibrium. In fact, as a rational player, the leader will deviate from his commitment\nwithout the followers’ knowledge and we obtain the defection strategy. Hence the\nStackelberg equilibrium is time inconsistent. Next, we introduce the third party\nwith appreciate mandatory penalties for the leader’s payoff in the games. Finally,\nwe obtain the corresponding condition to effectively prevent defection, which makes\nthe Stackelberg equilibrium be credible.\nModel 1: Discrete-time Stackelberg games model. This model is a repeated oligopoly Stackelberg game, in which the same game is played in each period.\nThe repeated game here consists of the following static game as the original game,\nwhich comes from [4]. We consider a duopoly market consisting of firm-0 and firm-1\ncompeting as Stackelberg rivals, which produce a homogeneous good. Two firms repeat the duopoly games N times and the period is [0, T ]. They both move one shot\nat the beginning of each period. Denote ui (t) as the output of the firm-i, i ∈ {0, 1}\nat time t, xi (t) as his stock of knowledge, and ci as his unit cost. Assume that the\nstock xi ∈ [0, 1], and the inverse demand function K satisfies K = a − b(u1 + u0 ),\nwhere a and b are positive constants. As the leader, firm-0 has complete knowledge\nand dominates the other. Hence we assume that\n(1.1)\n\nẋ0 (t) = 0,\n\nwith initial stocks x0 (0), and c0 ≤ c1 . It implies that his stock of knowledge is\ninvariant, and his unit cost is the lowest than others. For the followers, firm-1 is\nexperiencing learning-by-doing and his knowledge subjects to\n(1.2)\n\nẋ1 (t) = u1 (t) − δx1 (t),\n\nwith the initial condition x1 (0) = 0, where δ > 0 is the rate of depreciation of\nknowledge. Denote the classes of admissible controls for the firm-i by Ui , i ∈ {0, 1}.\nNow we consider a pair of optimal control problems on [0, T ] as follows.\n\b\nLeader’s problem: max J0 (u0 ) = max u0 (t)(a − b(u1 (t) + u0 (t)) − c0 ) ,\nu0 ∈U0\nu0 ∈U0\n\b\n(P1)\nFollower’s problem: max J1 (u1 ) = max u1 (t)(a − b(u1 (t) + u0 (t)) − c1 ) .\nu1 ∈U1\n\nu1 ∈U1\n\nDenote\n• the Stackelberg equilibrium by (u∗0 , u∗1 ), the defection strategy by (û0 , u∗1 ).\n• the payoff of firm-0 with the strategy u∗0 in the nth game by J0∗ (n).\n• the payoff of firm-0 with the defection strategy û0 in the nth game by Jˆ0 (n).\n• the extra payoff of û0 than u∗0 in the nth game by ∆J0 (n) := Jˆ0 (n) − J0∗ (n).\n• the actual payoff of firm-0 with the punishment by the third party in the\nnth game by J˜0 (n).\nIn the next section, we find that the leader can get extra gain with less production\nafter defection. It means that the time inconsistency of the Stackelberg equilibrium\nis originated from the structure of the above Stackelberg games itself. Hence we\nintroduce the third party in discrete-time Stackelberg games with the following\npunishment rules. If the leader defects with û0 from the mth game, then the third\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:94","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Rethinking Video-Text Understanding:\nRetrieval from Counterfactually Augmented Data\nWufei Ma1,2⋆ , Kai Li1 , Zhongshi Jiang1 , Moustafa Meshry1 , Qihao Liu2\nHuiyu Wang3 , Christian Häne1 , and Alan Yuille2\nMeta Reality Labs\nJohns Hopkins University\n3\nMeta AI\n\narXiv:2407.13094v1 [cs.CV] 18 Jul 2024\n\n1\n\n2\n\nAbstract. Recent video-text foundation models have demonstrated strong\nperformance on a wide variety of downstream video understanding tasks.\nCan these video-text models genuinely understand the contents of natural videos? Standard video-text evaluations could be misleading as many\nquestions can be inferred merely from the objects and contexts in a single frame or biases inherent in the datasets. In this paper, we aim to\nbetter assess the capabilities of current video-text models and understand their limitations. We propose a novel evaluation task for videotext understanding, namely retrieval from counterfactually augmented\ndata (RCAD), and a new Feint6K dataset. To succeed on our new evaluation task, models must derive a comprehensive understanding of the\nvideo from cross-frame reasoning. Analyses show that previous videotext foundation models can be easily fooled by counterfactually augmented data and are far behind human-level performance. In order to\nnarrow the gap between video-text models and human performance on\nRCAD, we identify a key limitation of current contrastive approaches on\nvideo-text data and introduce LLM-teacher, a more effective approach\nto learn action semantics by leveraging knowledge obtained from a pretrained large language model. Experiments and analyses show that our\napproach successfully learn more discriminative action embeddings and\nimproves results on Feint6K when applied to multiple video-text models.\nOur Feint6K dataset and project page is available here.\nKeywords: Video-Text Understanding · Retrieval\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nVideo-text foundation models have gained increasing attention due to their simple formulation and strong transferability [13, 17, 24]. By pretraining on web\nscale video-text datasets, these models demonstrate strong performance across\na wide range of downstream tasks, such as video-text retrieval [23, 29] and video\nquestion answering [26].\n⋆\n\nAll data collection and experiments were conducted at JHU.\n\n\f2\n\nW. Ma et al.\n\nAs video-text foundation models evolve and achieve increasingly better performance on various benchmarks, we raise the following questions: Can these\nvideo-text model truly grasp the semantics of natural videos? Are these models genuinely reaching a level of understanding comparable to humans? Despite\nthe remarkable achievements made in previous works, our study suggests that\ncurrent video-text models still fall far behind human-level understanding.\nWe argue that existing prominent results on standard video-text tasks can\nbe misleading as models largely exploit the shortcuts and biases inherent in the\ndataset. Many of the questions can be answered by objects or context extracted\nfrom a single frame without capturing cross-frame relations. For the examples in\nFig. 2a, the video-text alignment can be easily inferred from shortcuts such as\n“cymbals” or “football”. Moreover, the models may utilize biases in the datasets,\nsuch as the spurious correlation between “outdoor” and “football”. Current evaluations of video-text understanding are compromised by shortcuts and biases,\nwhich obscure us from analyzing the limitations of current models. As we proceed\nfrom image-text understanding to video-text understanding, we should focus on\nmore challenging semantics in the video domain that require cross-frame reasoning to solve, such as the interaction between person and object or the change of\nappearances over a sequence of frames (see Fig. 1a).\nIn this paper we propose a novel evaluation paradigm of video-text understanding, i.e., retrieval from counterfactually augmented data (RCAD). As\ndemonstrated in Fig. 2b, the goal is to retrieve the only positive caption with\nmatched semantics among “hard” negative captions. Negative captions are counterfactually augmented so that video-text models must derive a holistic understanding of the video sequence for both objects and actions, in order to retrieve\nthe correct caption. As a comparison, negative examples from standard videoto-text retrieval [23, 25, 29] are captions of different videos in the same dataset,\noften containing different object entities that are easy to distinguish.\nWe follow the previous human-in-the-loop system [10] and develop a benchmark dataset for our new RCAD task, i.e., the Feint6K dataset. We test a\nwide range of public video-text methods with various pretraining strategies on\nthis new benchmark. Two failure examples are visualized in Fig. 1a and quantitative results are summarized in Fig. 1b. Note how state-of-the-art video-text\nmodel, InternVideo [24], features a 87.9% rank-1 accuracy on standard video-totext retrieval but drops by 32.8% (pretraining only) and 29.7% (finetuned) when\ntested on our benchmark with counterfactually augmented data. By establishing\na human-level baseline on our benchmark, the InternVideo model surprisingly\nfalls far behind human performance by 38.6%. Our findings sharply contrast\nto the prominent performance obtained on previous video-text benchmarks and\nquestion the common belief that latest video-text models can develop a fairly\neffective representation for texts and videos in existing datasets.\nIn light of our evaluation results on Feint6K, we identify a significant limitation of the widely adopted contrastive representation learning on video-text\ndata, which is the issue of shortcut learning (see Sec. 4.1). To address this,\nwe propose LLM-teacher that enables a more effective learning of action se-\n\n\fRethinking Video-Text Understanding: RCAD and Feint6K\n\n(a) Failures of InternVideo [24] on Feint6K\n\n3\n\n(b) Quantitative results on Feint6K dataset\n\nFig. 1: (a): Although with large-scale pretraining on web-scale data, current videotext model like [24] can be easily fooled by counterfactually augmented data. (b):\nThe performance of InternVideo on retrieval from counterfactually augmented data\n(RCAD) drops by over 30% when compared to the standard video-to-text retrieval\nand by 38.6% when compared to human-level performance. We also evaluate models\non standard video-text retrieval from only 6 sampled candidates and show that our\nRCAD task is indeed much more challenging. Our LLM-teacher successfully improves\nthe performance on RCAD by enforcing a more effective learning of action semantics.\n\nmantics by introducing extra knowledge from pretrained large language models\n(LLMs). Specifically we generate synthetic captions by modifying the contents\nof an existing caption and LLM serves as the teacher to determine if a synthetic\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:95","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Submitted to Applied Energy\n\nA Two-Stage AI-Powered Motif Mining Method for Efficient\nPower System Topological Analysis\nYiyan Li a,c, Zhenghao Zhou a,c, Jian Ping a,c, Xiaoyuan Xu b,c, Zheng Yan b,c*, Jianzhong Wu d\na\n\nb\n\nCollege of Smart Energy, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, China\nThe Key Laboratory of Control of Power Transmission and Conversion, Ministry of Education, Shanghai\nJiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China\nc\nShanghai Non-Carbon Energy Conversion and Utilization Institute, Shanghai Jiao Tong University,\nShanghai 200240, China\nd\nSchool of Engineering, Cardiff University, Cardiff, UK\n\nAbstract: Graph motif, defined as the microstructure that appears repeatedly in a large graph, reveals important\ntopological characteristics of the large graph and has gained increasing attention in power system analysis\nregarding reliability, vulnerability and resiliency. However, searching motifs within the large-scale power\nsystem is extremely computationally challenging and even infeasible, which undermines the value of motif\nanalysis in practice. In this paper, we introduce a two-stage AI-powered motif mining method to enable efficient\nand wide-range motif analysis in power systems. In the first stage, a representation learning method with\nspecially designed network structure and loss function is proposed to achieve ordered embedding for the power\nsystem topology, simplifying the subgraph isomorphic problem into a vector comparison problem. In the second\nstage, under the guidance of the ordered embedding space, a greedy-search-based motif growing algorithm is\nintroduced to quickly obtain the motifs without traversal searching. A case study based on a power system\ndatabase including 61 circuit models demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method.\nKeywords: Motif mining, power system topology analysis, representation learning, ordered embedding, greedy\nsearch algorithm.\n\n1. Introduction\nAfter decades of development, the power grid has evolved into a hyper-complex physical system. The\nlarge-scale integration of renewable energy resources and flexible loads accelerates the electronization of the\npower system, leading to low system inertia and high operation uncertainty. Meanwhile, advanced operation\nmodes such as peer-to-peer energy trading, demand response and virtual power plants put further challenges to\nthe system operation. Therefore, it is important to continually deepen the understanding of the fast-developing\npower grid topology for both planning and operation purposes.\nBecause the power grid naturally has a graph structure with nodes and edges, complex network theory has\nbeen widely used to analyze the power system from the topological perspective. In [1], the North American\npower grid is analyzed from a topological perspective to determine the power transfer capability and identify\nkey transmission substations that make the grid vulnerable when being disrupted. Similar study is conducted by\n[2], using complex network theory to analyze the static robustness of the European power grid when failures or\nattacks happen. Ref [3] implements graph neural network to achieve fast reliability evaluation for the park-level\nelectricity-hydrogen system by analyzing the topological structures. A feature-selection algorithm is introduced\n1\n\n\fSubmitted to Applied Energy\n\nto assess the importance of the system components to the evaluation results. Focusing on bulk power systems,\n[4] proposes an efficient topological procedure to calculate the reliability indices and determine the contribution\nof each system state to those indices. Ref [5] focuses instead on the medium and low voltage grids to analyze\nthe adequacy of the current distribution infrastructure for a decentralized energy market, based on statistical\ntools from the complex network theory. Ref [6] examines the effect of network topology on the time-invariant\ndynamics of power systems. Stability metrics based on H2-norm are defined to assist the planning for new or\nexisting power systems. Note that purely relying on topological analysis may ignore the unique characteristics\nof power systems. To address this issue, [7] introduces an extended topological method to rank the most critical\nlines and buses by incorporating several physical features of power systems. Ref [8] considers electrical features\nof power systems such as electrical distance, power transfer distribution and line flow limits. An extended\nbetweenness centrality is then defined to identify critical components in power grids.\nAs one of the major characteristics of graphs, topology motif has gained increasing attention in recent years\nin analyzing power system stability, vulnerability and resiliency. As shown in Fig. 1, motif is defined as the\nmicrostructure of a complex network that appears more frequently than other microstructures, which is\nconsidered as the basic building block of a complex network and is related with many global topological\ncharacteristics [9]. Ref [10] proposes a motif-based power grid robustness analysis method, focusing on the\ninfluence of local network structures to the system robustness under attacks. Ref [11] demonstrates that the grid\nfragility tends to increase when the grid becomes more interconnected and complex motifs starts to appear,\nbased on the assessment of European grid reliability. Based on the topological analysis of power grids, Ref [12]\nconcludes that certain motif structures such as “dead trees” will degrade the overall dynamic stability of the\nsystem. On the contrary, the appearance of certain motifs in the power grid such as three-cycles is helpful to the\ndynamical stability of the power grid [13]. Ref [14] studies the reliability of a cyber-physical system based on\nmotif analysis, considering the influence of both the cyber layer and the physical layer. Ref [15] implements\nmotif theory to analyze the multiple line outage patterns in the transmission systems. Results show that multiple\nline outages usually have certain spatial patterns that occur much more frequently than random selection, which\nare defined as contingency motifs. The N-k contingency lists constructed from motifs can improve risk\nestimation in cascading outage simulations and help to confirm utility contingency selection.\n\nFig. 1 Illustration of the graph motif concept. Frequency is calculated by traversal searching.\n\nHowever, as the value of motif is being recognized in power system analysis, how to efficiently find motifs\nin large-scale power systems is extremely computationally challenging because of the following three reasons\n[16]. First, according to the motif definition, the key to find motifs is to count the appearances of the candidate\nmicrostructures in the target large graph, which is a typical NP-hard problem [17]. Second, there exists the graph\nisomorphic issue when matching the motif candidates to the large graph, which brings additional complexity to\nthe searching process. Third, as the number of nodes increases, the possible structures of the candidate motifs\n2\n\n\fSubmitted to Applied Energy\n\nincrease exponentially. All the above reasons triple the complexity of the motif mining process, making the\ntraditional traversal searching methods infeasible especially in large graphs such as power systems. As a result,\nthe value of motif in power system analysis is still far from being fully exploited. For instance, ref\n[10][11][13][14] all focus on the simplest 4-node motifs to conduct analysis, which is considered limited without\nexploring more sophisticated structures to better reflect power system topological characteristics. To address\nthis issue, artificial-intelligence-based (AI-based) approaches have been proposed in recent years in the\ncomputer science domain to accelerate the motif mining process [18]-[20], which brings new opportunity for\nthe motif analysis in power system domain.\nIn this paper, we introduce a two-stage AI-powered motif mining method to enable efficient and widerange motif analysis for large-scale power systems, inspired by the works of Rex Ying et al [21][22]. In the first\nstage, a representation learning method with specially designed network structure and loss function is proposed\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Experiments demonstrate its superior performance: UI-TARS achieves SOTA performance in 10+ GUI agent benchmarks evaluating\nperception, grounding, and GUI task execution (see below). Notably, in the OSWorld benchmark, UI-TARS achieves scores of 24.6 with 50 steps and 22.7 with 15 steps, outperforming\nClaude’s 22.0 and 14.9 respectively. In AndroidWorld, UI-TARS achieves 46.6, surpassing\nGPT-4o’s 34.5. UI-TARS incorporates several key innovations: (1) Enhanced Perception:\nleveraging a large-scale dataset of GUI screenshots for context-aware understanding of\nUI elements and precise captioning; (2) Unified Action Modeling, which standardizes\nactions into a unified space across platforms and achieves precise grounding and interaction\nthrough large-scale action traces; (3) System-2 Reasoning, which incorporates deliberate\nreasoning into multi-step decision making, involving multiple reasoning patterns such as task\ndecomposition, reflection thinking, milestone recognition, etc. (4) Iterative Training with\nReflective Online Traces, which addresses the data bottleneck by automatically collecting,\nfiltering, and reflectively refining new interaction traces on hundreds of virtual machines.\nThrough iterative training and reflection tuning, UI-TARS continuously learns from its\nmistakes and adapts to unforeseen situations with minimal human intervention. We also\nanalyze the evolution path of GUI agents to guide the further development of this domain.\nUI-TARS is open sourced at https://github.com/bytedance/UI-TARS.\n\n∗\n\nIndicates equal contribution.\n\n†\n\nCorresponding author.\n\n♢\n\nThe work is done when interning at ByteDance.\n\n\fCONTENTS\n\nContents\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\n3\n\n2\n\nEvolution Path of GUI Agents\n\n6\n\n2.1\n\nRule-based Agents . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .\n\n6\n\n2.2\n\nFrom Modular Agent Framework to Native Agent Model . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .\n\n7\n\n2.3\n\nActive and Lifelong Agent (Prospect) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .\n\n9\n\n3\n\n4\n\n5\n\n6\n\nCore Capabilities of Native Agent Model\n\n9\n\n3.1\n\nCore Capabilities . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .\n\n10\n\n3.2\n\nCapability Evaluation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

We envision experimenters switching\noff the couplings between two parts of the system and preparing them in independent equilibrium\nstates. One side of the chain is prepared in a disordered phase, and the other in a symmetrybreaking ground state. When the couplings are switched back on, time evolution ensues. We\nargue that in integrable systems the front separating the ordered region recedes at the maximal\nvelocity of quasiparticle excitations over the ground state. We infer that, generically, the order\nparameters should vary on a subdiffusive scale of order t1/3 , where t is time, and their fluctuations\nshould exhibit the same scaling. This interfacial region exhibits full range correlations, indicating\nthat it cannot be decomposed into nearly uncorrelated subsystems. Using the transverse-field Ising\nchain as a case study, we demonstrate that all order parameters follow the same universal scaling\nfunctions. Through an analysis of the skew information, we uncover that the breakdown of cluster\ndecomposition has a quantum contribution: each subsystem within the interfacial region, with extent\ncomparable to the region, exists in a macroscopic quantum state.\n\nLocal relaxation is a key concept in the study of isolated quantum many-body systems prepared out of equilibrium [1, 2]. One of the key aspects of their late-time description is indeed that, eventually, small parts of the system behave as if they were in thermal equilibrium, which\nis often explained invoking the eigenstate thermalization\nhypothesis [3–6]. Exception to this local form of thermalization are well known in 1D, where integrable and\nquasi-integrable systems stand out for their peculiarities,\nobserved even in experiments [7–10]. In conventional situations also integrable systems exhibit local relaxation,\nbut the expectation values of local observables are not\ncaptured by Gibbs ensembles whereas by so-called generalised Gibbs ensembles (GGE) [11–14], which account\nfor the presence of infinitely many integrals of motion.\nIn integrable systems with inhomogeneities, local relaxation was shown in a multitude of works, culminating in\nthe theory of generalized hydrodynamics (GHD) [15–22],\nwhose deviations from standard hydrodynamic predictions have been detected also in experiments [23–25].\nNote that, since (generalised) Gibbs ensembles are\ncharacterised by a nonzero thermodynamic entropy and,\nin 1D, entropy disrupts order very effectively, every global\nsymmetry common to all relevant integrals of motions is\nexpected to be locally restored [14, 26]. This is supposed\nto happen everywhere, even beyond the nonequilibrium\nsteady state (NESS) emerging in the genuine infinite time\nlimit; for instance, in bipartitioning protocols it occurs in\nthe Euler scaling limit in which the position of the subsystem is regarded as a time-dependent variable.\nWe remark, however, that referring to the emergent\nstationary states capturing the late-time local properties\nas to states of (generalised) equilibrium might be misleading: stationarity is not sufficient to characterise an\nequilibrium state [27], which is also supposed to be sta-\n\nble under small perturbations, as well as to exhibit other\nsubtle properties that manifest themselves, e.g., in the\nweak clustering of correlations (see also Ref. [28] in the\npresence of time reversal symmetry). In this regard, we\nmention Ref. [29], which pointed out a form of instability of the NESS emerging after joining two thermal\nreservoirs to a finite subsystem.\nIn this work, we examine the cluster decomposition\nproperties of the stationary states emerging in late-time\ndescriptions. In an equilibrium state of a local system,\nexperiments conducted at large, space-like separations\ndo not influence each other. In the nonequilibrium time\nevolution of an inhomogeneous, isolated system—where\nrelaxation is a local phenomenon and subsystems are\nthe focus—it would then be reasonable to expect such\na separation not to exceed the maximum size of the subsystem that can be described by a stationary state. Is\nthat always the case? Remarkably, we show that even\nsuch a weak requirement is not always satisfied. Fluctuations and, more generally, the full counting statistics of\noperators in subsystems of nonequilibrium systems has\nattracted a lot of attention [30–37]. Several unusual behaviours have been uncovered, including algebraically decaying correlations [38–43] and anomalous fluctuations\n[44–47]. In those cases it is still not excluded that local stationary states could be understood as equilibrium\nstates of effective long-range models (see also Ref. [48]).\nInstead, we are investigating whether connected correlations might not decay at all in the effective stationary\nstates. One scenario that could lead to this is when time\nevolution fails to smooth out the discontinuity induced by\na bipartite preparation of the system, because there are\nno clustering stationary states that can bridge the properties of the two sides. In such cases, we could expect\nan unusually rapid crossover between areas with signif-\n\n\f2\n\n⟨O⟩\n\n⟨O⟩gs\n\nt=0\n\ndisorder\n\norder\n\nvmax t\n0\n\n∼ t1/3\nx\n\nFIG. 1. In this review, we present a comprehensive analysis of tensor decomposition methods such as Tucker, CANDECOMP/PARAFAC, spiked tensor decomposition, etc. and their diverse\napplications across biomedical domains such as imaging, multi-omics, and spatial transcriptomics. To\nsystematically investigate the literature, we applied a topic modeling-based approach that identifies and\ngroups distinct thematic sub-areas in biomedicine where tensor decomposition has been used, thereby\nrevealing key trends and research directions. We evaluated challenges related to the scalability of latent\nspaces along with obtaining the optimal rank of the tensor, which often hinder the extraction of meaningful features from increasingly large and complex datasets. Additionally, we discuss recent advances\nin quantum algorithms for tensor decomposition, exploring how quantum computing can be leveraged\nto address these challenges. Our study includes a preliminary resource estimation analysis for quantum\ncomputing platforms and examines the feasibility of implementing quantum-enhanced tensor decomposition methods on near-term quantum devices. Collectively, this review not only synthesizes current\napplications and challenges of tensor decomposition in biomedical analyses but also outlines promising quantum computing strategies to enhance its impact on deriving actionable insights from complex\nbiomedical data.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nBiomedical data encompasses a diverse set of multi-modal and multi-omics data from healthcare and life\nsciences providing critical insights into the complexity of disease etiology and biological functions. A compre∗ Correspondence: a.bose@ibm.com\n\n1\n\n\fhensive understanding of biomedical data is required to advance precision medicine and treatment strategies\nfor complex diseases. Innovations in technologies like genomics, proteomics, medical imaging, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), etc. have resulted in high-quality data that\nenable personalized clinical decision making. This opportunity also presents us with significant challenges\nin data management and analysis [1], such as representing the different modalities of biomedical data and\nfinding higher-order relationships between them. With the increasing size and complexity of biomedical data,\ntensors are emerging as a powerful method for the integration and analysis of data [2–9].\nTensors are multidimensional arrays rendered as generalizations of vector spaces [10]. A vector is a\nfirst-order tensor, a matrix is a second-order tensor, and tensors of order three or higher are called higherorder tensors. Tensor decompositions, although originally proposed about a century ago [11], have found\nrenewed interest in several applications in signal processing, machine learning [12], data mining [13], computer vision [14], neuroscience [15], etc. in the past two decades, with the ability to represent complex and\nmultimodal data. Tensors have the ability to represent each biomedical modality in a different dimension.\nHence, they can be used to integrate multi-modal biomedical data spanning multi-omics variables. Tensor factorization or decomposition allows us to obtain a latent structure from a higher-dimensional space\nspanned by all the multi-modal variables and identify interactions between them [3, 10]. Tensor decomposition (TD) has widespread application in biomedical data analysis ranging from analyzing MRI images and\nelectroencephalography (EEG) signals [16], finding genotype-phenotype relationships [17], quantifying expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) [18], integrating multi-omics data [5], to spatial transcriptomics [19].\nThere have been attempts to review the applications of TD in biomedical data but they were focused on\nspecific applications such as imaging analysis [16], precision medicine [3] or, on specific diseases such as\ncancer [20], and often limited in scope.\nThis review examines recent advances in tensor decomposition for biomedical data analysis. We review its\napplication across diverse biomedical domains over the past decade, highlighting state-of-the-art practices,\nthe inherent advantages of these methods, and the scalability challenges that remain. Moreover, we discuss\na unique perspective of adapting emerging technologies such as quantum computing (QC) and strategies for\ndeveloping quantum algorithms for TD to ameliorate the challenges arising in classical TD applications. QC\nhas the potential to accelerate computing in biomedicine [21–24], with possible applications in single-cell\nanalysis [25, 26], predictive modeling in multi-omics analysis [27], clinical trials [28], breast cancer subtyping\nusing histopathological images [29], protein structure prediction [30, 31], among others. We explore development of a quantum tensor decomposition (QTD) algorithm and provide a framework of how it can be\nimplemented in present-day pre-fault tolerant quantum devices (PFTQD) to solve problems in biomedicine,\nas well as discussing future implementations in fault-tolerant quantum devices.\nThis review is structured as follows. We introduce different types of TD approaches in Section 2 and\ndiscuss methods to evaluate computational hardness in Section 2.2. Next, we continue to review the current\nliterature for TD applications in biomedicine using an unsupervised technique in Section 3 and subsequently\ndiscuss the main findings on imaging (Section 3.1), multi-omics (Section 3.2), and spatial transcriptomics\n(Section 3.3). We also analyze the hardness of tensor decomposition in real-world MRI and multi-omics\ndata, highlighting the factors impacting its implementation in classical systems in Section 3.4. Thereafter,\nwe review the literature on quantum algorithms for TD (Section 4) and discuss particular use cases where\nquantum tensor decomposition can be applied in biomedical data (Section 5). Finally, we summarize our\nreview and future perspective in Section 6.\n\n2\n\nA Primer on Tensor Decomposition\n\nMathematically, tensors are defined as multilinear functions on the Cartesian product of vector spaces [32];\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

We propose tau-bench, a benchmark emulating dynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a language agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines. We employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database state at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose a new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple trials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like gpt-4o) succeed on <50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 <25% in retail). At the same time, thanks to improvements in large language models (LLMs), there has also been a rapid development in AI agents that interact with and affect change in their surrounding environments. But how performant are AI agents at helping to accelerate or even autonomously perform work-related tasks? The answer to this question has important implications for both industry looking to adopt AI into their workflows, and for economic policy to understand the effects that adoption of AI may have on the labor market. To measure the progress of these LLM agents' performance on performing real-world professional tasks, in this paper, we introduce TheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact with the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker: by browsing the Web, writing code, running programs, and communicating with other coworkers. We build a self-contained environment with internal web sites and data that mimics a small software company environment, and create a variety of tasks that may be performed by workers in such a company. We test baseline agents powered by both closed API-based and open-weights language models (LMs), and find that with the most competitive agent, 24% of the tasks can be completed autonomously. This paints a nuanced picture on task automation with LM agents -- in a setting simulating a real workplace, a good portion of simpler tasks could be solved autonomously, but more difficult long-horizon tasks are still beyond the reach of current systems.\nCommunity\nHow performant are AI agents at helping to accelerate or even autonomously perform work-related tasks?\nWe introduce TheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact with the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"TheAgentCompany: Benchmarking LLM Agents on\nConsequential Real World Tasks\n\narXiv:2412.14161v3 [cs.CL] 10 Sep 2025\n\nFrank F. Xu1∗ Yufan Song2∗ Boxuan Li2∗ Yuxuan Tang2 Kritanjali Jain1\nMengxue Bao2 Zora Z. Wang1 Xuhui Zhou1 Zhitong Guo1 Murong Cao2\nMingyang Yang2 Hao Yang Lu2 Amaad Martin1 Zhe Su1 Leander Melroy Maben1\nRaj Mehta1 Wayne Chi1 Lawrence Jang1 Yiqing Xie1 Shuyan Zhou3 Graham Neubig1\n1\nCarnegie Mellon University 2 Independent 3 Duke University\n{fangzhex, gneubig}@cs.cmu.edu, {yufans, boxuanli}@alumni.cmu.edu\n\nAbstract\nWe interact with computers on an everyday basis, be it in everyday life or work,\nand many aspects of work can be done entirely with access to a computer and\nthe Internet. At the same time, thanks to improvements in large language models\n(LLMs), there has also been a rapid development in AI agents that interact with and\naffect change in their surrounding environments. But how performant are AI agents\nat accelerating or even autonomously performing work-related tasks? The answer\nto this question has important implications both for industry looking to adopt AI\ninto their workflows and for economic policy to understand the effects that adoption\nof AI may have on the labor market. To measure the progress of these LLM agents’\nperformance on performing real-world professional tasks, in this paper we introduce\nTheAgentCompany, an extensible benchmark for evaluating AI agents that interact\nwith the world in similar ways to those of a digital worker: by browsing the Web,\nwriting code, running programs, and communicating with other coworkers. We\nbuild a self-contained environment with internal web sites and data that mimics\na small software company environment, and create a variety of tasks that may be\nperformed by workers in such a company. We test baseline agents powered by both\nclosed API-based and open-weights language models (LMs), and find that the most\ncompetitive agent can complete 30% of tasks autonomously. This paints a nuanced\npicture on task automation with LM agents–in a setting simulating a real workplace,\na good portion of simpler tasks could be solved autonomously, but more difficult\nlong-horizon tasks are still beyond the reach of current systems. We release code,\ndata, environment, and experiments on https://the-agent-company.com.\nWebsite\nCode\nEvaluations\n\n1\n\nhttps://the-agent-company.com\nhttps://github.com/TheAgentCompany/TheAgentCompany\nhttps://github.com/TheAgentCompany/experiments\n\nIntroduction\n\nWe are in the midst of a technological transformation. With the rapid month-by-month progress\nbrought about by large language models (LLMs), we are seeing AI-based assistance or automation\nbecome commonplace in tasks that were unthinkable only years ago. In fact, the pace of progress is\nso fast that some have gone so far as to claim that the majority of human labor may be automatable\nwithin the next couple of years (Eloundou et al., 2023; Amodei & Fridman, 2024). On the other hand,\nothers are skeptical, claiming that language models cannot truly reason (Kambhampati et al., 2024),\n∗\n\nEqual contribution.\n\nPreprint.\n\n\fDiverse\nRealistic Tasks\nProfessional\n\nSimulated\nColleague\n\nGitLab\nCTO\n\nHR\n\n>_\n\nPlane\n\nBrowser\n\nEngineer\n\naction\n\nPython\nobserve\nCode\n\nTerminal\nReproducible\nEnvironment\nSelf-hosted\n\nAgent\n\nAdmin\n\narrange meeting room\n\nDS\n\nanalyze spreadsheet\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
