{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"arXiv:2412.17258v1 [cs.LG] 23 Dec 2024\n\nAn Intrinsically Explainable Approach to\nDetecting Vertebral Compression Fractures in CT\nScans via Neurosymbolic Modeling\nBlanca Inigo1 , Yiqing Shen1 , Benjamin D. Killeen1 ,\nMichelle Song2 , Axel Krieger2 , Christopher Bradley3 ,\nMathias Unberath1\n1\n\nDepartment of Computer Science, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD\nDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD\n3\nDepartment of Interventional Radiology, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD\n2\n\nDecember 24, 2024\nAbstract\nVertebral compression fractures (VCFs) are a common and potentially\nserious consequence of osteoporosis. Yet, they often remain undiagnosed.\nOpportunistic screening, which involves automated analysis of medical\nimaging data acquired primarily for other purposes, is a cost-effective\nmethod to identify undiagnosed VCFs. In high-stakes scenarios like opportunistic medical diagnosis, model interpretability is a key factor for\nthe adoption of AI recommendations. Rule-based methods are inherently\nexplainable and closely align with clinical guidelines, but they are not\nimmediately applicable to high-dimensional data such as CT scans. To\naddress this gap, we introduce a neurosymbolic approach for VCF detection in CT volumes. The proposed model combines deep learning (DL)\nfor vertebral segmentation with a shape-based algorithm (SBA) that analyzes vertebral height distributions in salient anatomical regions. This\nallows for the definition of a rule set over the height distributions to detect\nVCFs. Evaluation of VerSe19 dataset shows that our method achieves an\naccuracy of 96% and a sensitivity of 91% in VCF detection. In comparison, a black box model, DenseNet, achieved an accuracy of 95% and\nsensitivity of 91% in the same dataset. Our results demonstrate that our\nintrinsically explainable approach can match or surpass the performance\nof black box deep neural networks while providing additional insights into\nwhy a prediction was made. This transparency can enhance clinician’s\ntrust thus, supporting more informed decision-making in VCF diagnosis\nand treatment planning.\n\nKeywords: Explainable AI, Transparency, Interpretable AI, Machine Learning (ML), Artificial Intelligence (AI), Medical Image Analysis\n\n301\n\n\f1\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nVertebral compression fractures (VCFs) are a significant health concern, particularly in aging populations.[1, 2, 3] As the most common complication of\nosteoporosis,[4] VCFs affect more than 700,000 Americans annually[5, 6, 2, 7]\nand have a global incidence rate of 10.7 per 1000 women and 5.7 per 1000 men\nas of 2012.[2]. Automated detection of VCFs in computed tomography (CT)\nimages is an important step toward low-cost opportunistic screening of VCFs. It\nenables straightforward screening of CT images acquired in the course of routine\ncare, [8] and supports large-scale extraction of the desired cohort from existing\nimage databases. Ultimately, these cohorts can be used to generate large-scale\nin silico simulations of VCF cases in other medical image modalities [9, 10],\nfacilitating further research and development in the field.\nIn recent years, deep neural networks (DNNs) have yielded significant improvements to VCF detection and classification,[11, 12] but the lack of explainability in these algorithms is regarded as a barrier to their real-world implementation and adoption.[13] One avenue for explainability is the use of saliency maps\nto identify image regions that most influenced a DNN’s output.[14] However,\nrecent work suggests that saliency-based explainability is vulnerable to imperceptible perturbations in the input, which cause a DNN to reverse its decision\nwithout affecting the corresponding saliency map.[15] Other promising strategies leverage deep learning techniques with interpretable algorithms, ensuring\nthe decision-making process adheres to standardized medical guidelines. [16,\n17, 18] There is, however, an unmet need for more interpretable VCF diagnosis\nalgorithms based on CT imaging.\nNeurosymbolic AI, which combines the strengths of neural networks and\nsymbolic AI, has been explored to create more interpretable AI systems[19].\nThere is a growing interest in evaluating user trust and adherence given different explainability mechanisms[20, 21]. In the scope of VCF detection, Burns et\nal.[22] and Baum et al.[23] automated the extraction of vertebral height parameters and used them to detect fractures, employing a machine learning model\nand statistical tests, respectively. However, these approaches still fall short of\nproviding a fully transparent and interpretable solution. To address this need,\nwe propose an intrinsically explainable model that extracts symbolic representations of knowledge and defines logical rules to accurately detect VCFs. Our\napproach generates standardized 2D height maps for each vertebra and computes statistical measurements from multiple sections of the map. Unlike traditional methods that focus on anterior, middle, and posterior heights (AH, MH,\nPH)[24, 23, 22, 25], our model captures height measurements across the entire\naxial plane of the vertebra. This allows for a more complete representation of\nvertebral structures and deformations. Using these parameters, a predefined\n2-rule set indicates whether the vertebra is moderate or severely fractured, ensuring generalization and interpretability.\n\n\f(a) Height Map Extraction\n\n(b) Height\nSections\n\nMap\n\nFigure 1: (a) The height map extraction process relies on vertebral body segmentation with TotalSegmentator [28] to obtain height maps in a consistent\nlocal coordinate system. (b) Drawing on real diagnostic decision-making, we\ndefine 7 regions of the vertebral body to inform the severity rating.\n\n2\n\nMETHODS\n\n2.1\n\nData processing and parameter extraction overview\n\nThis method aims to provide an interpretable decision-making pipeline to identify VCFs in a straightforward and intuitive way. Our model generates rules\nbased on domain knowledge about vertebral anatomy and fracture characteristics, ensuring that the decision-making process is interpretable and evidencebased.[26, 27, 24]\nFigure 1a shows the pipeline followed to generate the vertebral shape measurements. First, for every CT scan, the vertebral bodies are segmented using\nTotalSegmentator [28, 29]. Then, individual 3D meshes are generated for each\nvertebral body using the marching cubes algorithm[30]. Height map generation\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"UniGuardian: A Unified Defense for Detecting Prompt Injection,\nBackdoor Attacks and Adversarial Attacks in Large Language Models\nHuawei Lin 1 Yingjie Lao 2 Tong Geng 3 Tan Yu 4 Weijie Zhao 1\n1\nRochester Institute of Technology 2 Tufts University\n3\nUniversity of Rochester 4 NVIDIA\n\narXiv:2502.13141v1 [cs.CL] 18 Feb 2025\n\nAbstract\nLarge Language Models (LLMs) are vulnerable to attacks like prompt injection, backdoor\nattacks, and adversarial attacks, which manipulate prompts or models to generate harmful\noutputs. In this paper, departing from traditional deep learning attack paradigms, we\nexplore their intrinsic relationship and collectively term them Prompt Trigger Attacks (PTA).\nThis raises a key question: Can we determine\nif a prompt is benign or poisoned? To address\nthis, we propose UniGuardian, the first unified\ndefense mechanism designed to detect prompt\ninjection, backdoor attacks, and adversarial attacks in LLMs. Additionally, we introduce\na single-forward strategy to optimize the detection pipeline, enabling simultaneous attack\ndetection and text generation within a single\nforward pass. Our experiments confirm that\nUniGuardian accurately and efficiently identifies malicious prompts in LLMs.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nLarge Language Models (LLMs) have achieved\nremarkable success across a wide range of\nfields, including machine translation (Zhang et al.,\n2023; Cui et al., 2024), text generation (Zhang\net al., 2024a; Li et al., 2024a) and questionanswering (Shao et al., 2023). Their capabilities\nhave revolutionized LLM applications, enabling\nmore accurate and context-aware interactions.\nAttacks on LLMs. However, LLMs have become attractive targets for various forms of attacks (He and Vechev, 2023; Yao et al., 2023; Lin\net al., 2024b). Many studies have investigated attacks that involve harmful or malicious prompts, as\nthese are some of the easiest ways for attackers to\nexploit these models (Kandpal et al., 2023; Li et al.,\n2024c; Xiang et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024b; Huang\net al., 2024). These include prompt injection (Liu\net al., 2023, 2024; Piet et al., 2024), backdoor attacks (Li et al., 2024c; Zhang et al., 2024c; Lin\net al., 2023), and adversarial attacks (Zou et al.,\n\n2023; Shayegani et al., 2023). Such attacks aim\nto manipulate the model’s behavior using carefully\ncrafted prompts, often leading to harmful or unexpected outputs. Below is an overview of these\nattack types, where blue text is the original prompt\nand red text denotes the injected part by attackers:\n• Prompt Injection is a novel threat to LLMs,\nwhere attackers manipulate inputs to override\nintended prompts, leading to unintended outputs.\nFor example, “{Original Prompt} Ignore previous prompt and do {Target Behavior}”.\n• Backdoor Attacks embed backdoors into the\nmodel during training or finetuning. These backdoor remain dormant during typical usage but can\nbe activated by specific triggers. For example,\n“{Original Prompt} {Backdoor Trigger}”.\n• Adversarial Attacks involve subtle perturbations to input prompts that cause the model to\ndeviate from its expected output. For example,\n“{Original Prompt} ??..@%$*@”.\nDefense Approaches. Despite extensive research, defending against these attacks remains a\nsignificant challenge (Kumar, 2024; Raina et al.,\n2024; Kumar, 2024; Dong et al., 2024). Qi\net al. (2021) propose ONION, a textual defense\nmethod for backdoor attacks that detects outlier\nwords by calculating perplexity (PPL). However, in\nLLMs, many backdoor techniques embed triggers\nwithout disrupting fluency or coherence (Zhang\net al., 2024c; Zhao et al., 2024), and Liu et al.\n(2024) show that PPL-based detection is insufficient against prompt injection. Yang et al. (2021)\nintroduce RAP, a method that assesses prompts\nby analyzing loss behavior under perturbations,\nbut it requires training a soft embedding, which\nis computationally intensive for LLMs. Moreover,\nLLMs’ ability to detect and disregard such perturbations makes differentiation challenging (Dong\net al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023; Kumar et al., 2023).\nDefensing by Fine-tuned LLMs. Recent detection approaches utilize fine-tuned LLMs for content\n\n\fsafety classification (Sawtell et al., 2024; Zhang\net al., 2024b; Zheng et al., 2024), including Llama\nGuard from Mate (Inan et al., 2023) and Granite\nGuardian from IBM (Padhi et al., 2024). These\nmodels classify content from prompts and conversations to detect harmful, unsafe, biased, or inappropriate material. While effective against explicit\nharmful prompts, they struggle with more subtle\nthreats such as misinformation, privacy breaches,\nand other unseen attack target that are harder to\nidentify (Liu et al., 2024; Qi et al., 2024).\nWhile existing approaches can mitigate attacks,\nthey typically detect only one type of attacks. However, in LLMs, these threats share a common\nmechanism–manipulating model behavior by poisoning prompts, as shown in Figure 1. We define\nthese collectively as Prompt Trigger Attacks (PTA)–\na class of attacks that alter prompts to manipulate\nmodel behavior and outputs. This definition clarifies the inter-relationships among these attacks and\nsupports the development of a unified detection.\nResearch Questions. This paper explores three\nkey research questions: RQ1: What are the intrinsic relationships among prompt injection, backdoor\nattacks, and adversarial attacks in LLMs? RQ2:\nHow does an LLM’s behavior differ between an\ninjected and a clear prompt? RQ3: Can we reliably\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"arXiv:2407.18401v1 [math.OC] 25 Jul 2024\n\nSTACKELBERG GAMES WITH THE THIRD PARTY\nYIMING JIANG, YAWEI WEI, AND JIE XUE\nAbstract. In this paper, we introduce the third party to achieve the Stackelberg equilibrium with the time inconsistency in three different Stackelberg\ngames, which are the discrete-time games, the dynamic games, and the mean\nfield games. Here all followers are experiencing learning-by-doing. The role\nof a third party is similar to industry associations, they supervise the leader’s\nimplementation and impose penalties for the defection with the discount factor. Then we obtain different forms of discount factors in different models and\neffective conditions to prevent defection. These results are consistent and the\nthird party intervention is effective and maneuverable in practice.\n\n1. INTRODUCTION\n1.1. Research history and motivations. The model of learning-by-doing has\noften been identified as one of the important sources of economic growth. During\nthe production process, knowledge as the by-product may improve the technique\nlevel of the producer, which is clearly different from that in the research institutions.\nThe earliest work about learning-by-doing model can be traced back to Arrow [1] in\n1962. Later Sheshinski [29] and Romer [28] introduced this idea in the neoclassical\ngrowth model with optimizing agents. Benchekroun et al. [4] discussed a duopoly\nmarket in one-shot Stackelberg games.\nStackelberg games are hierarchical ones where there are several leaders with a\ndominant position over the followers, which was first proposed by Stackelberg in\n[30]. There are many scholars studied the Stackelberg game, see Bensoussan et al.\n[2] and Moon [23]. For mean field Stackelberg games, the mean field behavior of\nthe followers will depend on an arbitrary strategy of the leader. Mean field games\n(briefly, MFG) theory is devoted to the analysis of differential games with infinitely\nmany players. This theory has been introduced by Lasry and Lions in 2006 [21]\n[22]. At about the same time, Huang et al. [20] solved the large population games\nindependently. The MFG problem with major and minor players was considered in\nMoon and Bassar [24], Nguyen and Huang [26]. The mass behavior of the minor\nplayers is strongly affected by the major players through the cost function and\nstochastic differential equations (briefly, SDE). Here SDE is driven by the white\nnoise, but not limited to white noises, see Bo and Yang [7].\nThe time inconsistency is an obviously important problem for Stackelberg games,\nwhich makes Stackelberg equilibrium inappropriate. Dating back to Strotz [31],\nthere are three approaches to handle the time inconsistency: precommitment, equilibrium and naive strategies. A precommitment strategy optimizes the objective\n2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 49N10; 49N80; 49N90.\nKey words and phrases. Mean field games; Stackelberg equilibrium; Time inconsistency;\nLearning-by-doing.\nAcknowledgements: This work is supported by the NSFC under the grands 12271269 and\nsupported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.\n1\n\n\f2\n\nYIMING JIANG, YAWEI WEI, AND JIE XUE\n\nfunctional anticipated at the very beginning time and the leader sticks with this\nstrategy over the whole game period, see Zhou and Li [33]. An equilibrium strategy consistently optimizes the objective functional anticipated at every time point\nin the similar manner of dynamic programming but using the concept of subgame\nperfect equilibrium, see Pun [34]. A naive strategy is such that, at any time, it\ncoincides with the optimal strategy with respect to the preference at that time. In\nfact, the theory asserts that, smart followers will not believe any promises made by\nthe leader, and therefore the leader cannot adopt the naive strategy in this way.\nHowever, there are some disadvantages to precommitment and equilibrium strategies. On the one hand, the precommitment strategy is sometimes inferior because\nits strong commitment leads to time inconsistency in efficiency, see Cui et al. [13],\nand its error-accumulation property brings huge estimation errors, see Chiu et al.\n[12]. On the other hand, since precommitment is not always a feasible solution to\nthe problem of intertemporal conflict, the leader with insight into his future unreliability may adopt an equilibrium strategy and reject any plan which he will not\nfollow through. The leader’s goal is then to find the best plan among those that\nhe will actually follow. Although the equilibrium strategy is time consistent, it\nemphasizes local optimality which is always not globally optimal.\nCompared with precommitment and equilibrium strategies, the Stackelberg equilibrium strategy sometimes enables to gain more benefits for all players. Due to\nthe time inconsistency of Stackelberg equilibrium, followers find that the risk of\ntrusting the leader’s commitment is significant, and the leader also finds it difficult\nto withstand the temptation in the future. Introducing a third party is a good\napproach for leaders and followers in the Stackelberg game, even if players have to\npay a certain fee. This idea is inspired by the trigger strategy for Nash games in\nDockner et al. [14]. In [14], to keep the Nash equilibrium, every player has to agree\nto follow a certain target path and sustain this agreement with an effective threat\nto regulate any defector.\nThis motivated us to introduce the third party in the Stackelberg games with\ntime inconsistency. Here the duty of the third party is to enable all players to keep\nthe Stackelberg equilibrium strategy. In this paper, the Stackelberg game with the\nthird party is set as follows. First, the followers need to pay part of the fee and the\nleader needs to pay a certain deposit to begin with the game. Second, the third\nparty will supervise the leader’s execution during the game, and impose mandatory\npenalties in the event of his defection. The appreciated penalties by the third party\ncan ensure Stackelberg equilibrium is credible. Thus it provides a theoretical basis\nfor the game rules. Third, the leader’s deposit is returned and the followers pay\nthe remaining fee at the end of the game. Note that the Stackelberg equilibrium\nwith the third party can be regarded as a modified naive strategy. This discussion\nis similar to an inverse game problem, except that the third party here is not all\nplayers. A similar role to the third party is an industry association, government\ndepartment, or banking institution.\n1.2. Statement of the problems and main results. In this subsection, we first\nset up three different Stackelberg games, which are the discrete-time games, the\ndynamic games and the MFG. In the Stackelberg games, the leader announces his\noptimum strategy in advance by taking into account the rational reactions of the\nfollowers, where the followers are experiencing learning-by-doing with fewer knowledge. Then followers choose their optimum strategies non-cooperatively based on\nthe leader’s strategy. Finally, the leader comes back and implements his announced\n\n\fSTACKELBERG GAMES WITH THE THIRD PARTY\n\n3\n\nstrategy. Hence we obtain the resulting optimum strategies for the leader and\nfollowers by the Pontryagin maximum principle, which forms the Stackelberg equilibrium. In fact, as a rational player, the leader will deviate from his commitment\nwithout the followers’ knowledge and we obtain the defection strategy. Hence the\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Karazin Kharkiv National University, Kharkiv, Ukraine\n3\nSamtskhe-Javakheti State University, Kharadze Abastumani Astrophysical Observatory, Georgia\n2\n\nEmail: shevchenko@astron.kharkov.ua\nAbstract\nWe presented photometric observations for the one UV Ceti type and three W Ursae Majoris- type\nvariable stars. The flare of the UV Ceti type star lasted about two hours, and the star changed magnitude\nto 3.9 within about two minutes. The values of color indices V-R, the rotational periods and the composite\nlightcurves have been obtained for the EW stars. Using a relation of an absolute magnitude- period\nobtained by Mateo and Rucinski (2017) and interstellar extinction from the three-dimensional map of\nMilky Way dust (http://argonaut.skymaps.info) and Green et al. (2019), we have calculated the absolute\nmagnitudes of the EW stars and distances to them. The parallaxes obtained from our data differ from those\ngiven in Gaia DR 3, which may be due to insufficient quality calibration of the absolute magnitude-period\nrelation and with the estimations of interstellar extinction.\nKey words: flare stars, binary stars, CCD-observations, lightcurves, periods\n1. Introduction\n\n2. Observations and Results\n\nMany of different types of variable stars\nwere discovered last times due to special groundbased and space-based survey programs,\nespecially Gaia (Ansari et al. 2023, Chen et al.\n2020, Gavras et al. 2023, Mowlavi et al. 2023.\nQian et al. 2017, Rimoldini, et al. 2023, Samus et\nal. 2017, etc.). This made it possible to\nsignificantly improve statistics on the number of\nvariable stars in the Milky Way, to identify some\ncorrelations between the period of brightness\nchanges and absolute magnitude, and to determine\nthe general location of variables of different types\nin our Galaxy. Despite these achievements, the\ntotal number of some types of variables is still not\nsufficient to clarify their nature of variability. An\nexample would be flaring or pulsating stars, whose\nvariability time is about one-two hours. Checking\nstar fields in different observing programs allows\nto detect new variable stars or to get a new\ninformation about already discovered stars. In our\nasteroid programs (Chiorny et al. 2023,\nShevchenko et al. 2021, 2022), we have checked\nstar fields for star variability and we discovered\nseveral variable stars of different types\n(Shevchenko et al. 2013, 2018, Zheleznyak et al.\n2015). In this paper, we present new observational\nresults of some variable stars of different types\nincluding one new detecting flaring star.\n\nWe have carried out the CCD-observations\nof variable stars in 2013 - 2015 for 15 nights with\nthe 0.7-m reflector at the Chuguyiv Observational\nStation of the Astronomical Institute of V.N.\nKarazin Kharkiv National University as a part of\nstudent astrophysical practice (Shevchenko et al.\n2013, 2018) and the some programs of an\ninvestigation of the asteroids (Shevchenko et al.\n2021, 2022). Observations on this telescope were\nstopped on February 24, 2022 because of russian\ninvasion to Ukraine. The observation station was\noccupied and barbarously destroyed by russian\ntroops. Unfortunately, the telescope is now\ninactive. The telescope was equipped with a\nML4710 CCD camera (1056×1027 pixels, pixel\nsize 13×13 mkm). The camera’s field of view was\n16.2′×16.2′ with the scale 0.96″/pixel. The\nquantum efficiency of the ML 4710 in the\nwavelength range of 0.55 to 0.75 mkm is 93 - 95%,\nwhich makes it possible to perform highly efficient\nand high-quality photometric observations of faint\nobjects. Images were taken using V and R bands of\nthe Johnson–Cousins photometric system.\nOriginal CCD-frames were calibrated for dark\ncurrent and flat field in the standard manner. The\nCCD-observations and data reduction methods\nwere explained by Krugly et al. (2002) and by\nChiorny et al. (2023). The brightness\nmeasurements of stars on CCD-images were done\n\n\fusing the aperture photometry package\n(ASTPHOT) developed by Mottola et al. (1995).\nThe absolute calibrations of the magnitudes were\nperformed using the comparison stars in the SDSS\n(ugriz) photometric system, taken from the\nAPASS DR9 (Henden et al. 2012) and PanSTARRS DR1 (Chambers et al. 2016) catalogs. To\ntransform them to the Johnson-Cousins (UBVRI)\nphotometric system, we used the corresponding\nequations given in Fukugita et al. (1996), and in\nTonry et al. (2012). The accuracy of the resultant\nabsolute photometry is within 0.01-0.03 mag. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"1\n\nData-Driven Approaches for Modelling Target\nBehaviour\n\narXiv:2410.10538v1 [stat.ML] 14 Oct 2024\n\nIsabel Schlangen, Member, IEEE, André Brandenburger, Mengwei Sun, James R. Hopgood, Senior Member, IEEE\n\nAbstract—The performance of tracking algorithms strongly\ndepends on the chosen model assumptions regarding the target\ndynamics. If there is a strong mismatch between the chosen model\nand the true object motion, the track quality may be poor or\nthe track is easily lost. Still, the true dynamics might not be\nknown a priori or it is too complex to be expressed in a tractable\nmathematical formulation. This paper provides a comparative\nstudy between three different methods that use machine learning\nto describe the underlying object motion based on training\ndata. The first method builds on Gaussian Processes (GPs) for\npredicting the object motion, the second learns the parameters of\nan Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) filter and the third uses a\nLong Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network as a motion model.\nAll methods are compared against an Extended Kalman Filter\n(EKF) with an analytic motion model as a benchmark and their\nrespective strengths are highlighted in one simulated and two\nreal-world scenarios.\nIndex Terms—Gaussian Process, Interacting Multiple Model,\nLong Short-Term Memory, Dynamic Models, ChapmanKolmogorov Equation, Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Bayes Filter.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nConventional single-target tracking usually builds on the\nparadigm of Bayesian filtering, which comprises the prediction\nacross time and the correction of the predicted belief using an\nincoming measurement. The performance of the Bayes filter is\nsubject to the chosen mathematical models for the target dynamics and the properties of the sensor in use. In the majority\nof applications, the sensor characteristics are known a priori\nor can be calibrated in preliminary experiments to minimise\na possible model mismatch. The dynamics of the object of\ninterest, on the other hand, might be hard to formalise in\nadvance if the object type is unclear or its physics are not wellunderstood. In many cases, well-known object dynamics such\nas Constant White Noise Acceleration (CWNA) [1] are chosen\nfor convenience, compensating small model inaccuracies by\nappropriate amounts of process noise. Unfortunately, a grave\nmismatch between the true target behaviour and the chosen\nmodel leads to poor estimation results or even track loss.\nOne option for a more versatile description of the target\nmotion is to involve more than one model, for example in an\nInteracting Multiple Model (IMM) approach. The parameters\nof the IMM method are usually predefined but could also be\nlearned as proposed in [2]. A traditional method for filter\nThe first two authors are with Fraunhofer FKIE, Fraunhoferstr. 20, Wachtberg, Germany. The third author is with Cranfield University, Bradford, UK.\nThe last author is with the University of Edinburgh, Edinburgh, UK.\nManuscript submitted for review to IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing\non October 14, 2024.\n\nparameter optimization is expectation maximization, which\ninvolves successive calculation of the expected parameter\nlikelihood and its maximization [3]–[5]. This typically requires\ncomplex analytical calculations, which may need adjustments\nif the model or sensor architecture changes. Barrat et al. [6]\napply convex optimization tools to train Kalman smoother\nparameters [6]. While simpler, this method also relies on\nanalytical calculations, which can be challenging for complex architectures. Abbeel et al. [7] demonstrated training\nan Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) using coordinate ascent,\nand Greenberg et al. [8] utilized gradient descent for Kalman\nfilter optimization. These methods can be trained on groundtruth data or the measurement likelihood, outperforming handtuned parameters in real systems [7]. Xu et al. [9] introduced\nEKFNet, fitting EKF parameters using gradient descent akin\nto neural network optimization. However, this method requires\nproblem-specific parameter regularization functions. Furthermore, Coskun et al. [10] proposed generating Kalman filter\nmatrices online using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, outperforming traditional filters and LSTM networks.\nInstead of optimising the filter parameters with machine\nlearning, it is possible to replace parts of the Bayes recursion\nwith a neural network. A recent work by Liang and Meyer [11]\nuses belief propagation to enhance measurement association\nfor multi-target tracking problems. Several research groups\nhave implemented transformer-based data association to include temporal information in the measurement update [12]–\n[14]. In all of these methods, however, the target dynamics\nare assumed to follow a CWNA-like motion, which cannot\ncope well with highly manoeuvring targets. In contrast, [15]\nintroduced a particle filter that performs the temporal transition\nwith a trained LSTM network. Following this, a Kalman\nfilter has been equipped with a similar LSTM architecture in\nthe prediction step, leading to the Mnemonic Kalman Filter\n(MKF).\nA parallel approach was developed by Sun et al. [16],\n[17], which builds on Gaussian Processes (GPs) rather than\nrecurrent networks to model the state transition. All of these\nmethods have in common that the motion model is found based\non a set of suitable training data, hence no prior knowledge\non the physics of the target of interest is necessary. This\npaper, in contrast, focuses on data-driven methods to describe\nobject motion in a single-target Bayesian framework. The\nadvantage of data-driven models is that they solely depend on\nthe availability of a suitable set of training data, from which\nthe characteristic properties of the underlying target dynamics\nare derived. This is especially advantageous in cases with a\nrapid change of motion behaviour or very complex dynamics\n\n\f2\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Does the Z boson have a lighter cousin?\nJeff Greensite\n\narXiv:2501.02083v1 [hep-lat] 3 Jan 2025\n\nPhysics and Astronomy Department\nSan Francisco State University\nSan Francisco, CA 94132, USA\n(Dated: January 7, 2025)\n\nIn the quenched electroweak theory on the lattice I construct a set of physical states which overlap the physical photon and Z boson states. This is done by employing eigenstates of the covariant lattice Laplacian, in\naddition to the Higgs and lattice link variables, to construct gauge invariant vector boson creation operators.\nDiagonalizing the transfer matrix in the subspace of Hilbert space spanned by this set yields a massless photon\nand massive Z particle, as expected. But in the numerical data there is evidence for more vector bosons in the\nspectrum, albeit with considerable uncertainty in their masses, with the lowest finite mass particle in the range\nof 3-4 GeV.\nI.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nParticles in the spectrum of any non-trivial quantum field\ntheory are extended objects which include, depending on the\ntheory, surrounding color electric and Higgs fields, and a\ncloud of virtual particles. In this sense even an “elementary”\nparticle in gauge Higgs theories can be regarded as a composite object. But bound composite objects in quantum mechanics tend to have a discrete spectrum, leading to the speculation that elementary particles may themselves have a spectrum\nof excited states. In [1] I have presented numerical evidence\nthat the gauge and Higgs fields surrounding a static charge in\nSU(3) gauge Higgs theory do have such a spectrum of excitations in the Higgs phase. In the absence of a lattice formulation of chiral fermions one cannot carry out a similarly reliable\ncalculation in the electroweak sector of the Standard Model.\nOne may, however, ask whether there might be new and unanticipated vector boson states in the quenched electroweak theory, aside from the usual photon, W, and Z bosons.1\nIn this investigation, as in [1], the use of what we have elsewhere called “pseudomatter” fields [3] is essential. A pseudomatter field is a functional of the gauge fields alone which\ntransforms like a field in the fundamental representation of\nthe gauge group, except that, unlike a true matter field, it is invariant under the global center subgroup of the gauge group.\nThese fields can be combined with standard gauge and matter fields to generate physical states which are invariant under\ninfinitesimal gauge transformations, and yet transform nontrivially under the global center subgroup of the gauge group.\nStates of this kind are charged states. Indeed, since an uncharged (= color neutral) state is gauge invariant, a charged\nstate by contrast must transform in some way under the gauge\n\n1\n\nThis question was recently addressed in [2], in a version with a fixed magnitude Higgs field, and there we encountered difficulties with scaling. The\npresent article improves on this situation in several ways, using (i) the standard Higgs potential rather than a fixed-modulus Higgs; (ii) a less ambiguous procedure, namely the solution of the generalized energy eigenvalue\nproblem, to determine masses of the low-lying excitations; and (iii) a much\nlarger set of pseudomatter fields, as discussed below.\n\ngroup, and yet conform to the Gauss law constraint on physical states. An example appears in the simple case of a static\ncharged source coupled to the quantized Maxwell field. The\nground state of this system in A0 = 0 gauge was derived long\nago by Dirac [4]. G. Shevchenko1,2, D. O.,Danylko2, I. G. Slyusarev1,2, R. A. Chigladze3\n1\n\nInstitute of Astronomy of V. N. Karazin Kharkiv National University, Kharkiv, Ukraine\n3\nSamtskhe-Javakheti State University, Kharadze Abastumani Astrophysical Observatory, Georgia\n2\n\nEmail: shevchenko@astron.kharkov.ua\nAbstract\nWe presented photometric observations for the one UV Ceti type and three W Ursae Majoris- type\nvariable stars. The flare of the UV Ceti type star lasted about two hours, and the star changed magnitude\nto 3.9 within about two minutes. The values of color indices V-R, the rotational periods and the composite\nlightcurves have been obtained for the EW stars. Using a relation of an absolute magnitude- period\nobtained by Mateo and Rucinski (2017) and interstellar extinction from the three-dimensional map of\nMilky Way dust (http://argonaut.skymaps.info) and Green et al. (2019), we have calculated the absolute\nmagnitudes of the EW stars and distances to them. The parallaxes obtained from our data differ from those\ngiven in Gaia DR 3, which may be due to insufficient quality calibration of the absolute magnitude-period\nrelation and with the estimations of interstellar extinction.\nKey words: flare stars, binary stars, CCD-observations, lightcurves, periods\n1. Introduction\n\n2. Observations and Results\n\nMany of different types of variable stars\nwere discovered last times due to special groundbased and space-based survey programs,\nespecially Gaia (Ansari et al. 2023, Chen et al.\n2020, Gavras et al. 2023, Mowlavi et al. 2023.\nQian et al. 2017, Rimoldini, et al. 2023, Samus et\nal. 2017, etc.). This made it possible to\nsignificantly improve statistics on the number of\nvariable stars in the Milky Way, to identify some\ncorrelations between the period of brightness\nchanges and absolute magnitude, and to determine\nthe general location of variables of different types\nin our Galaxy. Despite these achievements, the\ntotal number of some types of variables is still not\nsufficient to clarify their nature of variability. An\nexample would be flaring or pulsating stars, whose\nvariability time is about one-two hours. Checking\nstar fields in different observing programs allows\nto detect new variable stars or to get a new\ninformation about already discovered stars. In our\nasteroid programs (Chiorny et al. 2023,\nShevchenko et al. 2021, 2022), we have checked\nstar fields for star variability and we discovered\nseveral variable stars of different types\n(Shevchenko et al. 2013, 2018, Zheleznyak et al.\n2015). In this paper, we present new observational\nresults of some variable stars of different types\nincluding one new detecting flaring star.\n\nWe have carried out the CCD-observations\nof variable stars in 2013 - 2015 for 15 nights with\nthe 0.7-m reflector at the Chuguyiv Observational\nStation of the Astronomical Institute of V.N.\nKarazin Kharkiv National University as a part of\nstudent astrophysical practice (Shevchenko et al.\n2013, 2018) and the some programs of an\ninvestigation of the asteroids (Shevchenko et al.\n2021, 2022). Observations on this telescope were\nstopped on February 24, 2022 because of russian\ninvasion to Ukraine. The observation station was\noccupied and barbarously destroyed by russian\ntroops. Unfortunately, the telescope is now\ninactive. The telescope was equipped with a\nML4710 CCD camera (1056×1027 pixels, pixel\nsize 13×13 mkm). The camera’s field of view was\n16.2′×16.2′ with the scale 0.96″/pixel. The\nquantum efficiency of the ML 4710 in the\nwavelength range of 0.55 to 0.75 mkm is 93 - 95%,\nwhich makes it possible to perform highly efficient\nand high-quality photometric observations of faint\nobjects. Images were taken using V and R bands of\nthe Johnson–Cousins photometric system.\nOriginal CCD-frames were calibrated for dark\ncurrent and flat field in the standard manner. The\nCCD-observations and data reduction methods\nwere explained by Krugly et al. (2002) and by\nChiorny et al. (2023). The brightness\nmeasurements of stars on CCD-images were done\n\n\fusing the aperture photometry package\n(ASTPHOT) developed by Mottola et al. (1995).\nThe absolute calibrations of the magnitudes were\nperformed using the comparison stars in the SDSS\n(ugriz) photometric system, taken from the\nAPASS DR9 (Henden et al. 2012) and PanSTARRS DR1 (Chambers et al. 2016) catalogs. To\ntransform them to the Johnson-Cousins (UBVRI)\nphotometric system, we used the corresponding\nequations given in Fukugita et al. (1996), and in\nTonry et al. (2012). The accuracy of the resultant\nabsolute photometry is within 0.01-0.03 mag. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"1\n\nData-Driven Approaches for Modelling Target\nBehaviour\n\narXiv:2410.10538v1 [stat.ML] 14 Oct 2024\n\nIsabel Schlangen, Member, IEEE, André Brandenburger, Mengwei Sun, James R. Hopgood, Senior Member, IEEE\n\nAbstract—The performance of tracking algorithms strongly\ndepends on the chosen model assumptions regarding the target\ndynamics. If there is a strong mismatch between the chosen model\nand the true object motion, the track quality may be poor or\nthe track is easily lost. Still, the true dynamics might not be\nknown a priori or it is too complex to be expressed in a tractable\nmathematical formulation. This paper provides a comparative\nstudy between three different methods that use machine learning\nto describe the underlying object motion based on training\ndata. The first method builds on Gaussian Processes (GPs) for\npredicting the object motion, the second learns the parameters of\nan Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) filter and the third uses a\nLong Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network as a motion model.\nAll methods are compared against an Extended Kalman Filter\n(EKF) with an analytic motion model as a benchmark and their\nrespective strengths are highlighted in one simulated and two\nreal-world scenarios.\nIndex Terms—Gaussian Process, Interacting Multiple Model,\nLong Short-Term Memory, Dynamic Models, ChapmanKolmogorov Equation, Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Bayes Filter.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nConventional single-target tracking usually builds on the\nparadigm of Bayesian filtering, which comprises the prediction\nacross time and the correction of the predicted belief using an\nincoming measurement. The performance of the Bayes filter is\nsubject to the chosen mathematical models for the target dynamics and the properties of the sensor in use. In the majority\nof applications, the sensor characteristics are known a priori\nor can be calibrated in preliminary experiments to minimise\na possible model mismatch. The dynamics of the object of\ninterest, on the other hand, might be hard to formalise in\nadvance if the object type is unclear or its physics are not wellunderstood. In many cases, well-known object dynamics such\nas Constant White Noise Acceleration (CWNA) [1] are chosen\nfor convenience, compensating small model inaccuracies by\nappropriate amounts of process noise. Unfortunately, a grave\nmismatch between the true target behaviour and the chosen\nmodel leads to poor estimation results or even track loss.\nOne option for a more versatile description of the target\nmotion is to involve more than one model, for example in an\nInteracting Multiple Model (IMM) approach. The parameters\nof the IMM method are usually predefined but could also be\nlearned as proposed in [2]. A traditional method for filter\nThe first two authors are with Fraunhofer FKIE, Fraunhoferstr. 20, Wachtberg, Germany. The third author is with Cranfield University, Bradford, UK.\nThe last author is with the University of Edinburgh, Edinburgh, UK.\nManuscript submitted for review to IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing\non October 14, 2024.\n\nparameter optimization is expectation maximization, which\ninvolves successive calculation of the expected parameter\nlikelihood and its maximization [3]–[5]. This typically requires\ncomplex analytical calculations, which may need adjustments\nif the model or sensor architecture changes. Barrat et al. [6]\napply convex optimization tools to train Kalman smoother\nparameters [6]. While simpler, this method also relies on\nanalytical calculations, which can be challenging for complex architectures. Abbeel et al. [7] demonstrated training\nan Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) using coordinate ascent,\nand Greenberg et al. [8] utilized gradient descent for Kalman\nfilter optimization. These methods can be trained on groundtruth data or the measurement likelihood, outperforming handtuned parameters in real systems [7]. Xu et al. [9] introduced\nEKFNet, fitting EKF parameters using gradient descent akin\nto neural network optimization. However, this method requires\nproblem-specific parameter regularization functions. Furthermore, Coskun et al. [10] proposed generating Kalman filter\nmatrices online using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, outperforming traditional filters and LSTM networks.\nInstead of optimising the filter parameters with machine\nlearning, it is possible to replace parts of the Bayes recursion\nwith a neural network. A recent work by Liang and Meyer [11]\nuses belief propagation to enhance measurement association\nfor multi-target tracking problems. Several research groups\nhave implemented transformer-based data association to include temporal information in the measurement update [12]–\n[14]. In all of these methods, however, the target dynamics\nare assumed to follow a CWNA-like motion, which cannot\ncope well with highly manoeuvring targets. In contrast, [15]\nintroduced a particle filter that performs the temporal transition\nwith a trained LSTM network. Following this, a Kalman\nfilter has been equipped with a similar LSTM architecture in\nthe prediction step, leading to the Mnemonic Kalman Filter\n(MKF).\nA parallel approach was developed by Sun et al. [16],\n[17], which builds on Gaussian Processes (GPs) rather than\nrecurrent networks to model the state transition. All of these\nmethods have in common that the motion model is found based\non a set of suitable training data, hence no prior knowledge\non the physics of the target of interest is necessary. This\npaper, in contrast, focuses on data-driven methods to describe\nobject motion in a single-target Bayesian framework. The\nadvantage of data-driven models is that they solely depend on\nthe availability of a suitable set of training data, from which\nthe characteristic properties of the underlying target dynamics\nare derived. This is especially advantageous in cases with a\nrapid change of motion behaviour or very complex dynamics\n\n\f2\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Does the Z boson have a lighter cousin?\nJeff Greensite\n\narXiv:2501.02083v1 [hep-lat] 3 Jan 2025\n\nPhysics and Astronomy Department\nSan Francisco State University\nSan Francisco, CA 94132, USA\n(Dated: January 7, 2025)\n\nIn the quenched electroweak theory on the lattice I construct a set of physical states which overlap the physical photon and Z boson states. This is done by employing eigenstates of the covariant lattice Laplacian, in\naddition to the Higgs and lattice link variables, to construct gauge invariant vector boson creation operators.\nDiagonalizing the transfer matrix in the subspace of Hilbert space spanned by this set yields a massless photon\nand massive Z particle, as expected. But in the numerical data there is evidence for more vector bosons in the\nspectrum, albeit with considerable uncertainty in their masses, with the lowest finite mass particle in the range\nof 3-4 GeV.\nI.\n\nINTRODUCTION\n\nParticles in the spectrum of any non-trivial quantum field\ntheory are extended objects which include, depending on the\ntheory, surrounding color electric and Higgs fields, and a\ncloud of virtual particles. In this sense even an “elementary”\nparticle in gauge Higgs theories can be regarded as a composite object. But bound composite objects in quantum mechanics tend to have a discrete spectrum, leading to the speculation that elementary particles may themselves have a spectrum\nof excited states. In [1] I have presented numerical evidence\nthat the gauge and Higgs fields surrounding a static charge in\nSU(3) gauge Higgs theory do have such a spectrum of excitations in the Higgs phase. In the absence of a lattice formulation of chiral fermions one cannot carry out a similarly reliable\ncalculation in the electroweak sector of the Standard Model.\nOne may, however, ask whether there might be new and unanticipated vector boson states in the quenched electroweak theory, aside from the usual photon, W, and Z bosons.1\nIn this investigation, as in [1], the use of what we have elsewhere called “pseudomatter” fields [3] is essential. A pseudomatter field is a functional of the gauge fields alone which\ntransforms like a field in the fundamental representation of\nthe gauge group, except that, unlike a true matter field, it is invariant under the global center subgroup of the gauge group.\nThese fields can be combined with standard gauge and matter fields to generate physical states which are invariant under\ninfinitesimal gauge transformations, and yet transform nontrivially under the global center subgroup of the gauge group.\nStates of this kind are charged states. Indeed, since an uncharged (= color neutral) state is gauge invariant, a charged\nstate by contrast must transform in some way under the gauge\n\n1\n\nThis question was recently addressed in [2], in a version with a fixed magnitude Higgs field, and there we encountered difficulties with scaling. The\npresent article improves on this situation in several ways, using (i) the standard Higgs potential rather than a fixed-modulus Higgs; (ii) a less ambiguous procedure, namely the solution of the generalized energy eigenvalue\nproblem, to determine masses of the low-lying excitations; and (iii) a much\nlarger set of pseudomatter fields, as discussed below.\n\ngroup, and yet conform to the Gauss law constraint on physical states. An example appears in the simple case of a static\ncharged source coupled to the quantized Maxwell field. The\nground state of this system in A0 = 0 gauge was derived long\nago by Dirac [4]. In an infinite volume this is\nΨchrg = ψ (x)ρ (x; A)Ψ0 ,\n\n(1)\n\nwhere Ψ0 is the ground state, ψ creates a static fermion, with\n\u0015\n\u0014\nZ\n1\n∂\ne\n3\n(2)\nd z Ai (z )\nρ (x; A) = exp −i\n4π\n∂ zi |x − z |\nand [5]\n\u0014 Z\n\u0015\nZ\n3\n3 ∇ × A(x) · ∇ × A(y)\nΨ0 [A] = exp − d x d y\n.\n16π 3|x − y|2\n\n(3)\n\nThe operator ρ (x; A) is an example of a pseudomatter field.\nIt is easy to check that this field transforms covariantly except under global U(1) transformations which, of course, do\nnot transform the gauge field. As a result, under global U(1)\ntransformations g(x) = eiθ which do not depend on position\nΨchrg → e−iθ Ψchrg .\n\n(4)\n\nIt is this covariance of a physical state under the global center subgroup of the gauge group which distinguishes, in an\ninfinite volume, charged from neutral states in the massless\nand confining phases of a gauge Higgs theories. If the global\ncenter subgroup is unbroken, all charged and neutral states\nare orthogonal. This distinction breaks down when the global\ncenter subgroup of the gauge group, “GCS” hereafter, is spontaneously broken, and in that case the system is in the Higgs\nphase, as argued in [6].\nThese considerations extend to non-abelian theories, where\nthe gauge transformations in the GCS of the SU(N) gauge\ngroup consist of elements of the ZN center, and we consider\ngauge Higgs theories with the Higgs field φ transforming in\nthe fundamental representation of the gauge group. Pseudomatter operators can be used to construct gauge transformations to a physical gauge, and in fact, in the abelian theory, the\n\n\f2\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"1\n\nD2D Coded Caching Schemes for Multiaccess\nNetworks with Combinatorial Access Topology\n\narXiv:2501.10756v2 [cs.IT] 19 Jan 2026\n\nRashid Ummer N.T. and B. Sundar Rajan\nDepartment of Electrical Communication Engineering, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, India\nE-mail: {rashidummer, bsrajan}@iisc.ac.in\n\nAbstract—Device-to-device (D2D) communication is one of the\nmost promising techniques for next-generation wireless Internet\nof Things networks. This paper considers coded caching in a\nwireless multiaccess D2D network, where users communicate\nwith each other and can access multiple cache nodes. Access\ntopologies derived from two combinatorial designs known as\nthe t-design and t-group divisible design (t-GDD), referred to\nas the t-design and t-GDD topologies, respectively, have been\nstudied recently for the multiaccess coded caching (MACC)\nnetwork. These access topologies, which subsume the other\nknown topologies except the cyclic wrap-around topology, are\nextended to a multiaccess D2D coded caching (MADCC) network.\nNovel MADCC schemes are proposed, and schemes are also\nderived from existing MACC schemes with t-design and t-GDD\ntopologies. To compare different MADCC schemes, the metrics\nof load per user and subpacketization level are used while\nkeeping the number of caches, cache size, and access degree\nthe same. A comparison of the proposed schemes with those\nderived from existing MACC schemes, as well as with the existing\nMADCC scheme under a cyclic wrap-around topology, shows\nthat the proposed schemes have an advantage in either load per\nuser or subpacketization level or both. Additionally, several low\nsubpacketization level coded caching schemes are obtained for\nthe original D2D network with dedicated caches.\nIndex Terms—D2D coded caching, placement delivery array,\nmultiaccess networks, t-design, t-group divisible design.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nDevice-to-device (D2D) communication is one of the most\npromising techniques for next-generation wireless Internet of\nThings (IoT) networks [1], [2]. D2D communication also\nserves as a key enabler for the realization of IoT in future\nﬁfth-generation and beyond wireless cellular communication\nsystems [3], [4]. Many emerging IoT applications require very\nlow latency, and edge caching in IoT networks has been\nidentiﬁed as a promising solution to decrease latency [5].\nStudying cache-aided D2D networks is therefore of great\nrelevance to IoT networks. In [6], a cache-enabled mobile\nmulti-D2D network has been studied. Motivated by the coded\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Various IT personas ranging\nfrom Chief Information Officers to Site Reliability Engineers, Security and Compliance officers and IT engineers\nin general are struggling to ensure resiliency, reliability,\nsecurity and cost effective operations of IT Systems.\n\nRealizing the vision of using AI agents to automate critical IT tasks depends on the ability to\nmeasure and understand effectiveness of proposed\nsolutions. We introduce ITBench, a framework\nthat offers a systematic methodology for benchmarking AI agents to address real-world IT automation tasks. Our initial release targets three\nkey areas: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE),\nCompliance and Security Operations (CISO), and\nFinancial Operations (FinOps). The design enables AI researchers to understand the challenges\nand opportunities of AI agents for IT automation\nwith push-button workflows and interpretable metrics. ITBench includes an initial set of 94 realworld scenarios, which can be easily extended by\ncommunity contributions. Our results show that\nagents powered by state-of-the-art models resolve\nonly 13.8% of SRE scenarios, 25.2% of CISO\nscenarios, and 0% of FinOps scenarios. We expect ITBench to be a key enabler of AI-driven IT\nautomation that is correct, safe, and fast.\n\nThe recent CrowdStrike outage highlighted these challenges\nas it brought down our society’s most critical systems from\nhospital services to air travel and was estimated to cost US\nFortune 500 companies a staggering $5.4 billion (Kerner,\n2024). Given the complexity of IT tasks,\na major hurdle for this research is establishing systematic\nmethods to assess the effectiveness of AI agents prior to\ntheir production deployment (Bogin et al., 2024; Kapoor\net al., 2024). {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Data-Driven Reduced-Order Models for Port-Hamiltonian Systems with\nOperator Inference\nYuwei Genga , Lili Jua , Boris Kramerb , Zhu Wanga,∗\na\n\narXiv:2501.02183v2 [math.NA] 18 Jul 2025\n\nb\n\nDepartment of Mathematics, University of South Carolina, Columbia, SC 29208, USA\nDepartment of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA\n\nAbstract\nHamiltonian operator inference has been developed in [Sharma, H., Wang, Z., Kramer, B., Physica D: Nonlinear Phenomena, 431, p.133122, 2022] to learn structure-preserving reduced-order models (ROMs) for\nHamiltonian systems. The method constructs a low-dimensional model using only data and knowledge of\nthe functional form of the Hamiltonian. The resulting ROMs preserve the intrinsic structure of the system, ensuring that the mechanical and physical properties of the system are maintained. In this work, we\nextend this approach to port-Hamiltonian systems, which generalize Hamiltonian systems by including energy dissipation, external input, and output. Based on snapshots of the system’s state and output, together\nwith the information about the functional form of the Hamiltonian, reduced operators are inferred through\noptimization and are then used to construct data-driven ROMs. To further alleviate the complexity of evaluating nonlinear terms in the ROMs, a hyper-reduction method via discrete empirical interpolation is applied.\nAccordingly, we derive error estimates for the ROM approximations of the state and output. Finally, we\ndemonstrate the structure preservation, as well as the accuracy of the proposed port-Hamiltonian operator\ninference framework, through numerical experiments on a linear mass-spring-damper problem and a nonlinear Toda lattice problem.\nKeywords: port-Hamiltonian system, operator inference, model order reduction, data-driven modeling\n\n1. Introduction\nA port-Hamiltonian (pH) system [1] provides a mathematical framework for modeling and controlling\na wide range of physical systems, with applications in fields such as electrical circuits, thermodynamics,\nchemical processes, and mechanical engineering [2]. Unlike a traditional Hamiltonian system, which describes the evolution of a physical system in time based on energy conservation, a pH system incorporates\nenergy dissipation and external inputs and outputs through ports. We consider a finite-dimensional pH\nsystem of the form\n.\n\n(1a)\n\nx(t) = (J − R)∇x H(x(t)) + Bu(t),\n\n\ny(t) = B⊺ ∇ H(x(t)),\n(1b)\nx\n∗\n\nCorresponding author\nEmail addresses: ygeng@email.sc.edu (Yuwei Geng), ju@math.sc.edu (Lili Ju), bmkramer@ucsd.edu (Boris\nKramer), wangzhu@math.sc.edu (Zhu Wang)\n\nPreprint submitted to Elsevier\n\nJuly 21, 2025\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Macroscopic Quantum States and Universal Correlations\nin a Disorder-Order Interface Propagating over a 1D Ground State\nVanja Marić, Florent Ferro, and Maurizio Fagotti\n\narXiv:2410.10645v3 [cond-mat.stat-mech] 7 Jul 2025\n\nUniversité Paris-Saclay, CNRS, LPTMS, 91405, Orsay, France\n(Dated: July 8, 2025)\nWe consider translationally invariant quantum spin- 21 chains with local interactions and a discrete\nsymmetry that is spontaneously broken at zero temperature. We envision experimenters switching\noff the couplings between two parts of the system and preparing them in independent equilibrium\nstates. One side of the chain is prepared in a disordered phase, and the other in a symmetrybreaking ground state. When the couplings are switched back on, time evolution ensues. We\nargue that in integrable systems the front separating the ordered region recedes at the maximal\nvelocity of quasiparticle excitations over the ground state. We infer that, generically, the order\nparameters should vary on a subdiffusive scale of order t1/3 , where t is time, and their fluctuations\nshould exhibit the same scaling. This interfacial region exhibits full range correlations, indicating\nthat it cannot be decomposed into nearly uncorrelated subsystems. Using the transverse-field Ising\nchain as a case study, we demonstrate that all order parameters follow the same universal scaling\nfunctions. Through an analysis of the skew information, we uncover that the breakdown of cluster\ndecomposition has a quantum contribution: each subsystem within the interfacial region, with extent\ncomparable to the region, exists in a macroscopic quantum state.\n\nLocal relaxation is a key concept in the study of isolated quantum many-body systems prepared out of equilibrium [1, 2]. One of the key aspects of their late-time description is indeed that, eventually, small parts of the system behave as if they were in thermal equilibrium, which\nis often explained invoking the eigenstate thermalization\nhypothesis [3–6]. Exception to this local form of thermalization are well known in 1D, where integrable and\nquasi-integrable systems stand out for their peculiarities,\nobserved even in experiments [7–10]. In conventional situations also integrable systems exhibit local relaxation,\nbut the expectation values of local observables are not\ncaptured by Gibbs ensembles whereas by so-called generalised Gibbs ensembles (GGE) [11–14], which account\nfor the presence of infinitely many integrals of motion.\nIn integrable systems with inhomogeneities, local relaxation was shown in a multitude of works, culminating in\nthe theory of generalized hydrodynamics (GHD) [15–22],\nwhose deviations from standard hydrodynamic predictions have been detected also in experiments [23–25].\nNote that, since (generalised) Gibbs ensembles are\ncharacterised by a nonzero thermodynamic entropy and,\nin 1D, entropy disrupts order very effectively, every global\nsymmetry common to all relevant integrals of motions is\nexpected to be locally restored [14, 26]. This is supposed\nto happen everywhere, even beyond the nonequilibrium\nsteady state (NESS) emerging in the genuine infinite time\nlimit; for instance, in bipartitioning protocols it occurs in\nthe Euler scaling limit in which the position of the subsystem is regarded as a time-dependent variable.\nWe remark, however, that referring to the emergent\nstationary states capturing the late-time local properties\nas to states of (generalised) equilibrium might be misleading: stationarity is not sufficient to characterise an\nequilibrium state [27], which is also supposed to be sta-\n\nble under small perturbations, as well as to exhibit other\nsubtle properties that manifest themselves, e.g., in the\nweak clustering of correlations (see also Ref. [28] in the\npresence of time reversal symmetry). In this regard, we\nmention Ref. [29], which pointed out a form of instability of the NESS emerging after joining two thermal\nreservoirs to a finite subsystem.\nIn this work, we examine the cluster decomposition\nproperties of the stationary states emerging in late-time\ndescriptions. In an equilibrium state of a local system,\nexperiments conducted at large, space-like separations\ndo not influence each other. In the nonequilibrium time\nevolution of an inhomogeneous, isolated system—where\nrelaxation is a local phenomenon and subsystems are\nthe focus—it would then be reasonable to expect such\na separation not to exceed the maximum size of the subsystem that can be described by a stationary state. Is\nthat always the case? Remarkably, we show that even\nsuch a weak requirement is not always satisfied. Fluctuations and, more generally, the full counting statistics of\noperators in subsystems of nonequilibrium systems has\nattracted a lot of attention [30–37]. Several unusual behaviours have been uncovered, including algebraically decaying correlations [38–43] and anomalous fluctuations\n[44–47]. In those cases it is still not excluded that local stationary states could be understood as equilibrium\nstates of effective long-range models (see also Ref. [48]).\nInstead, we are investigating whether connected correlations might not decay at all in the effective stationary\nstates. One scenario that could lead to this is when time\nevolution fails to smooth out the discontinuity induced by\na bipartite preparation of the system, because there are\nno clustering stationary states that can bridge the properties of the two sides. In such cases, we could expect\nan unusually rapid crossover between areas with signif-\n\n\f2\n\n⟨O⟩\n\n⟨O⟩gs\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Submitted to Biometrika (2025), xx, x, p. 1\nPrinted in Great Britain\n\narXiv:2501.01657v2 [stat.ME] 26 Aug 2025\n\nChange point detection for random objects with periodic\nbehaviour\nBy JIAZHEN XU\nResearch School of Finance, Actuarial Studies and Statistics, Australian National University,\nCanberra ACT 2600, Australia\njiazhen.xu@anu.edu.au\nANDREW T.A. WOOD\nResearch School of Finance, Actuarial Studies and Statistics, Australian National University,\nCanberra ACT 2600, Australia\nandrew.wood@anu.edu.au\nTAO ZOU\nResearch School of Finance, Actuarial Studies and Statistics, Australian National University,\nCanberra ACT 2600, Australia\ntao.zou@anu.edu.au\nSummary\nTime-varying random objects have been increasingly encountered in modern data analysis.\nMoreover, in a substantial number of these applications, periodic behaviour of the random objects\nhas been observed. We develop a novel procedure to identify and localize abrupt changes in the\ndistribution of non-Euclidean random objects with periodic behaviour. The proposed procedure\nis flexible and broadly applicable, accommodating a variety of suitable change point detectors for\nrandom objects. We further construct a specific detector used in the proposed procedure which\nis nonparametric and effectively captures the entire distribution of these random objects. The\ntheoretical results cover the limiting distribution of the detector under the null hypothesis of\nno change point, the power of the test in the presence of change points under local alternatives\nand the consistency in estimating the number and locations of change points, whether dealing\nwith a single change point or multiple ones. We demonstrate that the most competitive method\ncurrently in the literature for change point detection in random objects is degraded by periodic\nbehaviour, as periodicity leads to blurring of the changes that this procedure aims to discover.\nThrough comprehensive simulation studies, we demonstrate the superior power and accuracy of\nour approach in both detecting change points and pinpointing their locations. Our main application\nis to weighted networks, represented through graph Laplacians. The proposed method delivers\nhighly interpretable results, as evidenced by the identification of meaningful change points in the\nNew York City Citi Bike sharing system that align with significant historical events.\nSome key words: Metric distribution function; Multiple change point detection; Non-Euclidean data; Nonparametric\ntest; Transportation networks; 𝑈-statistics.\n\n⃝\nC 2021 Biometrika Trust\n\n\f2\n\nJIAZHEN Xu et al.\n\n1. Introduction\nChange point analysis has a rich history with applications in diverse areas such as quality\ncontrol (Shewhart and Deming 1986; Lai 1995; Hawkins et al. 2003), biology (Olshen et al.\n2004; Erdman and Emerson 2008; Fearnhead and Rigaill 2019; Jiang et al. 2023), stock market\nanalysis (Andreou and Ghysels 2002; Bai and Perron 2003; Fryzlewicz 2014), climate and\nenvironmental studies (Rodionov 2004; Reeves et al. 2007; Beaulieu and Killick 2018) and social\nsciences (Kossinets and Watts 2006; Wang et al. 2017), to name but a few. The core aim of change\npoint analysis is the identification and precise localization of abrupt changes in the distribution\nof observations that are ordered in some way, usually by time.\nTraditionally, change point detection techniques have been developed for Euclidean data; see\ne.g., Basseville et al. (1993), Aminikhanghahi and Cook (2017) and Truong et al. (2020). However, data that do not reside in Euclidean spaces are becoming increasingly common. Such\nnon-Euclidean observations, often referred to as random objects (Marron and Dryden 2021),\nare neither scalars nor vectors, but instead take values in a general metric space which will\noften have non-Euclidean structure. Important examples include transportation networks, compositional data, functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging, phylogenetic trees; see e.g., Worsley\net al. (2002), Holmes (2003), Kolar et al. (2010) and Scealy and Wood (2023). The absence of\nvector operations such as addition, scalar multiplication, or inner products in such spaces poses\nsignificant challenges in the statistical analysis.\n\nFig. 1: The hourly number of trips between bike stations in the New York City Citi Bike sharing system for 15 weekdays\nin November 2019.\n\nTo tackle the issue of non-Euclidean structure, numerous change point detection techniques\nhave been developed for random objects. Some methods focus on detecting mean changes at a\nspecific instant, such as those proposed in Chen and Zhang (2015), Chen and Friedman (2017),\nDubey and Müller (2020), and Jiang et al. (2024), while others target distributional changes;\nsee, for example, Dubey and Zheng (2023), Kanrar et al. (2024), and Zhang et al. (2025).\nHowever, most existing approaches assume that, under the null hypothesis of no change point,\nthe observations are identically distributed. This assumption often does not hold in real-world\nscenarios, e.g., when periodic effects are present. For example, dynamic transportation networks\nobserved on an hourly basis are influenced by rush hour patterns. Figure 1 shows the hourly\nnumber of trips between bike stations in the New York City Citi Bike sharing system. Figure\n1 indicates that these networks exhibit periodic behaviour, with peaks of network sizes at 8 am\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

A. Millar\nSt. George and Sutherland Clinical School\nUniversity of New South Wales\nNSW 2217, Sydney, Australia\nDepartment of Anatomical Pathology\nNSW Health Pathology, St. George Hospital\nKogarah, NSW 2217, Australia\n\nPeter H. Graham, Lois Browne\nSt. George and Sutherland Clinical School\nUniversity of New South Wales\nNSW 2217, Sydney, Australia\nCancer Care Centre, St George Hospital\nKogarah, NSW 2217, Australia\nArcot Sowmya, Erik Meijering\nSchool of Computer Science and Engineering\nUniversity of New South Wales\nSydney, NSW 2052, Australia\n{a.sowmya, erik.meijering}@unsw.edu.au\n\nA BSTRACT\nAbstract\nExplainable AI (XAI) in medical histopathology is essential for enhancing the interpretability and\nclinical trustworthiness of deep learning models in cancer diagnosis. However, the black-box\nnature of these models often limits their clinical adoption. We introduce GRAPHITE (Graph-based\nInterpretable Tissue Examination), a post-hoc explainable framework designed for breast cancer\ntissue microarray (TMA) analysis. GRAPHITE employs a multiscale approach, extracting patches\nat various magnification levels, constructing an hierarchical graph, and utilising graph attention\nnetworks (GAT) with scalewise attention (SAN) to capture scale-dependent features. We trained the\nmodel on 140 tumour TMA cores and four benign whole slide images from which 140 benign samples\nwere created, and tested it on 53 pathologist-annotated TMA samples. GRAPHITE outperformed\ntraditional XAI methods, achieving a mean average precision (mAP) of 0.56, an area under the\nreceiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.94, and a threshold robustness (ThR) of 0.70,\nindicating that the model maintains high performance across a wide range of thresholds. In clinical\nutility, GRAPHITE achieved the highest area under the decision curve (AUDC) of 4.17e+5, indicating\nreliable decision support across thresholds. These results highlight GRAPHITE’s potential as a\nclinically valuable tool in computational pathology, providing interpretable visualisations that align\nwith the pathologists’ diagnostic reasoning and support precision medicine.\n\n\fKeywords Breast cancer, whole slide images, tissue microarrays, digital pathology, computaional pathology, deep\nneural network, explainable artificial intelligence.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nDigital pathology has revolutionised diagnostic medicine by enabling the digitisation of histopathology slides, facilitating\nhigh-throughput analysis [1, 2]. The availability of large-scale image datasets and advances in computational power\nhave catalysed the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) with cancer diagnosis [3, 4]. Deep learning models, in\nparticular, have shown exceptional promise in analysing complex tissue structures and offer automated predictions\nwith high accuracy [5, 6, 7, 8]. Cancer diagnosis, especially for breast cancer, has benefitted significantly from these\ntechnologies, which provide robust decision support in areas such as tumour detection, grading and subtype classification\n[9, 10, 11, 12, 13]. These applications of AI in digital pathology not only streamline workflows but also help mitigate\nchallenges posed by the increasing volume of pathology cases, particularly in oncology.\nDespite advances in AI for digital pathology, challenges remain in the interpretability of deep learning models [14].\nThese models often operate as “black boxes,” making it difficult for pathologists to understand the rationale behind\nspecific predictions. This lack of transparency can hinder the clinical adoption of AI tools, as pathologists may\nbe reluctant to trust a system that does not provide clear explanations for its decisions [15, 16, 17]. In addition,\nthe interpretability of AI models is crucial in cancer diagnosis, where erroneous interpretations have significant\nconsequences for patient care. The challenge of interpretability is compounded by the complexity of histopathology\nimages, which contain intricate patterns that may not be easily discernible even to experienced pathologists. Specifically,\nin breast cancer tissue microarrays (TMAs), tissue heterogeneity further complicates interpretation, requiring models to\nnot only make accurate predictions but also provide meaningful explanations.\nExplainable AI (XAI) has emerged as a key area of research addressing these challenges by enhancing the transparency\nof deep learning models. XAI methods provide visual or textual explanations that align with clinical reasoning, thereby\nbuilding trust among pathologists and clinicians [18, 19, 20, 21, 22]. This alignment is essential for fostering confidence\nin AI-assisted diagnoses and ensuring that pathologists can effectively integrate these tools into their workflows.\nIn cancer diagnosis, this translates to models generating visual explanations that highlight diagnostically relevant\nregions within tissue samples, allowing pathologists to validate the AI’s findings. Furthermore, XAI can facilitate the\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

In this review, we present a comprehensive analysis of tensor decomposition methods such as Tucker, CANDECOMP/PARAFAC, spiked tensor decomposition, etc. and their diverse\napplications across biomedical domains such as imaging, multi-omics, and spatial transcriptomics. To\nsystematically investigate the literature, we applied a topic modeling-based approach that identifies and\ngroups distinct thematic sub-areas in biomedicine where tensor decomposition has been used, thereby\nrevealing key trends and research directions. We evaluated challenges related to the scalability of latent\nspaces along with obtaining the optimal rank of the tensor, which often hinder the extraction of meaningful features from increasingly large and complex datasets. Additionally, we discuss recent advances\nin quantum algorithms for tensor decomposition, exploring how quantum computing can be leveraged\nto address these challenges. Our study includes a preliminary resource estimation analysis for quantum\ncomputing platforms and examines the feasibility of implementing quantum-enhanced tensor decomposition methods on near-term quantum devices. Collectively, this review not only synthesizes current\napplications and challenges of tensor decomposition in biomedical analyses but also outlines promising quantum computing strategies to enhance its impact on deriving actionable insights from complex\nbiomedical data.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nBiomedical data encompasses a diverse set of multi-modal and multi-omics data from healthcare and life\nsciences providing critical insights into the complexity of disease etiology and biological functions. A compre∗ Correspondence: a.bose@ibm.com\n\n1\n\n\fhensive understanding of biomedical data is required to advance precision medicine and treatment strategies\nfor complex diseases. Innovations in technologies like genomics, proteomics, medical imaging, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), etc. have resulted in high-quality data that\nenable personalized clinical decision making. This opportunity also presents us with significant challenges\nin data management and analysis [1], such as representing the different modalities of biomedical data and\nfinding higher-order relationships between them. With the increasing size and complexity of biomedical data,\ntensors are emerging as a powerful method for the integration and analysis of data [2–9].\nTensors are multidimensional arrays rendered as generalizations of vector spaces [10]. A vector is a\nfirst-order tensor, a matrix is a second-order tensor, and tensors of order three or higher are called higherorder tensors. Tensor decompositions, although originally proposed about a century ago [11], have found\nrenewed interest in several applications in signal processing, machine learning [12], data mining [13], computer vision [14], neuroscience [15], etc. in the past two decades, with the ability to represent complex and\nmultimodal data. Tensors have the ability to represent each biomedical modality in a different dimension.\nHence, they can be used to integrate multi-modal biomedical data spanning multi-omics variables. Tensor factorization or decomposition allows us to obtain a latent structure from a higher-dimensional space\nspanned by all the multi-modal variables and identify interactions between them [3, 10]. Tensor decomposition (TD) has widespread application in biomedical data analysis ranging from analyzing MRI images and\nelectroencephalography (EEG) signals [16], finding genotype-phenotype relationships [17], quantifying expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) [18], integrating multi-omics data [5], to spatial transcriptomics [19].\nThere have been attempts to review the applications of TD in biomedical data but they were focused on\nspecific applications such as imaging analysis [16], precision medicine [3] or, on specific diseases such as\ncancer [20], and often limited in scope.\nThis review examines recent advances in tensor decomposition for biomedical data analysis. We review its\napplication across diverse biomedical domains over the past decade, highlighting state-of-the-art practices,\nthe inherent advantages of these methods, and the scalability challenges that remain. Moreover, we discuss\na unique perspective of adapting emerging technologies such as quantum computing (QC) and strategies for\ndeveloping quantum algorithms for TD to ameliorate the challenges arising in classical TD applications.
