{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"1\n\nD2D Coded Caching Schemes for Multiaccess\nNetworks with Combinatorial Access Topology\n\narXiv:2501.10756v2 [cs.IT] 19 Jan 2026\n\nRashid Ummer N.T. and B. Sundar Rajan\nDepartment of Electrical Communication Engineering, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, India\nE-mail: {rashidummer, bsrajan}@iisc.ac.in\n\nAbstract—Device-to-device (D2D) communication is one of the\nmost promising techniques for next-generation wireless Internet\nof Things networks. This paper considers coded caching in a\nwireless multiaccess D2D network, where users communicate\nwith each other and can access multiple cache nodes. Access\ntopologies derived from two combinatorial designs known as\nthe t-design and t-group divisible design (t-GDD), referred to\nas the t-design and t-GDD topologies, respectively, have been\nstudied recently for the multiaccess coded caching (MACC)\nnetwork. These access topologies, which subsume the other\nknown topologies except the cyclic wrap-around topology, are\nextended to a multiaccess D2D coded caching (MADCC) network.\nNovel MADCC schemes are proposed, and schemes are also\nderived from existing MACC schemes with t-design and t-GDD\ntopologies. To compare different MADCC schemes, the metrics\nof load per user and subpacketization level are used while\nkeeping the number of caches, cache size, and access degree\nthe same. A comparison of the proposed schemes with those\nderived from existing MACC schemes, as well as with the existing\nMADCC scheme under a cyclic wrap-around topology, shows\nthat the proposed schemes have an advantage in either load per\nuser or subpacketization level or both. Additionally, several low\nsubpacketization level coded caching schemes are obtained for\nthe original D2D network with dedicated caches.\nIndex Terms—D2D coded caching, placement delivery array,\nmultiaccess networks, t-design, t-group divisible design.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nDevice-to-device (D2D) communication is one of the most\npromising techniques for next-generation wireless Internet of\nThings (IoT) networks [1], [2]. D2D communication also\nserves as a key enabler for the realization of IoT in future\nﬁfth-generation and beyond wireless cellular communication\nsystems [3], [4]. Many emerging IoT applications require very\nlow latency, and edge caching in IoT networks has been\nidentiﬁed as a promising solution to decrease latency [5].\nStudying cache-aided D2D networks is therefore of great\nrelevance to IoT networks. In [6], a cache-enabled mobile\nmulti-D2D network has been studied. Motivated by the coded\ncaching technique introduced by Maddah-Ali and Niesen in\n[7] for a broadcast network (referred to as the MAN scheme),\ncoded caching in a wireless D2D network was ﬁrst studied by\nJi, Caire, and Molisch in [8] (referred to as the JCM scheme).\nA D2D network consists of K users, each equipped with a\ncache of size M ﬁles, and a central server with a library of\nN ﬁles.\n0 A part of the content has been presented at the IEEE ISIT 2025 held in\nAnn Arbor (Michigan), USA, June 2025.\n\nA D2D coded caching operates in two phases. First, in the\nplacement phase, the central server places data in all user\ncaches. Then, in the delivery phase, where the central server\nis not present, all user demands are served through inter-user\ncoded multicast transmissions. The transmission load of the\nJCM scheme is shown to be order optimal in [8] and [9]. The\nJCM scheme requires dividing ﬁles into F packets (referred to\nas the subpacketization level), which grows exponentially with\nthe number of users for a given memory ratio M\nN . Motivated by\nthe concept of Placement Delivery Array (PDA) introduced by\nYan et al. in [10], Wang et al. in [11] proposed an array called\nD2D Placement Delivery Array (DPDA) which characterizes\nboth the phases of a D2D coded caching scheme with uncoded\nplacement and one-shot delivery. A procedure to obtain a\nDPDA from a given regular PDA has been discussed in [11].\nDirect construction of a few classes of DPDAs is discussed in\n[12] and [13].\nCaching at the wireless edge nodes is a widely used\ntechnique in communication systems and IoT applications.\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

For\nnearly a century, the Peierls transition has been understood within the paradigm of electronelectron interactions mediated by longitudinal acoustic phonons. This classical picture has\nrecently been revised in topological semimetals, where transverse acoustic phonons can\ncouple with conducting p-orbital electrons and give rise to an unconventional Fermi surface\ninstability, dubbed the transverse Peierls transition (TPT). Most interestingly, the TPT\ninduced lattice distortions can further break rotation or mirror/inversion symmetries,\nleading to nematic or chiral charge density waves (CDWs). Quantum materials that host the\nTPT, however, have not been experimentally established. Here, we report the experimental\ndiscovery of an incommensurate TPT in the tetragonal Dirac semimetal EuAl4. Using\n\n\finelastic x-ray scattering with meV resolution, we observe the complete softening of a\ntransverse acoustic phonon at the CDW wavevector upon cooling, whereas the longitudinal\nacoustic phonon is nearly unchanged. Combining with first principles calculations, we show\nthat the incommensurate CDW wavevector matches the calculated charge susceptibility peak\nand connects the nested Dirac bands with Al 3px and 3py orbitals. Supplemented by second\nharmonic generation measurements, we show that the CDW induced lattice distortions break\nall vertical and diagonal mirrors whereas the four-fold rotational symmetry is retained below\nthe CDW transition. Our observations strongly suggest a chiral CDW in EuAl4 and highlight\nthe TPT as a new avenue for chiral quantum states.\nThe charge density wave (CDW), a spontaneous translational symmetry breaking in electron\nliquids, plays a central role in many correlated and topological materials, including high-Tc\nsuperconductors1-3, kagome metals4-8, axion insulators9,10, and quantum Hall liquids11-15.\nHistorically, the first microscopic mechanism of CDW was described by the Fröhlich model16,\nwhere electron-electron scatterings mediated by longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonons are divergent\nat the Fermi surface nesting vector, QCDW. Consequently, the formation of a CDW accompanied\nby softening of LA phonons, known as the Kohn anomaly17, and longitudinal lattice distortions as\nschematically shown in Figs. 1a and 1b. Recently, it was predicted that topological electrons with\nanisotropic p-orbital characters can strongly couple to transverse acoustic (TA) phonons, leading\nto CDW instabilities and TA Kohn anomaly, named the transverse Peierls transition (TPT)13. This\nnew mechanism is shown schematically in Fig. 1c and 1d. Interestingly, since transverse lattice\ndistortions are two-component vectors, TPTs can lead to unconventional CDWs such as nematic\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Data-Driven Reduced-Order Models for Port-Hamiltonian Systems with\nOperator Inference\nYuwei Genga , Lili Jua , Boris Kramerb , Zhu Wanga,∗\na\n\narXiv:2501.02183v2 [math.NA] 18 Jul 2025\n\nb\n\nDepartment of Mathematics, University of South Carolina, Columbia, SC 29208, USA\nDepartment of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA\n\nAbstract\nHamiltonian operator inference has been developed in [Sharma, H., Wang, Z., Kramer, B., Physica D: Nonlinear Phenomena, 431, p.133122, 2022] to learn structure-preserving reduced-order models (ROMs) for\nHamiltonian systems. The method constructs a low-dimensional model using only data and knowledge of\nthe functional form of the Hamiltonian. The resulting ROMs preserve the intrinsic structure of the system, ensuring that the mechanical and physical properties of the system are maintained. In this work, we\nextend this approach to port-Hamiltonian systems, which generalize Hamiltonian systems by including energy dissipation, external input, and output. Based on snapshots of the system’s state and output, together\nwith the information about the functional form of the Hamiltonian, reduced operators are inferred through\noptimization and are then used to construct data-driven ROMs. To further alleviate the complexity of evaluating nonlinear terms in the ROMs, a hyper-reduction method via discrete empirical interpolation is applied.\nAccordingly, we derive error estimates for the ROM approximations of the state and output. Finally, we\ndemonstrate the structure preservation, as well as the accuracy of the proposed port-Hamiltonian operator\ninference framework, through numerical experiments on a linear mass-spring-damper problem and a nonlinear Toda lattice problem.\nKeywords: port-Hamiltonian system, operator inference, model order reduction, data-driven modeling\n\n1. Introduction\nA port-Hamiltonian (pH) system [1] provides a mathematical framework for modeling and controlling\na wide range of physical systems, with applications in fields such as electrical circuits, thermodynamics,\nchemical processes, and mechanical engineering [2]. Unlike a traditional Hamiltonian system, which describes the evolution of a physical system in time based on energy conservation, a pH system incorporates\nenergy dissipation and external inputs and outputs through ports. We consider a finite-dimensional pH\nsystem of the form\n.\n\n(1a)\n\nx(t) = (J − R)∇x H(x(t)) + Bu(t),\n\n\ny(t) = B⊺ ∇ H(x(t)),\n(1b)\nx\n∗\n\nCorresponding author\nEmail addresses: ygeng@email.sc.edu (Yuwei Geng), ju@math.sc.edu (Lili Ju), bmkramer@ucsd.edu (Boris\nKramer), wangzhu@math.sc.edu (Zhu Wang)\n\nPreprint submitted to Elsevier\n\nJuly 21, 2025\n\n\fwith x(0) = x0 , where x(t) ∈ Rn represents the n-dimensional state vector, H(x) is the Hamiltonian function,\nwhich is continuously differentiable and represents the internal energy of the system, the matrix J = −J⊺ ∈\nRn×n is skew-symmetric, describing the interconnection of the system’s energy storage elements; the matrix\nR = R⊺ ∈ Rn×n is symmetric and positive semi-definite, characterizing the energy dissipation in the system,\nB ∈ Rn×m is the port matrix, which describes the modalities through which energy is imported into or\nexported from the system, u(t) ∈ Rm represents the external input vector, and y(t) ∈ Rm is the system’s\noutput vector. Based on the structural properties of J and R, one can conclude that the Hamiltonian function\nsatisfies the dissipation inequality: for any t2 > t1 ≥ 0,\nH(x(t )) − H(x(t )) =\n2\n\nZ t2\n\n1\n\nt1\nZ t2\n\n(∇x H(x(t))⊺ [(J − R)∇x H(x(t) + Bu(t)] dt\n(2)\n⊺\n\n≤\n\ny(t) u(t) dt.\nt1\n\nGiven an initial condition, the high-dimensional dynamical system (1) can be simulated using geometric\nnumerical integration schemes [3]. However, when the system needs to be solved repeatedly, those highdimensional numerical simulations become computationally expensive. Model order reduction (MOR) can\nbe used to design a surrogate model with fewer degrees of freedom to accelerate the simulations. It has been\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

In this review, we present a comprehensive analysis of tensor decomposition methods such as Tucker, CANDECOMP/PARAFAC, spiked tensor decomposition, etc. and their diverse\napplications across biomedical domains such as imaging, multi-omics, and spatial transcriptomics. To\nsystematically investigate the literature, we applied a topic modeling-based approach that identifies and\ngroups distinct thematic sub-areas in biomedicine where tensor decomposition has been used, thereby\nrevealing key trends and research directions. We evaluated challenges related to the scalability of latent\nspaces along with obtaining the optimal rank of the tensor, which often hinder the extraction of meaningful features from increasingly large and complex datasets. Additionally, we discuss recent advances\nin quantum algorithms for tensor decomposition, exploring how quantum computing can be leveraged\nto address these challenges. Our study includes a preliminary resource estimation analysis for quantum\ncomputing platforms and examines the feasibility of implementing quantum-enhanced tensor decomposition methods on near-term quantum devices. Collectively, this review not only synthesizes current\napplications and challenges of tensor decomposition in biomedical analyses but also outlines promising quantum computing strategies to enhance its impact on deriving actionable insights from complex\nbiomedical data.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nBiomedical data encompasses a diverse set of multi-modal and multi-omics data from healthcare and life\nsciences providing critical insights into the complexity of disease etiology and biological functions. A compre∗ Correspondence: a.bose@ibm.com\n\n1\n\n\fhensive understanding of biomedical data is required to advance precision medicine and treatment strategies\nfor complex diseases. Innovations in technologies like genomics, proteomics, medical imaging, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), etc. have resulted in high-quality data that\nenable personalized clinical decision making. This opportunity also presents us with significant challenges\nin data management and analysis [1], such as representing the different modalities of biomedical data and\nfinding higher-order relationships between them. With the increasing size and complexity of biomedical data,\ntensors are emerging as a powerful method for the integration and analysis of data [2–9].\nTensors are multidimensional arrays rendered as generalizations of vector spaces [10]. A vector is a\nfirst-order tensor, a matrix is a second-order tensor, and tensors of order three or higher are called higherorder tensors. Tensor decompositions, although originally proposed about a century ago [11], have found\nrenewed interest in several applications in signal processing, machine learning [12], data mining [13], computer vision [14], neuroscience [15], etc. in the past two decades, with the ability to represent complex and\nmultimodal data. Tensors have the ability to represent each biomedical modality in a different dimension.\nHence, they can be used to integrate multi-modal biomedical data spanning multi-omics variables. Tensor factorization or decomposition allows us to obtain a latent structure from a higher-dimensional space\nspanned by all the multi-modal variables and identify interactions between them [3, 10]. Tensor decomposition (TD) has widespread application in biomedical data analysis ranging from analyzing MRI images and\nelectroencephalography (EEG) signals [16], finding genotype-phenotype relationships [17], quantifying expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) [18], integrating multi-omics data [5], to spatial transcriptomics [19].\nThere have been attempts to review the applications of TD in biomedical data but they were focused on\nspecific applications such as imaging analysis [16], precision medicine [3] or, on specific diseases such as\ncancer [20], and often limited in scope.\nThis review examines recent advances in tensor decomposition for biomedical data analysis. We review its\napplication across diverse biomedical domains over the past decade, highlighting state-of-the-art practices,\nthe inherent advantages of these methods, and the scalability challenges that remain. Moreover, we discuss\na unique perspective of adapting emerging technologies such as quantum computing (QC) and strategies for\ndeveloping quantum algorithms for TD to ameliorate the challenges arising in classical TD applications. QC\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"An Experimental Study on Joint Modeling for\nSound Event Localization and Detection with\nSource Distance Estimation\nYuxuan Dong, Qing Wang∗ , Hengyi Hong, Ya Jiang, Shi Cheng\n\narXiv:2501.10755v1 [cs.SD] 18 Jan 2025\n\nNERC-SLIP, University of Science and Technology of China (USTC), Hefei, China\n{yxdong0320, hyhong, yajiang, chengshi}@mail.ustc.edu.cn, qingwang2@ustc.edu.cn\nAbstract—In traditional sound event localization and detection\n(SELD) tasks, the focus is typically on sound event detection\n(SED) and direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, but they fall\nshort of providing full spatial information about the sound\nsource. The 3D SELD task addresses this limitation by integrating source distance estimation (SDE), allowing for complete\nspatial localization. We propose three approaches to tackle this\nchallenge: a novel method with independent training and joint\nprediction, which firstly treats DOA and distance estimation as\nseparate tasks and then combines them to solve 3D SELD; a dualbranch representation with source Cartesian coordinate used for\nsimultaneous DOA and distance estimation; and a three-branch\nstructure that jointly models SED, DOA, and SDE within a\nunified framework. Our proposed method ranked first in the\nDCASE 2024 Challenge Task 3, demonstrating the effectiveness\nof joint modeling for addressing the 3D SELD task. The relevant\ncode for this paper will be open-sourced in the future.\nIndex Terms—DCASE, sound event localization and detection,\nsource distance estimation, Ambisonics\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nSound event localization and detection (SELD) aims to\ndetect the temporal activity of sound events and track their\nspatial locations using multichannel audio inputs. The spatiotemporal data provided by SELD systems is useful for various\nmachine awareness applications, including smart homes and\naudio surveillance [1], [2].\n3D SELD consists of three subtasks [3]: sound event detection (SED), sound source localization (SSL), and source\ndistance estimation (SDE). SED uses classification methods\nsuch as Gaussian mixture models (GMM) [4], hidden Markov\nmodels (HMM) [5], and recurrent neural networks (RNN) [6]\nto identify the time occurrence of sound events. SSL estimates\nthe direction-of-arrival (DOA) using both traditional [7], [8]\nand deep learning-based methods [9]. Research on SDE has\nmainly focused on binaural formats, though [10] expanded this\nby exploring loss functions and integrating activity detection\nwith a tetrahedral microphone array for distance estimation.\nDevelopment of complex scene analysis systems has driven\nthe emergence of multi-task learning methods comparing to\nsingle-task models. In [11], a CRNN model with dual-branch\noutput was proposed, jointly modeling the SED and DOA\ntasks. Later, activity-coupled Cartesian DOA (ACCDOA) [12]\nand multi-ACCDOA [13] representations improved the model\n∗ Corresponding author\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

PREPRINT VERSION. ACCEPTED FEBRUARY, 2025\n\n1\n\nDR-MPC: Deep Residual Model Predictive Control\nfor Real-world Social Navigation\n\narXiv:2410.10646v2 [cs.RO] 14 Feb 2025\n\nJames R. Han1 , Hugues Thomas2 , Jian Zhang2 , Nicholas Rhinehart1 , Timothy D. Barfoot1\nAbstract—How can a robot safely navigate around people with\ncomplex motion patterns? Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)\nin simulation holds some promise, but much prior work relies on\nsimulators that fail to capture the nuances of real human motion.\nThus, we propose Deep Residual Model Predictive Control (DRMPC) to enable robots to quickly and safely perform DRL from\nreal-world crowd navigation data. By blending MPC with modelfree DRL, DR-MPC overcomes the DRL challenges of large data\nrequirements and unsafe initial behavior. DR-MPC is initialized\nwith MPC-based path tracking, and gradually learns to interact\nmore effectively with humans. To further accelerate learning, a\nsafety component estimates out-of-distribution states to guide the\nrobot away from likely collisions. In simulation, we show that DRMPC substantially outperforms prior work, including traditional\nDRL and residual DRL models. Hardware experiments show\nour approach successfully enables a robot to navigate a variety\nof crowded situations with few errors using less than 4 hours\nof training data (video: https://youtu.be/GUZlGBk60uY, code:\nhttps://github.com/James-R-Han/DR-MPC).\nIndex Terms—Social HRI, Reinforcement Learning, Model\nPredictive Control, Real-world Robotics, Autonomous Agents.\n\nI. I NTRODUCTION\nCHIEVING reliable robotic navigation remains one of\nthe main challenges in integrating mobile robots into\nsociety. Applications such as food service and equipment\ntransport could see major cost and time savings but require\nrobots to navigate human environments with static obstacles.\nSocial robot navigation research focuses on enabling robots\nto move safely and efficiently around humans. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has emerged as a promising alternative to traditional learning-free approaches including reactive,\ndecoupled, and coupled planning. Reactive methods model\nhumans as static objects [1], decoupled planning approaches\nforecast future human trajectories to generate a cost map for\nnavigation [2], and coupled planning approaches model human\nmotion and solve a joint optimization problem [3]. These\nmethods have notable shortcomings. Reactive approaches are\nshortsighted, resulting in intrusive behaviour [2]. Decoupled\napproaches neglect the robot’s impact on humans, which can\ncause the robot to ‘freeze’ when the robot’s plan conflicts with\nforecasted human trajectories [4]. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Median eigenvalues of subcubic graphs\n\narXiv:2502.13139v2 [math.CO] 17 Apr 2025\n\nHricha Acharya∗\n\nBenjamin Jeter∗\n\nZilin Jiang∗,†\n\nAbstract\nWe show that the median eigenvalues of every connected graph of maximum degree at most\nthree, except for the Heawood graph, are at most 1 in absolute value, resolving open problems\nposed by Fowler and Pisanski, and by Mohar.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nA graph is subcubic if its maximum degree is at most 3. In mathematical chemistry, connected\nsubcubic graphs are often referred to as chemical graphs because they naturally model the skeletons\nof organic molecules, where atoms such as carbon typically form bonds with up to three neighboring\natoms.\nThe study of chemical graphs has significant applications in theoretical chemistry, particularly\nin the analysis of molecular orbital models. For instance, in the Hückel molecular orbital model,\nthe eigenvalues of a molecule’s skeleton graph correspond to its π-electron energy levels. Among\nthese, the median eigenvalues — associated with the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)\nand the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) — are of particular interest (see [6, 12] and\nthe references therein).\nTo formalize, let G be an n-vertex graph, and let λ1 (G) ≥ λ2 (G) ≥ · · · ≥ λn (G) denote the\neigenvalues of its adjacency matrix. The median eigenvalues are defined as λh and λl , where\nh = ⌊(n + 1)/2⌋ and l = ⌈(n + 1)/2⌉.\nComputational studies of Fowler and Pisanski [2, 3] suggest that the median eigenvalues of most\nchemical graphs lie in the interval [−1, 1]. However, there is a single notable exception: the Heawood\n√\ngraph, whose median eigenvalues are ± 2 (see Figure 1).\nFowler and Pisanski [2] conjectured that the median eigenvalues of all but finitely many chemical\ngraphs lie in [−1, 1], and they confirmed their conjecture for all chemical trees. The interval [−1, 1]\nwould be optimal because of the construction of an infinite family of bipartite chemical graphs whose\nmedian eigenvalues are ±1 by Guo and Mohar [4].\n∗\n\nSchool of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ 85281, USA. Kasowski1 , Apurv Varshney2 , Roksana Sadeghi2 ,\nand Michael Beyeler2,3\n1\n\nInterdepartmental Graduate Program in Dynamical Neuroscience, University of\nCalifornia, Santa Barbara, CA, 93106\n2\nDepartment of Computer Science, University of California, Santa Barbara, CA,\n93106\n3\nDepartment of Psychological & Brain Sciences, University of California, Santa\nBarbara, CA, 93106\nE-mail: mbeyeler@ucsb.edu\nAbstract. Objective: Spatial scheduling of electrode activation (“rastering”) is\nessential for safely operating high-density retinal implants, yet its perceptual\nconsequences remain poorly understood. This study systematically evaluates the\nimpact of raster patterns, or spatial arrangements of sequential electrode activation,\non performance and perceived difficulty in simulated prosthetic vision (SPV). By\naddressing this gap, we aimed to identify patterns that optimize functional vision\nin retinal implants.\nApproach:\nSighted participants completed letter recognition and motion\ndiscrimination tasks under four raster patterns (horizontal, vertical, checkerboard, and\nrandom) using an immersive SPV system. The simulations emulated epiretinal implant\nperception and employed psychophysically validated models of electrode activation,\nphosphene appearance, nonlinear spatial summation, and temporal dynamics, ensuring\nrealistic representation of prosthetic vision. Performance accuracy and self-reported\ndifficulty were analyzed to assess the effects of raster patterning.\nMain Results: The checkerboard pattern consistently outperformed other raster\npatterns, yielding significantly higher accuracy and lower difficulty ratings across\nboth tasks. The horizontal and vertical patterns introduced biases aligned with\napparent motion artifacts, while the checkerboard minimized such effects. Random\npatterns resulted in the lowest performance, underscoring the importance of structured\nactivation. Notably, checkerboard matched performance in the “No Raster” condition,\ndespite conforming to groupwise safety constraints.\nSignificance: This is the first quantitative, task-based evaluation of raster patterns\nin SPV. Checkerboard-style scheduling enhances perceptual clarity without increasing\ncomputational load, offering a low-overhead, clinically relevant strategy for improving\nusability in next-generation retinal prostheses.\n\nKeywords: prosthetic vision, raster patterns, letter recognition, motion discrimination,\nvirtual reality, eye tracking, retinal prostheses\n\n\fSimulated prosthetic vision confirms effective raster patterns\n\n2\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

They also label the response that the\nLLM should generate based on the system’s output.\nThe annotation for Plan+Retrieval+Call is similar to that of Retrieval+Call, with the difference\nbeing that annotators don’t decompose the complex\nquery into simpler ones. They are required to annotate a sequential chain of API calls and the response\nderived from the execution of the last API.\nThe introduction of APIs increases the difficulty\nof annotation. Despite recruiting computer science\nstudents for dialogue annotation, each dialogue requires discussion between two annotators to decide\nhow to annotate. Additionally, two additional annotators are involved to ensure the quality of annotations, including the format, logical consistency, and\nreasonability of API calls. The average annotation\ncost per dialogue is $8. Out of the 400 dialogues\nannotated, we discard 21.5% due to various annotation issues. Ultimately, we retained 314 dialogues\nwith a total of 753 API Calls.\n3.3\n--\ntool-augmented LLMs. However, it is challenging\nto construct a large-scale training dataset at a low\ncost while meeting the our design principles of domain diversity and API authenticity. The manual\nannotation cost for each dialogue in our evaluation\nset reaches $8, making it expensive to create a large\ndataset. Furthermore, it is challenging for the annotators to design a diverse and authentic API pool.\n--\n\nThe emergence of LLMs has brought about a\nparadigm shift in data annotation, offering an alternative to human annotators for the automated\ngeneration of labeled data. One representative approach is self-instruct (Wang et al., 2022). {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"Abstract\nExisting benchmarks do not test language agents on their interaction with human\nusers or ability to follow domain-specific rules, both of which are vital for deploying\nthem in real world applications. We propose τ -bench, a benchmark emulating\ndynamic conversations between a user (simulated by language models) and a\nlanguage agent provided with domain-specific API tools and policy guidelines.\nWe employ an efficient and faithful evaluation process that compares the database\nstate at the end of a conversation with the annotated goal state. We also propose\na new metric (pass^k) to evaluate the reliability of agent behavior over multiple\ntrials. Our experiments show that even state-of-the-art function calling agents (like\ngpt-4o) succeed on < 50% of the tasks, and are quite inconsistent (pass^8 < 25%\nin retail). Our findings point to the need for methods that can improve the ability\nof agents to act consistently and follow rules reliably.\n--\nrequires several key desiderata to be satisfied. Agents must (1) interact seamlessly with both humans\nand programmatic APIs over long horizons to incrementally gather information and resolve intents,\n(2) accurately adhere to complex policies and rules specific to a task or domain, and (3) maintain\nconsistency and reliability at scale, across millions of interactions. For instance, consider the case of\nan airline booking agent (Figure 1). When a user wants to change their flight reservation to a different\n--\nModeling realistic human interaction and rule following in agent evaluation is vital for developing\nand deploying trustworthy agents in the wild, and tackling challenges like long-context reasoning\nand planning in a methodical fashion. Existing benchmarks [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] for language agents\noften feature simplified instruction-following setups, where the agent autonomously interacts with an\nenvironment (web, code terminal, or APIs) given all the information upfront, without any human-inthe-loop interaction and without the need to consult any domain-specific guidelines.\nIn this work, we introduce τ -bench (short for Tool-Agent-User Interaction Benchmark) to measure\nan agent’s ability to interact with (simulated) human users and programmatic APIs while following\ndomain-specific policies in a consistent manner. τ -bench is built in a modular framework with\n(1) realistic databases and APIs, (2) domain-specific policy documents, and (3) instructions for\n∗\n\n--\n\nFigure 1: (a) In τ -bench, an agent interacts with database API tools and an LM-simulated user to\ncomplete tasks. The benchmark tests an agent’s ability to collate and convey all required information\nfrom/to users through multiple interactions, and solve complex issues on the fly while ensuring it\nfollows guidelines laid out in a domain-specific policy document. (b) An example trajectory in\nτ -airline, where an agent needs to reject the user request (change a basic economy flight) following\ndomain policies and propose a new solution (cancel and rebook). This challenges the agent in\nlong-context zero-shot reasoning over complex databases, rules, and user intents.\ndiverse user scenarios and corresponding ground truth annotations. As a first demonstration, we\nfocus on the realm of customer service and create two different domains where agents need to assist\nsimulated users with diverse requests (τ -retail and τ -airline). We leverage the generative capabilities\nof language models (LMs) for data creation and realistic human user simulation [15] in conjunction\nwith manual annotation and verification.\nWe constructed τ -bench in three stages, including manual schema and API design, LM-assisted\ngeneration of data entries, and manual scenario generation and verification for the user simulator.\n--\nOur experiments reveal that agents built with simple LM constructs (like function calling or ReAct)\nperform poorly, highlighting the need for more sophisticated agent architectures. For instance, even\nstate-of-the-art LMs like gpt-4o achieve low task success rates (pass^1) using function calling\n(∼61% on τ -retail and ∼35% on τ -airline). With increasing k, the chance of consistently solving a\ntask drops rapidly, to as low as ∼25% for pass^8 on τ -retail for the same model. This showcases\nthe fragile nature of such agents in handling stochasticity and partial information, which is common\nin human-agent interaction. Upon analyzing the failure cases, we find that current agents struggle\nwith complex reasoning over databases, understanding and following ad-hoc policies, and handling\ncompound (more than one) requests. We hope that τ -bench enables the evaluation and development\nof more consistent and capable agents for real-world digital tasks involving human interaction.\n\n2\n--\nRelated Work\n\nMost existing benchmarks for agents and task-oriented dialogue systems focus on evaluating either\nconversational or tool-use capabilities. τ -bench aims to unify both under realistic settings, while also\ntesting how well agents can follow domain-specific policies in a consistent manner.\nBenchmarks for language agents and tool use. Several benchmarks have been developed to\nevaluate agents powered by LMs [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] . Recent efforts have focused specifically on\n--\nwhere the agent has to interact with human users to gather information and authorization.\nTask-oriented dialogue. Task-oriented dialogue has been a long-standing challenge for NLP, with\nseveral efforts over the years to build domain-specific offline datasets or user simulators. The former\ntypes of benchmarks are static and only test the conversational agent on pre-collected conversation\ntrajectories[4, 3, 2]. The latter either rely on user simulators that are rule-based or rely on symbolic\n--\nUser simulation with LMs. Our work is also related to recent efforts on using LMs as simulators\nof human characters. This includes papers on simulating non-player characters (NPCs) in text\nadventure games [13] multiple agents in human-like societies [15] or specific collaborative tasks [21],\nand enabling human-in-the-loop interaction for tasks like online shopping [6] or web search [28].\nHowever, all these past works have not used such simulators to benchmark the reliability of agents,\ninstead focusing on showcasing the ability of LMs to enable realistic simulations. Our work uses\n--\nτ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction\n\nEach individual task in τ -bench can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process\n(POMDP) (S, A, O, T , R, U) with state space S, action space A, observation space O, transition\nfunction T : S × A → S × O, reward function R : S → [0, 1], and instruction space U. The agent\ninteracts with both (1) databases (db) via API tools, and (2) a (simulated) user (user) to complete a\ntask, i.e., S = Sdb ⊗ Suser , A = Adb ∪ Auser , O = Odb ∪ Ouser . In addition, the agent is provided a\ndomain-specific policy document containing rules it must adhere to – one can think of this as partially\ndescribing the world model of the domain. We describe each component in more detail below.\nDatabases and APIs. Each τ -bench domain has several databases and associated APIs. The\ncontents of the database form the state sdb (Figure 2a), which is hidden from the agent and the\nuser, and can only be read from or written to using API actions adb , which are usually in the\nform tool_name(**kwargs). When an action is executed on the database, the transition Tdb :\n(sdb , adb ) 7→ (s′db , odb ) is deterministic and implemented as a Python function (Figure 2b).\nDomain policy. Each domain has a policy (Figure 2c) that explains the domain databases, task procedures, and restrictions for the agent to follow in its interactions. Some restrictions are implemented\nas checks in the API, e.g., using a payment ID not in the user profile will lead to odb = \"Error:\npayment not found\", and others not, e.g., the airline policy states different baggage allowances for\n--\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Given the complexity of IT tasks,\na major hurdle for this research is establishing systematic\nmethods to assess the effectiveness of AI agents prior to\ntheir production deployment (Bogin et al., 2024; Kapoor\net al., 2024). Let p be a prime. We prove bounds on short Dirichlet character sums evaluated at\na class of homogeneous polynomials in arbitrary dimensions. The\nbound (1.1) remains essentially the best bound for short multiplicative character sums.\nWe are interested in multiplicative character sums with polynomial arguments. A central motivation here is quite different; these play a role, for example, in counting solutions to Diophantine\nequations, such as in the works of [Pie06], [HB08], [Mun09], [HBP12], [Bon21], and [BP24]. Francis1 , E. F. van Dishoeck1, 3 , A. Caratti o Garatti6 , M. L. van Gelder1 , C. Gieser3, 2 , H. Beuther2 , T.P. Ray10\nL. Tychoniec1 , P. Nazari4 S. Reyes2 P. J. Kavanagh5 , P. Klaassen7 , M. The total deuterium abundance [D/H] in the universe is set by just two processes: the creation of deuterium in Big Bang\n\nNucleosynthesis at an abundance of [D/H]= 2.58 ± 0.13 × 10−5 , and its destruction within stellar interiors (astration). Measurements\nof variations in the total [D/H] abundance can thus potentially provide a probe of Galactic chemical evolution. However, most observational measurements of [D/H] are only sensitive to the gas-phase deuterium, and the amount of deuterium sequestered in dust\ngrains is debated. With the launch of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), it is now possible to measure the gas-phase [D/H]\nat unprecedented sensitivity and distances through observation of mid-IR lines of H2 and HD. Comparisons of gas-phase [D/H] with\nthe constraints on the total [D/H] from the primordial abundance and Galactic chemical evolution models can provide insight into the\ndegree of Deuterium lock-up in grains and the star formation history of our Galaxy.\nAims. We use data from the JWST Observations of Young protoStars (JOYS) program of 5 nearby and resolved low-mass protostellar\noutflows and 5 distant high-mass protostellar outflows taken with the JWST Mid Infrafred Instrument (MIRI) Medium Resolution\nSpectrometer (MRS) to measure gas-phase [D/H] via H2 and HD lines, assuming the gas is fully molecular.\nMethods. We extract spectra from various locations in the outflows. Using a rotational diagram analysis covering lines of H2 and\nHD with similar excitation energies, we derive the column density of HD and H2 or their upper limits. We then calculate the gasphase [D/H] from the column density results, and additionally apply a correction factor for the effect of chemical conversion of HD\nto atomic D and non-LTE excitation on the HD abundance in the shocks. To investigate the spatial distribution of the bulk gas and\nspecies refractory species associated with the dust grains, we also construct integrated line intensity maps of H2 , HD, [Fe II], [Fe I],\nand [S I] lines.\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"AnyPlace: Learning Generalizable Object Placement\nfor Robot Manipulation\n\narXiv:2502.04531v2 [cs.RO] 24 Sep 2025\n\nYuchi Zhao1, 2 , Miroslav Bogdanovic1, 2 , Chengyuan Luo3 , Steven Tohme4 ,\nKourosh Darvish1, 5 , Alán Aspuru-Guzik1,2,5 , Florian Shkurti1,2 , Animesh Garg6\n1\nUniversity of Toronto, 2 Vector Institute, 3 Shanghai Jiao Tong University,\n4\nWilfrid Laurier University, 5 Acceleration Consortium, 6 Georgia Institute of Technology\n\nFigure 1: Execution of the AnyPlace approach by the robot. (1) Given a language description of a placement\ntask, the robot first captures an RGBD image of the scene using its eye-in-hand camera. (2) A segmentation\nmodel and a VLM are used to segment objects and suggest possible placement locations. (3) Multiple placement\nposes are predicted for objects around suggested locations. (4) The robot realizes placement into any of the\npredicted poses. (A-O) AnyPlace shows generalization and robustness in predicting placement poses across 16\ntasks in the real world, despite being trained purely on a small synthetic dataset.\n\nAbstract: Object placement in robotic tasks is inherently challenging due to the\ndiversity of object geometries and placement configurations. We address this with\nAnyPlace, a two-stage method trained entirely on synthetic data, capable of predicting a wide range of feasible placement poses for real-world tasks. Our key\ninsight is that by leveraging a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to identify approximate placement locations, we can focus only on the relevant regions for precise\nlocal placement, which enables us to train the low-level placement-pose-prediction\nmodel to capture multimodal placements efficiently. For training, we generate a\nfully synthetic dataset comprising 13 categories of randomly generated objects in\n5370 different placement poses across three configurations (insertion, stacking,\nhanging) and train local placement-prediction models. We extensively evaluate\nour method in high-fidelity simulation and show that it consistently outperforms\nbaseline approaches across all three tasks in terms of success rate, coverage of\n9th Conference on Robot Learning (CoRL 2025), Seoul, Korea.\n\n\fplacement modes, and precision. In real-world experiments, our method achieves\nan average success and coverage rate of 76% across three tasks, where most baseline methods fail completely. We further validate the generalization of our approach\non 16 real-world placement tasks, demonstrating that models trained purely on\nsynthetic data can be directly transferred to the real world in a zero-shot setting.\nMore at: any-place.github.io.\nKeywords: Pick and Place, Robot Manipulation, Synthetic Dataset\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nPlacing objects is a fundamental task that humans perform effortlessly in daily life, from setting\nitems on a table to inserting cables into sockets. On the other hand, enabling a robot to perform\nsuch tasks can often be highly challenging. The challenges arise from the various constraints of\ndifferent placement tasks and the difficulty of generalizing to unseen objects. Additionally, predicting\nmultimodal placement outputs, which encompass a range of valid locations and modes, remains\nchallenging, particularly when multiple feasible solutions exist. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"STREETS-TIAN CONJECTURE HOLDS FOR 2-STEP SOLVMANIFOLDS\n\narXiv:2410.10540v1 [math.DG] 14 Oct 2024\n\nSHUWEN CHEN AND FANGYANG ZHENG\nAbstract. A Hermitian-symplectic metric is a Hermitian metric whose Kähler form is given\nby the (1, 1)-part of a closed 2-form. Streets-Tian Conjecture states that a compact complex\nmanifold admitting a Hermitian-symplectic metric must be Kählerian (i.e., admitting a Kähler\nmetric). The conjecture is known to be true in dimension 2 but is still open in dimensions\n3 or higher. In this article, we confirm the conjecture for all 2-step solvmanifolds, namely,\ncompact quotients of 2-step solvable Lie groups by discrete subgroups. In the proofs, we\nadopted a method of using special non-unitary frames, which enabled us to squeeze out some\nhidden symmetries to make the proof go through. Hopefully the technique could be further\napplied.\n\nContents\n1. Introduction and statement of results\n2. Hermitian geometry on Lie-complex manifolds\n3. 2-step solvable Lie algebras\nReferences\n\n1\n3\n6\n13\n\n1. Introduction and statement of results\nStreets and Tian introduced in [46] the notion of Hermitian-symplectic metric, namely a\nHermitian metric g on a compact complex manifold M n whose Kähler form ω is the (1, 1)-part\nof a closed 2-form. That is, there exists a global (2, 0)-form α on M n so that Ω = α + ω + α\nis closed. It is equivalent to the existence of a symplectic form (i.e., non-degenerate closed 2form) Ω such that Ω(x, Jx) > 0 for any non-zero tangent vector x. So a Hermitian-symplectic\nstructure is also called a symplectic structure taming a complex structure J ([20]).\nHermitian-symplectic metrics are natural and interesting. It is an obvious way to mix a Hermitian structure with a symplectic one. The definition immediately implies that any Hermitiansymplectic metric would satisfy ∂∂ω = 0, namely, it is pluriclosed, a type of special Hermitian\nmetrics that are extensively studied (see for instance [28] and the references therein). Hermitiansymplectic metrics also play an important role in the Hermitian curvature flow theory developed\nby Streets and Tian [45, 46, 47, 48]. For instance, Ye [56] shows that the Hermitian-symplectic\nproperty is preserved under the pluriclosed flow of Streets and Tian. Hermitian-symplectic\nmetrics enjoy other nice properties, including that it is stable under small deformations ([42,\nCh.12]), and by [55, Lemma 1] its presence guarantees the existence of a strongly Gauduchon\nmetric, which means a Hermitian metric whose Kähler form ω satisfies ∂ω n−1 = ∂Φ where Φ\nis a global (n, n − 2)-form on the n-dimensional manifold. An interesting and important open\nquestion in non-Kähler geometry is the following:\nConjecture 1 (Streets-Tian [46]). If a compact complex manifold admits a Hermitiansymplectic metric, then it must admit a Kähler metric.\n2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 53C55 (primary).\nKey words and phrases. However,\nthe fixed nature of the pruned model poses challenges in realworld inference scenarios, where tasks can vary significantly,\nfor instance, coding, mathematics, or domain-specific requirements, each demanding distinct skills and knowledge\nfrom the original language model. A static pruned model\nmay struggle to balance inference efficiency with high performance across diverse tasks.\n\nWith the rapid scaling of large language models\n(LLMs), structured pruning has become a widely\nused technique to learn efficient, smaller models from larger ones, delivering superior performance compared to training similarly sized models from scratch. In this paper, we move beyond\nthe traditional static pruning approach of determining a fixed pruning mask for a model, and\npropose a dynamic approach to structured pruning. In our method, the pruning mask is inputdependent and adapts dynamically based on the\ninformation described in a user instruction. Our\napproach, termed “instruction-following pruning”,\nintroduces a sparse mask predictor that takes the\nuser instruction as input and dynamically selects\nthe most relevant model parameters for the given\ntask. To identify and activate effective parameters,\nwe jointly optimize the sparse mask predictor and\nthe LLM, leveraging both instruction-following\ndata and the pre-training corpus. Experimental\nresults demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a wide range of evaluation benchmarks.\nFor example, our 3B activated model improves\nover the 3B dense model by 5-8 points of absolute\nmargin on domains such as math and coding, and\nrivals the performance of a 9B model.\n\nGiven this, we explore a paradigm shift from static pruning\nmasks to dynamic ones, addressing the central question:\nCan LLMs learn to select the most suited parameters\nbased on the task description?\nWe aim to automatically generate input-specific pruning\nmasks tailored to the tasks described in user prompts. This\ndynamic, context-aware pruning mechanism enables the\nlanguage model to perform inference using only the parameters necessary for the task, offering a compelling balance\nbetween efficiency and expressivity compared to using a\nstatic dense model. Moreover, because the parameters are\nselected and fixed, our method avoids reloading new parameters during the decoding process. Recent works inspired by distributional\nreinforcement learning use discrete histograms to approximate\ninteger reward distribution, but they struggle with continuous\nreward space and present challenges in balancing accuracy and\nscalability.\nWe propose a novel method for handling both continuous and\ndiscrete reward distributions in Discrete Time Markov Chains\nusing moment matching with Erlang mixtures. By analytically\nderiving higher-order moments through Moment Generating\nFunctions, our method approximates the reward distribution\nwith theoretically bounded error while preserving the statistical\nproperties of the true distribution. This detailed distributional\ninsight enables the formulation and robust model checking\nof quality properties based on the entire reward distribution\nfunction, rather than restricting to its expected value. We\ninclude a theoretical foundation ensuring bounded approximation\nerrors, along with an experimental evaluation demonstrating our\nmethod’s accuracy and scalability in practical model-checking\nproblems.\n\nsetting of bins, with fewer bins leading to coarser representations. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"AIDE: AI-Driven Exploration in the Space of Code\n\nZhengyao Jiang∗‡\nWeco AI\n\nDominik Schmidt∗†\nRunway ML\n\nDhruv Srikanth\nWeco AI\n\nDixing Xu\nWeco AI\n\nIan Kaplan\nWeco AI\n\nYuxiang Wu∗‡\nWeco AI\n\nDeniss Jacenko\nWeco AI\n\narXiv:2502.13138v1 [cs.AI] 18 Feb 2025\n\nAbstract\nMachine learning, the foundation of modern artificial intelligence, has driven innovations that have fundamentally transformed the world. Yet, behind advancements lies a complex and often tedious process requiring labor and compute intensive iteration and experimentation. Engineers and scientists developing machine learning models spend much of their time on trial-and-error tasks instead\nof conceptualizing innovative solutions or research hypotheses. To address this\nchallenge, we introduce AI-Driven Exploration (AIDE), a machine learning engineering agent powered by large language models (LLMs). AIDE frames machine\nlearning engineering as a code optimization problem, and formulates trial-anderror as a tree search in the space of potential solutions. By strategically reusing\nand refining promising solutions, AIDE effectively trades computational resources\nfor enhanced performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple machine\nlearning engineering benchmarks, including our Kaggle evaluations, OpenAI’s\nMLE-Bench and METR’s RE-Bench. The implementation of AIDE is publicly\navailable at https://github.com/WecoAI/aideml.\n\n1\n\nIntroduction\n\nMachine learning engineering supports many modern AI achievements, from basic regression on\ntabular data to the recent surge in large generative models. However, building a high-performance\nmachine learning model is always time consuming. Due to the inherent stochasticity of both the data\nand the optimization process, engineers and scientists rely heavily on trial-and-error. Researchers\nhave long sought to automate these iterative processes, leading to advancements in fields like AutoML (Feurer et al., 2015, 2020; LeDell and Poirier, 2020a; Olson and Moore, 2016; Jin et al., 2023,\n2019; Thornton et al., 2013a,b; Mueller and et al., 2024), Neural Architecture Search (Zoph and Le,\n2017; Pham et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Real et al., 2019; Elsken et al., 2019), and hyperparameter\noptimization (Falkner et al., 2018; Yang and Shami, 2020). These methods typically require a predefined search space of configurations, such as hyperparameters and network architectures, within\nwhich the algorithm explores potential solutions (Elsken et al., 2019; Yang and Shami, 2020; White\net al., 2023). Defining this space often requires significant domain expertise. Furthermore, search\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Gassert, Daniela Pfeiffer, Daniel Rueckert, and Franz Pfeiffer\n\nAbstract— Dark-field radiography of the human chest\nhas been demonstrated to have promising potential for the\nanalysis of the lung microstructure and the diagnosis of\nrespiratory diseases. However, previous studies of darkfield chest radiographs evaluated the lung signal only in\nthe inspiratory breathing state. Our work aims to add a\nnew perspective to these previous assessments by locally\ncomparing dark-field lung information between different\nrespiratory states. To this end, we discuss suitable image\nregistration methods for dark-field chest radiographs to\nenable consistent spatial alignment of the lung in distinct\nbreathing states. Utilizing full inspiration and expiration\nscans from a clinical chronic obstructive pulmonary disease study, we assess the performance of the proposed\nregistration framework and outline applicable evaluation\nThis work was supported by the European Research Council (ERC\nSynergy Grant SmartX, SyG 101167328 and ERC AdG 884622), the\nCenter for Advanced Laser Applications (CALA), the Federal Ministry of\nEducation and Research (BMBF) and the Free State of Bavaria under\nthe Excellence Strategy of the Federal Government and the Länder, the\nTechnical University of Munich – Institute for Advanced Study.\nF. Multi-agent remarkably reduces the annotation cost by 98% compared to human annotation. To verify the effectiveness of our training set, subsequently, we fine-tune\na popular LLM Alpaca-7B (Taori et al., 2023), resulting in our own tool-augmented LLM Lynx.\nWe conduct extensive experiments on API-Bank\nand obtained insightful results regarding the API\ntool usage capabilities of LLMs. Our findings reveal that even smaller models like Alpaca-7B and\nChatGLM-6.2B possess a certain level of API call\naccuracy, about 20%. However, their proficiency in\nAPI retrieval and planning is negligible. In contrast,\nthe larger-scale GPT-3 Davinci exhibits almost no\ncapability in API usage, indicating that API usage\nmight not be an inherent feature of LLMs. Our\nLynx, demonstrates an average improvement of\n24% in the three API usage capabilities compared\nto Alpaca-7B. While it approaches the performance\nof GPT-3.5, there remains a 21% gap from GPT-4.\nAdditionally, we present a comprehensive experimental analysis that sheds light on the primary\nchallenges encountered by GPT-4 and Lynx in utilizing APIs, addressing the third research question.\n\n2\n\nDesign Principles of API-Bank\n\nDue to the absence of authoritative ability definitions and benchmarks about tool-augmented LLM,\nwe conducted an extensive questionnaire survey\nin the initial phase. Through interviews with over\n\n500 users who expressed interest in incorporating\nadditional tools into LLM, we collected their requirements. Based on this foundation, we provide,\nfor the first time, a definition to measure the abilities of tool-augmented LLM and the data standards\nfor training and evaluating its abilities. We believe\nthat these design principles, which reflect the actual needs of users, can assist researchers in the\nfield and contribute to the future development of\ntool-augmented LLM benchmarks.\n2.1\n\nAbility Grading\n\nAn ideal tool-augmented LLM should enable users\nto define the APIs they require in a private API\nPool and request the LLM to invoke these APIs at\nthe appropriate times to fulfill their needs. Based\non our interviews with users, we have identified\ntwo dimensions that encompass the requirements\nfor tool-augmented LLM:\n• Few vs. Many APIs in Pool: Different users\nmay define varying numbers of APIs. When\nusers specify a small number of APIs (e.g.,\n2-3), the relevant information of all the specified APIs (such as name, definition, input\nparameters, and output parameters) and the\nuser’s query can be provided as input to the\nLLM, allowing it to determine which API to\ncall to meet the user’s needs. However, when\nusers have defined a large number of APIs\n(e.g., hundreds), it is not feasible to input all\nthe APIs to the LLM due to input length restrictions. In such cases, the LLM needs to\nretrieve potentially suitable APIs to fulfill the\nuser’s needs before calling them.\n• Single vs. Several API calls per Turn: Many\nuser requirements are complex and necessitate the combination of multiple API calls to\nachieve. Some users are willing to patiently\ndecompose requirements into multiple queries\nand guide the LLM gradually to fulfill their\nneeds, with each query involving a single API\ncall. But some users prefer to directly present\ncomplex requirements, expecting the LLM to\nautonomously perform step-by-step API calls\nwithin a single turn to meet their needs.\nAs shown in Figure 2, these two dimensions give\nrise to four expected conditions: Few APIs, Single\nCall; Few APIs, Multiple Calls; Many APIs, Single\nCall; and Many APIs, Multiple Calls. During implementation, we discovered that the difficulty level\n\n\fAPI Pool\n\nLLM\n\nAPI Search\n\nAPI\n\nRetrieve\n\nCall\nQuery\n\nCall\n\nResponse\n\nCall\nCall\nPlan\n\nRetrieve\n\nPlan\n\nRetrieve\n\nFigure 1: The abilities to Call, Retrieve+Call, and Plan+Retrieve+Call APIs of tool-augmented LLMs.\nFew APIs in Pool\n\nMany APIs in Pool\n\nSingle\nAPI\nCall\n\nFew APIs, Single Call Many APIs, Single Call\nCall\nRetrieve+Call\n\nMulti\nAPI\nCalls\n\nFew APIs, Multi Calls Many APIs, Multi Calls\nCall\nPlan+Retrieve+Call\n\nany errors during the call (such as incorrect API\nnames, input parameter errors, or incorrect API call\nsequencing) can result in unfulfilled user requirements. Therefore, for the benchmark construction,\nwe need to consider the following criteria:\n1. Domain diversity: The training and testing\ndata should cover a wide range of domains as\ncomprehensively as possible;\n\nFigure 2: Requirements of tool-augmented LLMs.\n\n2. API authenticity: The name, definition, input and output parameters of the APIs should\nclosely resemble those in the real world;\n\nof the first two conditions is similar. Since planning\nthe call of multiple APIs is straightforward when\nall the APIs are input, thus we merged the first two\nconditions. Evaluation authenticity: The evaluation\nshould incorporate a functional system that\nenables real-time interaction with the LLM.\nThe LLM offers an API call, which the system\nexecutes and subsequently returns the results\nto the LLM. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:99","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"one. Do you want me to do it?\nOh… In that case just cancel it\ncancel_reservation[JK9O19]\n{…, ’status’: ‘cancelled’}\n……\n\n(Write database)\n\nFigure 1: (a) In τ -bench, an agent interacts with database API tools and an LM-simulated user to\ncomplete tasks. The benchmark tests an agent’s ability to collate and convey all required information\nfrom/to users through multiple interactions, and solve complex issues on the fly while ensuring it\nfollows guidelines laid out in a domain-specific policy document. (b) An example trajectory in\nτ -airline, where an agent needs to reject the user request (change a basic economy flight) following\ndomain policies and propose a new solution (cancel and rebook). This challenges the agent in\nlong-context zero-shot reasoning over complex databases, rules, and user intents.\ndiverse user scenarios and corresponding ground truth annotations. As a first demonstration, we\nfocus on the realm of customer service and create two different domains where agents need to assist\nsimulated users with diverse requests (τ -retail and τ -airline). We leverage the generative capabilities\nof language models (LMs) for data creation and realistic human user simulation [15] in conjunction\nwith manual annotation and verification.\nWe constructed τ -bench in three stages, including manual schema and API design, LM-assisted\ngeneration of data entries, and manual scenario generation and verification for the user simulator.\nOur evaluation scheme compares the database state at the end of each episode with the ground truth\nexpected state. This allows for objective measurement of the agent’s decision making, while also\nproviding room for stochastic variation in the conversation itself, since the user may pose the same\nrequest in different ways that result in the same end state of the database. We also introduce the\nmetric of pass^k, which measures the consistency and robustness of the agent across k i.i.d. trials.\nOur experiments reveal that agents built with simple LM constructs (like function calling or ReAct)\nperform poorly, highlighting the need for more sophisticated agent architectures. For instance, even\nstate-of-the-art LMs like gpt-4o achieve low task success rates (pass^1) using function calling\n(∼61% on τ -retail and ∼35% on τ -airline). With increasing k, the chance of consistently solving a\ntask drops rapidly, to as low as ∼25% for pass^8 on τ -retail for the same model. This showcases\nthe fragile nature of such agents in handling stochasticity and partial information, which is common\nin human-agent interaction. Upon analyzing the failure cases, we find that current agents struggle\nwith complex reasoning over databases, understanding and following ad-hoc policies, and handling\ncompound (more than one) requests. We hope that τ -bench enables the evaluation and development\nof more consistent and capable agents for real-world digital tasks involving human interaction.\n\n2\n\nRelated Work\n\nMost existing benchmarks for agents and task-oriented dialogue systems focus on evaluating either\nconversational or tool-use capabilities. τ -bench aims to unify both under realistic settings, while also\ntesting how well agents can follow domain-specific policies in a consistent manner.\nBenchmarks for language agents and tool use. Several benchmarks have been developed to\nevaluate agents powered by LMs [27, 29, 12, 14, 16] . Recent efforts have focused specifically on\nevaluating tool use capabilities of LMs, i.e., their ability to generate the right function calls from a\nset of functions in an API. Projects like the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) [23],\n2\n\n\fToolBench [22] and MetaTool [11] test tool use/function calling in multiple programming languages\nand propose various methods for evaluating the accuracy of function calls. ToolEmu [16] uses\nlanguage models themselves to emulate the execution of tools, with a focus on exposing potential\nsafety risks when LM agents fail to use tools correctly. However, all these works only contain a\nsingle-step user interaction, where the human interacting with the agent provides an initial instruction\ncontaining all the required information. In contrast, our benchmark focuses on a more realistic setting\nwhere the agent has to interact with human users to gather information and authorization.\nTask-oriented dialogue. Task-oriented dialogue has been a long-standing challenge for NLP, with\nseveral efforts over the years to build domain-specific offline datasets or user simulators. The former\ntypes of benchmarks are static and only test the conversational agent on pre-collected conversation\ntrajectories[4, 3, 2]. The latter either rely on user simulators that are rule-based or rely on symbolic\nspecifications [17, 8] or perform tests with real humans through crowdsourcing platforms [9]. Some\nvery recent work explores the use of LMs as response raters to train dialogue systems [10] or evaluates\ntheir capability for simulating users [7]. τ -bench leverages the powerful text generation capabilities\nof state-of-the-art LMs to simulate realistic user utterances and long-context conversations using\ntextual scenario descriptions, with the goal of evaluating agents. The stochastic sampling from LMs\nallows for diverse yet faithful variations in the dialogue when re-run with the exact scenario – this is\nextremely useful for testing agent consistency, as we show in § 5.1.\nUser simulation with LMs. Our work is also related to recent efforts on using LMs as simulators\nof human characters. This includes papers on simulating non-player characters (NPCs) in text\nadventure games [13] multiple agents in human-like societies [15] or specific collaborative tasks [21],\nand enabling human-in-the-loop interaction for tasks like online shopping [6] or web search [28].\nHowever, all these past works have not used such simulators to benchmark the reliability of agents,\ninstead focusing on showcasing the ability of LMs to enable realistic simulations. Our work uses\nsuch realistic user simulation to provide an accurate assessment of the reliability and robustness of\nAI agents for deployment in systems that undertake millions of real-world interactions with humans.\n\n3\n\nτ -bench: A benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction\n\nEach individual task in τ -bench can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process\n(POMDP) (S, A, O, T , R, U) with state space S, action space A, observation space O, transition\nfunction T : S × A → S × O, reward function R : S → [0, 1], and instruction space U. The agent\ninteracts with both (1) databases (db) via API tools, and (2) a (simulated) user (user) to complete a\ntask, i.e., S = Sdb ⊗ Suser , A = Adb ∪ Auser , O = Odb ∪ Ouser . In addition, the agent is provided a\ndomain-specific policy document containing rules it must adhere to – one can think of this as partially\ndescribing the world model of the domain. We describe each component in more detail below.\nDatabases and APIs. Each τ -bench domain has several databases and associated APIs. The\ncontents of the database form the state sdb (Figure 2a), which is hidden from the agent and the\nuser, and can only be read from or written to using API actions adb , which are usually in the\nform tool_name(**kwargs). When an action is executed on the database, the transition Tdb :\n(sdb , adb ) 7→ (s′db , odb ) is deterministic and implemented as a Python function (Figure 2b).\nDomain policy. Each domain has a policy (Figure 2c) that explains the domain databases, task procedures, and restrictions for the agent to follow in its interactions. Some restrictions are implemented\nas checks in the API, e.g., using a payment ID not in the user profile will lead to odb = \"Error:\npayment not found\", and others not, e.g., the airline policy states different baggage allowances for\ndifferent membership statuses and cabin classes, but the agent needs to fill in the number of baggage\nitems to be paid for in the book_reservation API, similar to the freedom given real-world agents.\nUser simulation. We use a language model (gpt-4-0613) to simulate a human user interacting with\nthe agent. The user state suser consists of an initial system prompt with the task instruction (Figure 2d)\nalong with the entire conversation history between the user and the agent so far. The user cannot see the\ninteraction history between the agent and API tools. The agent can interact with the user using any natural language message, e.g., auser can be \"Your reservation has been updated, is there\nanything else I can help with?\". The transition Tuser : (suser , auser ) 7→ (s′user , ouser ) is\nstochastic and attaches the agent’s message to the chat history followed by sampling a new user message from the LM, e.g., ouser can then be \"Yes, I also want to cancel another flight.\"\nWhen the user issues ouser =\"###STOP###\", the episode finishes and the agent is evaluated.\n3\n\n\f{\"order_id\": \"#W2890441\",\n\"user_id\": \"mei_davis_8935\",\n\"items\": [{\n\"name\": \"Water Bottle\",\n\"product_id\": \"8310926033\",\n\"item_id\": \"2366567022\",\n\"price\": 54.04,\n\"options\": {\n\"capacity\": \"1000ml\",\n\"material\": \"stainless\nsteel\",\n\"color\": \"blue\"\n}}, ...], ...}\n\n## Return delivered order\n- After user confirmation, the order status\nwill be changed to 'return requested'...\n## Exchange delivered order\n- An order can only be exchanged if its\nstatus is 'delivered'...\n(c) Domain policy excerpts in τ -retail.\n\ndef exchange_delivered_order_items(\norder_id: str,\nitem_ids: List[str],\nnew_item_ids: List[str],\npayment_method_id: str,\n) -> str: ...\n\n{\"instruction\": \"You are Mei Davis in 80217.\nYou want to return the water bottle, and\nexchange the pet bed and office chair to the\ncheapest version. Mention the two things\ntogether. If you can only do one of the two\nthings, you prefer to do whatever saves you\nmost money, but you want to know the money\nyou can save in both ways. ServiceNow’s extensive\nreach makes it an ideal real-world environment for evaluating\nthe potential impact of UI assistants in the workplace.\n\ncollection of 125 toy web tasks ranging from clicking a specific button to using a basic text editor, and WebShop (Yao\net al., 2022), a simulated e-commerce website with shopping\ntasks that require searching and browsing a catalog of items.\nMore recently, Zhou et al. (2023) introduced WebArena, a collection of 190 tasks based on realistic websites that emulate\nreal-world domains such as e-commerce, social forums, collaborative software development, and content management.\nWebArena is a notoriously challenging benchmark, with a\nsuccess rate of 14% for a state-of-the-art web agent based\non GPT-4, and 78% for human agents. Deng et al. (2023)\nproposed Min2Web, a large-scale dataset of 2,000 web interactions from 137 websites curated by human annotators. Similarly, Lù et al. (2024) propose WebLINX, a curated dataset\nof web interactions composed of 2337 expert demonstrations\nfrom 155 different real-world websites. In WebLINX, each\ntask is composed of a turn-based chat dialogue averaging 43\ninteractions per task. Recently, He et al. (2024) propose 300\ninformation-retrieval tasks, from 15 real-world consumer\nwebsites (e.g., Amazon, Coursera, Booking), which are used\nto evaluate WebVoyager, a vision-based web agent.\n\nOur contributions are as follows:\n• WorkArena: A realistic benchmark of enterprise-related\ntasks for web agents comprising 19,912 unique task\ninstances (§3, Figure 1a);\n• BrowserGym: A new framework for the development and\nevaluation of web agents, compatible with previous benchmarks like WebArena (Zhou et al., 2023), MiniWoB (Liu\net al., 2018; Shi et al., 2017a) and WebShop (Yao et al.,\n2022), that offers a richer set of multimodal observations\n(e.g., screenshot, accessibility tree, screen coordinates),\na broader set of actions (e.g., Python code and high-level\nprimitives), and supports chat-based interactions (§ 4,\nFigure 1b); Surprisingly, these features contribute to\nbringing our GPT-4 agent at the top of the leaderboard\non WebArena, with a score of 25.4%, contrasting with the\nscore of the original paper, 14.4%.\n• Empirical study: We report a collection of experiments\nto assess the ability of state-of-the-art large language\nmodel (LLM)-based agents to solve WorkArena, as well\nas an analysis of the impact of the different BrowserGym\nfeatures on WorkArena and MiniWoB (§5).\n\nWorth mentioning is the related body of work on mobile\nUI agent benchmarks, which includes the early PixelHelp\ndataset (Li et al., 2020) and more recent Android in the Wild\n(Rawles et al., 2023) and Macro Mining (Huang et al., 2024).\nOur proposed benchmark, WorkArena, is designed to\ncomplement existing work by specifically focusing on\nreal-world enterprise software applications. It includes a\nwide range of tasks that collectively encompass several\nend-to-end workflows typically performed by knowledge\nworkers. Additionally, it poses a series of technical\nchallenges, such as pages with very large document object\nmodels (DOMs), non-standard HTML, and complex UI\nelements, which we outline in § 3.2. This benchmark\nintegrates into BrowserGym, a new environment that we\n\n2. Related Works\nBenchmarks for web agents: Early benchmarks for web\nagents were based on synthetic web environments where\nagents were tasked with performing low-level keyboard and\nmouse actions (Shi et al., 2017b). Notable examples are\nMiniWoB (Shi et al., 2017a; Liu et al., 2018), which offer a\n2\n\n\fWorkArena: Web Agents for Common Knowledge Work Tasks\n\npropose for the evaluation of web agents, which aggregates\nall features proposed in previous work, such as multimodal\nobservations and code-based actions while being the first\nto support chat-based agent-user interactions (§4.1).\n\nLists\nCreate a filter for the list to extract all entries\nwhere priority is \"high\"\nForms\n\nLLM-based Agents: The scope of our experimental\ncontributions is limited to web agents that rely on language\nmodels for reasoning. Recent studies include the seminal\nwork of Nakano et al. (2021) that introduces WebGPT, an\nagent capable of browsing the web and answering questions\nvia information retrieval. Other works have also explored\nweb agents that receive HTML as input and produce a series\nof high-level actions such as click, type, select (Deng et al.,\n2023; Liu et al., 2023a;b; Yao et al., 2023).\n\nCreate a new user with first name \"John\", last name\n\"Smith\", and email \"john.smith@workarena.com\"\nKnowledge Bases\nSearch the knowledge base to answer \"What is the\npassword for conference room A-651?\"\nService Catalogs\nOrder a new iPad pro with specifications {\"colour\":\n\"space grey\", \"storage\": \"128 Gb\" }\n\nOther works have shown that using only textual information as input is sometimes limiting and have considered\nmultimodal observations that combine visual information\n(screenshots of a page) with text (Humphreys et al., 2022;\nHe et al., 2024). Instead of directly interacting with a\nwebsite, recent works have proposed methods that can act\non websites using Python-generated code from task-specific\ninstructions (Gur et al., 2023a;b). Our proposed environment,\nBrowserGym is flexible in that it supports all observations\nand actions spaces utilized in prior research.\n\nDashboards\nWhat is the value of \"Hewlett-Packard\" in the\n\"Manufacturer\" chart (in percent)?\nMenus\nNavigate to the \"SLA > My Work\" module of the\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}

Consists of fixed question-answer pairs\nand encompasses a variety of scenarios, including\nsingle-turn dialogues, multi-turn dialogues, and\npersonalized scenario data. It also includes evaluations of atomic-level capabilities.\nSpecial. Includes imperfect instructions, such as\ninstructions containing incomplete parameters, incorrectly formatted parameters, or questions irrelevant to the capabilities of the candidate functions.\nAgent. Encompasses real-world scenarios, abstracted to construct multi-turn, multi-step tool\ninvocation scenarios, divided into multi-turn and\nmulti-step cases depending on whether the user\nparticipates in the dialogue process.\nThe three categories above cover most of the tool\nusage scenarios for LLMs, and detailed explanations of each category can be found in Appendix A.\nOur main contributions are as follows:\n\nevaluation of LLMs’ tool usage.\n\n2\n\nRelated Works\n\nThe emerging trend of leveraging LLMs’ tool-use\ncapabilities in real-world applications underscores\nthe need for comprehensive evaluations of their\nperformance and effectiveness. Despite recent advancements, existing benchmarks for evaluating\nthe tool-use capabilities of LLMs still have significant limitations\nStable ToolBench (Guo et al., 2024) addresses\nthe issue of unstable external APIs by employing\na virtual API server, but its dependence on large\nmodels for evaluation results in high costs and\nscalability challenges. BFCL (Yan et al., 2024) introduces a benchmark for tool use in multi-turn dialogue scenarios. Yet, it assembles dialogues from\nfixed content, failing to capture the dynamic and\nadaptive nature of real-world interactions. Similarly, τ -Bench (Yao et al., 2024) evaluates language\nagents’ ability to engage with human users while\nadhering to domain-specific rules. Still, its narrow\nfocus on just two scenarios limits its generalizability across diverse tasks. HammerBench (Wang\net al., 2024a) improves upon this by incorporating datasets derived from popular mobile applications and merging dialogues to simulate typical\nquestion-answer trajectories. However, like BFCL,\nits multi-turn dialogues are simplistic concatenations of pre-defined content, which do not reflect\nthe complexities of real-world conversational dynamics. In addition, some benchmarks (Qin et al.,\n2023; Guo et al., 2024) rely on large language models (LLMs) for result evaluation, leading to high\ncosts and unstable operations.\nIn contrast, our work addresses these limitations\nby expanding the scope of evaluation to encompass a broader range of tool usage scenarios. We\n\n• Comprehensive Benchmark Evaluation. We\npropose a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs’ tool usage, covering various scenarios, including more fine-grained evaluation\nperspectives and assessments under imperfect instructions and providing more stable evaluation\nmetrics.\n• Sandbox Environment and Automated Evaluation System. We build an end-to-end automated evaluation system and develop a sandbox\nenvironment construction scheme for multi-turn,\nmulti-step tool invocation based on real-world\nscenario abstraction.\n• Extensive Experimental Validation.
